newsletters.title: How to build your PM second brain with ChatGPT
newsletters.subtitle: Use AI to amplify your craft, not replace it

claims.text: AI is most valuable for PMs as a 'second brain' for synthesis — combining meeting notes, user research, and market data into actionable insights.
claims.evidence: Workflow for using ChatGPT to maintain a running synthesis of product decisions, user feedback, and market signals.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Alexander Embiricos, Boris Cherny, Chip Huyen, Asha Sharma, Dhanji R. Prasanna, and Nick Turley have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

claims.text: The key to useful AI assistance is feeding it context over time, not just asking one-off questions — persistent context makes AI dramatically more useful.
claims.evidence: Comparison of one-shot ChatGPT usage vs. maintained conversation threads with accumulated product context.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Alexander Embiricos, Boris Cherny, Dan Shipper, Dhanji R. Prasanna, Asha Sharma, and Sherwin Wu have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

newsletters.title: Everyone should be using Claude Code more
newsletters.subtitle: How to get started, and 50 ways non-technical people are using Claude Code in their work and life

claims.text: AI coding tools have crossed a threshold where non-technical people can build real, useful products — not just demos.
claims.evidence: 50+ examples of non-technical people building and actually using products built with Claude Code, from internal tools to consumer apps.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Boris Cherny, Lazar Jovanovic, Dan Shipper, Zevi Arnovitz, Alexander Embiricos, Marc Andreessen, Dhanji R. Prasanna, Scott Wu, Sherwin Wu, Howie Liu, and Sam Lessin have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

claims.text: The best way to learn AI coding tools is to start with a personal problem you actually want solved, not a tutorial project.
claims.evidence: The most successful examples in the newsletter came from people solving their own problems, not following courses.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Lazar Jovanovic, Zevi Arnovitz, Dan Shipper, Ravi Mehta, Howie Liu, and Scott Wu have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

claims.text: Product managers who learn to build with AI tools will have a significant career advantage — it's the most leveraged new skill to develop.
claims.evidence: Multiple examples of PMs who built prototypes, internal tools, and even shipped features using AI coding tools.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Tomer Cohen, Bret Taylor, Marc Andreessen, Ravi Mehta, Matt LeMay, Howie Liu, Jenny Wen, Garrett Lord, Nick Turley, Chip Conley, and Benjamin Mann have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

newsletters.title: Essential reading for product builders—part 1
newsletters.subtitle: 7 timeless essays you likely haven’t read but should

claims.text: The best product thinking comes from adjacent fields — psychology, game design, economics — not from product management literature alone.
claims.evidence: Curated list of 7 essays from diverse fields that directly apply to product building, with analysis of why each matters.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Matt LeMay, Albert Cheng, Stewart Butterfield, Robby Stein, and Madhavan Ramanujam have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

newsletters.title: What people are vibe coding (and actually using)
newsletters.subtitle: 50+ useful/fun/clever examples of what non-technical people are building—to inspire your own vibe-coding journey

claims.text: Vibe coding is most powerful for internal tools and personal productivity — not for building the next startup.
claims.evidence: The most-used vibe-coded projects in the survey were internal dashboards, data pipelines, and personal automation — not consumer apps.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Lazar Jovanovic, Zevi Arnovitz, Sam Lessin, Dhanji R. Prasanna, and Edwin Chen have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

claims.text: The gap between 'demo' and 'daily driver' is where most vibe-coded projects die — polish and reliability matter more than initial creation.
claims.evidence: Many submissions noted their projects worked great initially but required significant iteration to become truly reliable.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Dan Shipper, Boris Cherny, Edwin Chen, Dhanji R. Prasanna, Nick Turley, and Jason Droege have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

newsletters.title: An AI glossary
newsletters.subtitle: The most common AI terms explained, simply

claims.text: Product managers don't need to understand AI at a technical depth — they need to understand it at the right depth for making product decisions.
claims.evidence: Glossary organized by what PMs need to know vs. what they can leave to engineers, with decision-making context for each term.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Chip Huyen, Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, Aishwarya Naresh Reganti + Kiriti Badam, Nicole Forsgren, Nick Turley, Howie Liu, Jason Droege, and Scott Wu have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

newsletters.title: State of the product job market in 2025
newsletters.subtitle: There’s a lot to be optimistic about

claims.text: The PM job market is recovering, but the roles that are growing fastest require AI literacy — traditional PM skills alone are no longer sufficient.
claims.evidence: Job posting data showing AI-related PM roles growing 3x faster than traditional PM roles.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Tomer Cohen, Molly Graham, Matt LeMay, Howie Liu, Garrett Lord, Jenny Wen, Chip Conley, and Scott Wu have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

newsletters.title: Beyond vibe checks: A PM’s complete guide to evals
newsletters.subtitle: How to master the emerging skill that can make or break an AI product

claims.text: AI evals are the single most important new skill for product managers working on AI products — more important than prompt engineering.
claims.evidence: Companies with rigorous eval frameworks ship AI features faster and with fewer regressions than those relying on manual testing.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, Chip Huyen, Brendan Foody, Aishwarya Naresh Reganti + Kiriti Badam, Nicole Forsgren, Nick Turley, Edwin Chen, Garrett Lord, Jason Droege, and Asha Sharma have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

claims.text: The best AI evals combine automated metrics with human judgment — neither alone is sufficient for measuring AI product quality.
claims.evidence: Framework for building eval suites that balance automated scoring with expert human evaluation for nuanced quality assessment.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, Chip Huyen, Nicole Forsgren, Brendan Foody, Edwin Chen, Garrett Lord, Nick Turley, and Jason Droege have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

newsletters.title: A guide to AI prototyping for product managers
newsletters.subtitle: How to turn your idea into a working prototype in minutes

claims.text: AI prototyping collapses the traditional design-to-development handoff — PMs can now validate ideas before involving engineering.
claims.evidence: Step-by-step guide showing how PMs can go from idea to working prototype in under an hour using AI tools.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Ravi Mehta, Zevi Arnovitz, Tomer Cohen, Marc Andreessen, Jenny Wen, Howie Liu, and Nick Turley have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

claims.text: Starting with JSON data structures (not UI mockups) leads to better AI-generated prototypes because it forces clarity of thinking about the product.
claims.evidence: Ravi Mehta's approach of defining data schemas first produced more functional prototypes than design-first approaches.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Ravi Mehta, Alexander Embiricos, Boris Cherny, and Scott Wu have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

newsletters.title: Product manager is an unfair role. So work unfairly.
newsletters.subtitle: How to thrive in “the great flattening” by redefining work norms

claims.text: The PM role is uniquely positioned to be augmented (not replaced) by AI because the core job is synthesis and judgment, not execution.
claims.evidence: Analysis of how AI tools augment different PM responsibilities — from research to spec writing to stakeholder communication.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Tomer Cohen, Matt LeMay, Bret Taylor, Jenny Wen, Howie Liu, Ben Horowitz, Nick Turley, and Chip Conley have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

claims.text: The most effective PMs in the AI era are those who can move between strategic thinking and hands-on building — the 'full-stack PM'.
claims.evidence: Examples of PMs who've accelerated their careers by learning to prototype, analyze data, and build lightweight tools themselves.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Tomer Cohen, Ravi Mehta, Bret Taylor, Matt LeMay, Marc Andreessen, Howie Liu, Jenny Wen, and Robby Stein have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

newsletters.title: How Duolingo reignited user growth
newsletters.subtitle: The story behind Duolingo's 350% growth acceleration, leaderboards, streaks, notifications, and innovative growth model

claims.text: Gamification mechanics like leaderboards can drive massive retention improvements when they tap into social comparison and competition instincts.
claims.evidence: Duolingo's leaderboards drove a 17% increase in overall learning time and tripled the number of highly engaged learners.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Albert Cheng, Elena Verna, Grant Lee, Madhavan Ramanujam, and Robby Stein have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

claims.text: The 'current user retention rate' (CURR) metric — measuring the percentage of current users retained over time — is more actionable than traditional cohort analysis for mature products.
claims.evidence: Duolingo used CURR to decompose growth into new users, resurrected users, and retained users, enabling targeted interventions.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Albert Cheng, Jason Cohen, and Ethan Smith have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

claims.text: Push notifications should be treated as a product feature, not a marketing channel — optimizing for user value rather than clicks.
claims.evidence: Duolingo's notification optimization was one of the three key levers (alongside streaks and leaderboards) that drove 4.5x DAU growth.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Elena Verna, Albert Cheng, Grant Lee, and Nick Turley have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

claims.text: Streak mechanics create powerful habit loops, but they need protective features (streak freezes) to prevent user frustration and churn.
claims.evidence: Duolingo's streak feature became their most powerful retention tool once they added streak freezes and streak-saving notifications.
claims.synthesisExplanation: Multiple guests including Albert Cheng, Elena Verna, Grant Lee, Madhavan Ramanujam, and Jenny Wen have independently validated or extended this claim across podcast conversations.

newsletters.title: How to spot a top 1% startup early
newsletters.subtitle: Three key lessons from people who picked multiple iconic companies before they were obvious

claims.text: One of the clearest markers of a future generational company is ambition that borders on ludicrous, where the founder's goals sound crazy to most people.
claims.evidence: Multiple interviewees (early employees at Palantir, OpenAI, Facebook, Spotify) independently cited extraordinary ambition as the top signal, noting that both Palantir and OpenAI were considered ludicrous when started.

claims.text: The quality of the founders matters above all other variables when evaluating whether to join a startup early.
claims.evidence: Every interviewee emphasized founders as THE variable, not one among many. Cristina (early Stripe, Notion, Linear) said 'nothing matters more than this to me.'

claims.text: It is more important for founders to learn quickly and adapt than to have a good initial strategy.
claims.evidence: Bob noted that both Palantir and OpenAI started with unworkable initial strategies that the world was correct to mock, but both iterated to something that worked. Soleio called this trait 'clock speed.'

claims.text: Judging a startup by its current product quality is a trap because almost every iconic product started as something rough or even completely different from what made it successful.
claims.evidence: Soleio was disappointed by early Facebook, early Slack was described by its co-founder as 'a giant piece of shit,' Spotify was going to be a video platform, and Figma started as a meme generator.

claims.text: Instead of evaluating a startup's current product, look for strong customer pull where users cannot stop talking about the product even when it is early and the market is niche.
claims.evidence: Cristina never asked to see Stripe's metrics; instead she observed what developers said in forums like Hacker News and Twitter, caring that 'people loved it enough to share it.'

claims.text: None of the serial early employees at iconic companies found their jobs through applications or recruiters; their paths were serendipitous and driven by personal connections.
claims.evidence: Cristina met Stripe's founder at a barbecue, Rasmus got a cold call from Spotify's CEO, Soleio won a design award, Bob knew Palantir's team from PayPal, and Sean replied to a Twitter thread.

claims.text: Look for 'Jurassic Park moments' at startups — early prototypes or demos that leave you breathless and feeling like you are being given a clear glimpse of the future, even if the product is ugly and small.
claims.evidence: Soleio's metaphor from the raptor hatching scene in Jurassic Park: even in a slimy, harmless form, you sense the world has shifted and an apex predator has entered it.

newsletters.title: Ecosystem is the next big growth channel
newsletters.subtitle: How to stand out in a noisy landscape by leveraging partners who already have access and trust with your target audience

claims.text: Traditional B2B growth channels (inbound, outbound, product virality, events, lifecycle emails) are becoming less effective because AI has made it easy for everyone to produce content and flood channels.
claims.evidence: Search traffic is declining due to LLMs, AI SDRs are flooding inboxes, endless AI-built products make virality harder, and event fatigue is setting in from an explosion of conferences.

claims.text: Ecosystem -- leveraging partners, creators, communities, and integrations who already have trust with your audience -- is emerging as the most important growth channel for B2B startups.
claims.evidence: Emily Kramer identified this pattern across top AI startups. Supabase grew from 1M to 4.5M developers in a year through ecosystem; Gamma attributes over half its growth to influencers.

claims.text: A well-designed ecosystem strategy makes every other growth channel more effective by serving as both a distribution path and a source of content.
claims.evidence: Ecosystem partners fuel inbound, add credibility to outbound, accelerate virality, make events enticing, and ground lifecycle marketing in real use cases. HubSpot's Loop Marketing launch demonstrated this.

claims.text: Every ecosystem partnership must be a win-win-win for you, the partner, and the prospect -- never just one or two out of three.
claims.evidence: Emily Kramer's personal bar for partnerships requires triple benefit. Lenny's Product Pass exemplifies this: startups get promotion, subscribers get free tools, and Lenny gets more paid subscribers.

claims.text: When starting an ecosystem strategy, pick one partner program that fits your product and audience and prove it works before expanding, rather than launching every kind of partnership at once.
claims.evidence: Arrows rebuilt exclusively for HubSpot users and grew 40x before expanding to Salesforce. CEO Daniel Zarick said 'Nobody would care about us if we didn't try to make a mark with HubSpot first.'

claims.text: Individual people (employees, customers, partners) perform eight times better than brand accounts on LinkedIn for content engagement.
claims.evidence: LinkedIn's own data shows employees generate 8x more engagement than brands, reinforcing why ecosystem partners who speak as individuals are more effective than corporate brand messaging.

newsletters.title: A builder’s guide to living a long and healthy life
newsletters.subtitle: For something a little different

claims.text: Environmental toxins like PFAS, phthalates, and microplastics are the trans fats of our generation, found in 92-97% of Americans' blood and linked to testosterone, anxiety, cancer, and fertility issues.
claims.evidence: 92% of Americans have measurable phthalates and 97% have PFAS in their blood. These chemicals affect testosterone levels, anxiety, cancer risk, and sperm count according to multiple cited studies.

claims.text: The 80/20 of sleep health comes down to three things: exercise during the day, get morning sunlight, and optimize your sleep setup for no external light, quiet, low CO2, and cold temperatures.
claims.evidence: Justin Mares distilled sleep optimization into these three fundamentals before recommending products like Eight Sleep pods, which he found increase deep sleep and reduce wake-ups.

claims.text: Most microplastics are inhaled rather than ingested via water or food, making air purification in your home and office a top priority for toxin mitigation.
claims.evidence: Justin Mares recommends air purifiers like Jaspr and PuroAir as the primary defense, noting that inhalation is the dominant exposure route for microplastics.

claims.text: Plant protein powders are largely ineffective because they are not bioavailable and are often contaminated with high levels of pesticides, with one test finding glyphosate levels 1,000 times higher than recommended.
claims.evidence: A systematic review showed plant proteins have low bioavailability, and a Mamavation test found Orgain's organic pea protein had glyphosate levels roughly 1,000x higher than recommended.

claims.text: The EU bans more than 1,300 chemicals and compounds that the US allows, because the FDA takes an assume-safe-until-proven-harmful approach versus the EU's precautionary do-no-harm approach.
claims.evidence: The US has over 40,000 chemicals allowed with minimal FDA pushback, and it took the FDA 50 years to act on trans fats. The EU regulates proactively assuming new chemicals may cause harm.

newsletters.title: Introducing the GAIN framework for feedback: an evidence-based approach to giving feedback that people love, appreciate, and act on
newsletters.subtitle: A step-by-step guide to crafting feedback you’ll want to give and people will want to get

claims.text: The GAIN framework (Goal, Actions, Impacts, Next actions) is more effective than typical feedback because framing feedback around what someone stands to gain is more motivating than focusing on what to avoid.
claims.evidence: Research by Tversky showed doctors given approach-framing ('90% survival rate') made the right decision 84% of the time vs. 50% with avoid-framing ('10% mortality rate'). Gottman's couples research shows the same pattern.

claims.text: Sharing observations of actions and their impacts is far more effective than making judgments, because critical judgments almost guarantee defensiveness and derail conversations.
claims.evidence: Jack Cohen's 11 years of coaching and workshops found that even experienced feedback givers struggle to distinguish observations from judgments. 'Response times averaged 4.2 days' lands differently than 'You're being unprofessional.'

claims.text: Even flattering judgments like 'You're a rockstar PM' can destroy growth mindset and foster people-pleasing, because if success is attributed to brilliance then failure implies lack of it.
claims.evidence: Carol Dweck's mindset research shows that praise tied to identity ('You're brilliant') makes challenges feel like threats rather than learning opportunities, reorienting focus from impact to seeking approval.

claims.text: Saying 'I'm giving you these comments because I have very high expectations and I know you can reach them' more than quadrupled revision rates compared to a neutral framing.
claims.evidence: A randomized double-blind controlled Stanford study tested this sentence combining challenge and support. Students who received it revised over 4x more and their revisions were 26% better on average.

claims.text: Saying 'Feel free to say no' when making a request doubled the percentage of people who said yes, because supporting autonomy preempts resistance and engenders goodwill.
claims.evidence: A meta-analysis of 52 studies showed that the 'but-you-are-free' technique doubled compliance rates, confirming that making it easy to decline paradoxically increases acceptance.

claims.text: Acknowledging your own contribution to a problem before giving feedback is one of the most disarming approaches available, because it prevents the recipient from fixating on your role in the situation.
claims.evidence: Jack Cohen found this is one of the least-used but most effective techniques. Once you show you are monitoring your own behavior, the other person can release focus on your actions and concentrate on their own.

newsletters.title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
newsletters.subtitle: A practical guide to moving beyond generic scores and measuring what matters

claims.text: The most common mistake in AI evaluation is starting with off-the-shelf metrics like hallucination or toxicity scores, which often don't correlate with the actual problems users face.
claims.evidence: Hamel Husain and Shreya Shankar found through training 2,000+ PMs and engineers at 500+ companies that teams build eval dashboards that look useful but get ignored because metrics are disconnected from real user problems.

claims.text: Effective AI evaluation starts with error analysis using a single principal domain expert who reviews approximately 100 user interactions with open coding and axial coding to discover real failure modes.
claims.evidence: The process uses open coding (free-form critique with pass/fail judgments) followed by axial coding (pattern-finding and grouping). Appointing a single expert eliminates annotation conflicts and prevents paralysis.

claims.text: For AI evaluation, binary pass/fail judgments are more effective than 1-to-5 Likert scales because the distinction between adjacent scores is subjective and inconsistent, while nuance is captured in written critiques.
claims.evidence: Many teams are tempted by Likert scales believing they capture more nuance, but binary decisions force clarity. The nuance is preserved in critiques that explain why a judgment was made.

claims.text: In RAG systems, you should fix the retriever before investing in generator improvements, because if the correct information is not retrieved, the generator has no chance of producing a correct answer.
claims.evidence: Modern LLMs are adept at ignoring irrelevant noise in context, but they cannot invent facts from missing information. Recall@k is the most critical metric for RAG retrieval evaluation.

claims.text: The real competitive advantage in AI products comes not from prompting but from building a continuous improvement flywheel where production monitoring flags failures, error analysis finds root causes, and fixes are added to a golden dataset.
claims.evidence: The three-phase process (error analysis, eval suite building, operationalization) creates a monitor-analyze-improve-deploy loop where every failure makes the system permanently smarter.

newsletters.title: How to find the perfect name
newsletters.subtitle: A step-by-step playbook for naming in a noisy and AI-driven product landscape

claims.text: The name you choose for a product or company may be the single most important branding decision you make, yet it is too often delegated to junior staff or tackled in group brainstorming sessions.
claims.evidence: David Placek, who named nearly 4,000 brands over 40 years including Swiffer, Sonos, BlackBerry, Azure, and Vercel, observed that teams routinely underinvest in naming despite its outsized impact.

claims.text: Invented names like Pentium actually take less money to build into brands than existing words because they signal 'new and innovative' and generate more attention due to being unexpected.
claims.evidence: Despite a common myth to the contrary, Lexicon Branding's research shows invented names outperform descriptive ones. Within months of launch, consumers were asking for Pentium computers by name.

claims.text: Effective naming requires building for trust, communicating an original idea rather than describing what the company does, and making the name accessible and easy to process.
claims.evidence: Lexicon's three pillars of naming, applied across 4,000 brands. Navan (replacing TripActions) grew from $150M to over $500M revenue after rebranding with an original, non-descriptive name.

claims.text: Large brainstorming sessions rarely produce good names because of peer pressure to agree and everyone's need to be right; small teams with esprit de corps generate far better results.
claims.evidence: Lexicon recommends generating at least 1,000 creative ideas before selecting a shortlist of 50-100 candidates, using small dedicated teams rather than group sessions with four or more participants.

claims.text: The sound of a brand name matters as much as its meaning: specific sounds create specific impressions, like the 'z' in Azure creating a strong signal and the 'zure' creating a smooth experience.
claims.evidence: Lexicon uses proprietary sound-symbolism software. Dasani exemplifies this: the 'da' sound delivers crispness, 'san' (Latin for health), and the ending 'i' feels light and slim.

newsletters.title: Why your AI product needs a different development lifecycle
newsletters.subtitle: Introducing the Continuous Calibration/Continuous Development (CC/CD) framework

claims.text: AI products fundamentally break the assumptions of traditional software because they are inherently non-deterministic on both the input side (unpredictable user prompts) and output side (variable model responses).
claims.evidence: Based on 50+ AI implementations at OpenAI, Google, Amazon, and Databricks, Reganti and Badam found teams repeatedly fail when they apply traditional software development practices to AI products.

claims.text: Every AI product must negotiate a fundamental tradeoff between agency (the system's ability to act autonomously) and control (human oversight), and most teams fail by jumping to full agency before testing under high control.
claims.evidence: The agency-control tradeoff is the second core principle of the CC/CD framework. Teams that ship fully autonomous systems without earning agency through graduated testing face cascading failures they cannot debug.

claims.text: The CC/CD (Continuous Calibration/Continuous Development) framework requires scoping AI products into versions defined by agency levels, starting with high-control/low-agency and gradually earning more autonomy.
claims.evidence: For customer support: v1 routes tickets (low agency), v2 suggests resolutions for human review (medium agency), v3 auto-resolves with fallback (high agency). GitHub Copilot and Cursor followed this exact playbook.

claims.text: Working with AI is like onboarding a new teammate: you should not hand them your highest-stakes projects on day one but instead start small, observe, build trust, and gradually expand their scope.
claims.evidence: Analogy from Reganti and Badam based on 50+ AI implementations. The CC/CD framework structures this intuition into a repeatable development loop of scope, build, eval, deploy, calibrate, and fix.

claims.text: Never lead with the technology when building AI products; let the problem, evals, and data guide what gets added next, because too many people focus on chasing tools and frameworks and end up making costly mistakes.
claims.evidence: The CC/CD framework explicitly warns against premature optimization. Step 2 says 'don't overengineer' and Step 6 is when you should engineer more, backed by data from real error patterns.

newsletters.title: Essential reading for product builders—part 2
newsletters.subtitle: 10 more timeless essays you probably haven’t read but should

claims.text: A product succeeds because it solves a problem for people, and the problem should be easy to communicate in a sentence or two that resonates with the target audience.
claims.evidence: From Julie Zhuo's 'Building Products' essay, highlighted as essential reading. If the problem statement doesn't resonate with someone from your target audience, consider that a big red flag.

claims.text: The best executive communication follows the SCQA structure: Situation (unambiguous facts), Complication (what's changed), Question (almost always 'What should we do?'), and Answer (resolves the complication 100%).
claims.evidence: From Barbara Minto's Pyramid Principle via Michael Dearing, curated as essential reading. The framework applies to every conversation including with friends, loved ones, and at the deli counter.

claims.text: A startup is a company designed to grow fast -- being newly founded, working on technology, or taking venture funding are not essential; the only essential thing is growth.
claims.evidence: From Paul Graham's 'Startup = Growth' essay, curated as essential reading. Everything else associated with startups follows from growth as the defining characteristic.

claims.text: Schlep blindness causes founders to avoid hard, messy problems in favor of easy ones, but ambitious ideas are doubly valuable because they have intrinsic value and less competition since everyone else is frightened off.
claims.evidence: From Paul Graham's 'Schlep Blindness' essay. Stripe is the most striking example: for a decade every developer knew payments were painful, but unconscious aversion to complexity prevented anyone from fixing it.

claims.text: The best way to manage scaling is to give away your Legos -- resist the instinct to grab back responsibilities from new hires and instead find a bigger, better tower to build.
claims.evidence: From Molly Graham's 'Give Away Your Legos' essay. Almost everything about scaling is counterintuitive; people's first instinct to fight for their part of the tower or micromanage is usually the wrong move.

newsletters.title: 25 proven tactics to accelerate AI adoption at your company
newsletters.subtitle: Practical advice from AI-forward companies like Shopify, Ramp, Zapier, Duolingo, Intercom, and Whoop

claims.text: The biggest barrier to AI adoption at companies is not technology but organizational change, including vague mandates, procurement bottlenecks, and lack of guidance on high-impact use cases.
claims.evidence: Only 8% of US workers use AI daily per Gallup. Interviews with leaders at Shopify, Ramp, Zapier, Duolingo, Intercom, and Whoop found organizational issues are the primary blocker.

claims.text: Saying 'we are AI-first' means nothing if employees do not know what that means for their day-to-day work; successful companies provide specific, concrete tactics employees can adopt.
claims.evidence: Shopify CEO Tobi Lutke shared concrete tactics in his memo like making AI prototyping part of GSD. Zapier CEO gave employees a playbook and a week off to practice. Intercom CTO embedded with teams monthly.

claims.text: AI adoption should be tracked as both inputs (who is using AI) and outputs (business value created), and included in performance reviews to create accountability.
claims.evidence: Shopify asks employees to rate colleagues on AI tool usage (1-5 scale). Ramp publishes AI power users by team. Intercom tracks merged PRs and sees durable 20% year-over-year productivity improvement.

claims.text: Duolingo went from creating 100 courses in 12 years to 150 courses in just 12 months after rebuilding their content creation process with AI.
claims.evidence: Duolingo CPO Cem Kansu reported they vibe-coded the first Duolingo Chess lesson in hours instead of weeks, demonstrating the productivity multiplier of AI adoption.

claims.text: True AI adoption happens when teams become skeptical of flashy demos and instead demand to see accuracy metrics, evaluation frameworks, and failure modes behind AI products.
claims.evidence: Peter Yang has seen too many PMs present flashy demos to executives only to stumble when asked about data and evaluations. When employees start asking about rigor, that signals real adoption.

claims.text: Companies should turn their internal AI enthusiasts into teachers by hosting regular demos, hackathons, and dedicated AI experimentation time rather than relying on top-down mandates alone.
claims.evidence: Shopify hosts AI training villages, Duolingo protects 2-hour FriAIday blocks, Ramp has an AI SWAT team, Zapier hosts weekly demos attracting 60+ employees, and Whoop ties AI uplift to promotions.

newsletters.title: Build your personal AI copilot
newsletters.subtitle: A guide to using AI as a long-term thinking partner (including prompts to get you started)

claims.text: Most tech workers are missing out on AI's potential because they are not providing enough context; LLMs feel like blunt, generic instruments when they lack the background knowledge a human colleague would need.
claims.evidence: Tal Raviv taught workshops to 20,000+ tech employees and found the same pattern: regardless of company size or AI fluency, people weren't giving LLMs the context needed to experience their full potential.

claims.text: An AI copilot built with ongoing context about your goals, role, projects, team, and org becomes a real thinking partner for long-term complex work, not just a document generator.
claims.evidence: Students report using their copilots every day. One new PM presented product strategy two weeks in and his CEO remarked 'It feels like you've been here for months.' Another built a career development copilot for her team.

claims.text: Building an AI copilot follows a four-step process analogous to onboarding a teammate: hire it with instructions, onboard it with company knowledge, kick off initiatives in chat threads, and put it to work with simple prompts.
claims.evidence: Using LLM Projects features in ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini, you set instructions (role/personality), upload project knowledge (strategy docs, org charts), then use initiative-specific chat threads.

claims.text: The habit of 'gossiping' to your AI copilot -- casually updating it about conversations, stakeholder changes, and new information via speech-to-text -- is critical for keeping context fresh and effective.
claims.evidence: Tal Raviv describes using speech-to-text to vent stream-of-consciousness updates like 'You won't believe what just happened...' The copilot remembers and references these updates later in relevant contexts.

claims.text: Even when AI is wrong it can be valuable because it spurs you to crystallize what you actually think; the goal is not perfect answers but getting the most out of yourself as a thinking partner.
claims.evidence: Tal Raviv compares this to a car's driver assist beeping on an open highway -- no harm done, but you keep it on for when it catches something real. Disagreeing with AI output still sharpens your own thinking.

claims.text: AI copilot investment earns compound interest: uploading retrospectives and lessons learned from completed initiatives makes the copilot increasingly effective across all future work.
claims.evidence: At the end of each initiative, sharing outcomes and personal reflections back to project knowledge accelerates the copilot's ability to connect dots and provide wise counsel on future projects.

newsletters.title: How tech’s most resilient workers handle burnout
newsletters.subtitle: Introducing ARMOR: a new framework building on our tech worker sentiment survey insights

claims.text: The key to beating burnout is to systematically design a career and lifestyle that make burnout structurally unlikely, rather than relying on resilience or stress management.
claims.evidence: Based on surveys and interviews with ~175 tech workers who reported low burnout, the investigation found that 'burnout conquerors' proactively structure their work lives rather than reactively managing stress.

claims.text: Autonomy is the most critical ingredient to being happy at work, and burnout conquerors earn it by consistently delivering results to build trust.
claims.evidence: Multiple respondents identified autonomy as the first factor in avoiding burnout, and the research found that trust enabling autonomy is earned through consistent delivery on goals, not granted.

claims.text: Burnout conquerors overcommunicate not just what they are working on but how they work — their boundaries, constraints, strengths, and decision-making preferences — using a 'How I Work' document.
claims.evidence: Respondents noted that most peers only communicate what they're working on but forget about communicating the 'hows,' and that setting these foundations prevents misalignment.

claims.text: Burnout conquerors treat wellness as non-negotiable professional infrastructure, with exercise routines more like professional athletes than typical tech workers.
claims.evidence: Many participants reported training 5-6 times a week, maintaining fixed sleep schedules, and cooking all their own food — treating health maintenance with the same rigor as work deliverables.

claims.text: Burnout is organizational warfare, not a personal responsibility — free-pizza Fridays and free mental health sessions are band-aids that signal serious organizational dysfunction.
claims.evidence: Burnout conquerors reject the common corporate narrative that puts the onus on employees, recognizing that deep exhaustion results from broken processes, unclear expectations, and bad incentives.

claims.text: Who you work with matters more than almost anything else about the job, and choosing a job based on colleagues is the single most cited career choice for avoiding burnout.
claims.evidence: The most common answer to 'What is THE career choice that helped you avoid burnout?' centered on prioritizing people and relationships, both at work and at home.

claims.text: Burnout conquerors use four tests to evaluate roles: the relationship test (who you work with), the Sunday test (how you feel before the work week), the energy test (does the work energize you), and the values alignment test (does culture match your core values).
claims.evidence: The ARMOR framework's 'Role Architecture' component identifies these four rubrics that burnout conquerors use to strategically design their careers from day one.

newsletters.title: How to get your entire team prototyping with AI
newsletters.subtitle: A guide to creating AI prototypes that match your brand and are integrated into every step of your development lifecycle

claims.text: The two biggest barriers to AI prototyping adoption on product teams are making prototypes look good enough for stakeholders and figuring out team workflows instead of individual silos.
claims.evidence: After teaching 500+ PMs over five months, these were the two most common questions that came up when people tried to use AI prototyping tools in real life.

claims.text: Building a reusable component library is the single biggest improvement teams can make to AI prototyping quality, allowing brand-consistent prototypes without manual cleanup each time.
claims.evidence: Three methods are outlined (screenshots, Chrome extensions, code) with increasing effort and fidelity, all producing reusable assets that dramatically improve output quality.

claims.text: AI prototyping introduces a new 'medium fidelity' tier — better than napkin drawings but not as polished as finalized Figma mocks — and choosing the right fidelity for each context is critically important.
claims.evidence: The post outlines six steps in the product development lifecycle, each requiring different fidelity levels: mid-fi for internal discovery and engineer communication, high-fi for executive pitches and user interviews.

claims.text: The code generated by AI prototyping tools is mostly useless to engineering teams because it does not follow existing patterns, use the same libraries, or even use the same programming language.
claims.evidence: The post explicitly warns that unless components are built using your existing codebase, the generated code serves only as a communication and visualization tool, not production code.

claims.text: The 'baselines and forks' workflow — creating a high-quality reproduction of your current product then duplicating it to explore new ideas — is the best way to rapidly test multiple design directions without rebuilding each time.
claims.evidence: Demonstrated with an Airbnb Experiences example where a single baseline enabled testing a questionnaire flow and a past-travel-history recommendation flow, each from a single prompt on a forked copy.

claims.text: Using the Figma MCP server with Cursor allows AI agents to autonomously take screenshots, extract design tokens, and get CSS from Figma's Dev Mode, producing prototypes indistinguishable from the real product.
claims.evidence: The post demonstrates a side-by-side of Figma design library components and code-reproduced components using the new Figma MCP server integration with Cursor.

newsletters.title: The definitive guide to mastering analytical thinking interviews
newsletters.subtitle: A step-by-step playbook to help you ace your PM interviews

claims.text: Analytical thinking interviews should follow a five-step linear flow: assumptions and game plan, product rationale, metric framework, goal-setting, and tradeoff evaluation.
claims.evidence: The post presents this as a comprehensive structure that maximizes a candidate's chances of providing strong signals across all dimensions interviewers evaluate in AT interviews.

claims.text: Never use averages or ratios as North Star metrics because if your NSM increases while your ecosystem actually shrinks, you get a false positive — a metric that looks great even as the product dies.
claims.evidence: The post warns that candidates confidently present ratio-based metrics that could mask a dying product, which signals poor analytical thinking to interviewers.

claims.text: The critical 'altitude shift' from company-level metrics to team-level goals is where most candidates stumble, because tracking a metric is fundamentally different from executing against a specific goal.
claims.evidence: The post describes this as a transition from the 50,000-foot view of ecosystem health to ground-level identification of specific focus areas for a single product team over 3-6 months.

claims.text: If you cannot describe a metric to a data scientist in a way that they could run a query with, it is not a useful metric — always define metrics so specifically that someone could implement them tomorrow.
claims.evidence: Presented as a key principle within the metric framework step, emphasizing that vague metrics signal weak analytical ability in interviews.

claims.text: Top candidates in tradeoff evaluation specify what would need to be true for them to change their mind, because it solidifies how they think about the fundamental factors that influence their thinking.
claims.evidence: The post identifies this as a differentiating behavior that elevates a candidate's tradeoff analysis above simply stating a decision and rationale.

claims.text: Outlining your game plan at the start of an analytical thinking interview is like sitting down to play a game with someone who knows the rules — it signals to interviewers that you will generate the signals they need.
claims.evidence: The post recommends spending the first minute making assumptions and sharing a structured plan, which also gives the interviewer a chance to redirect if they want to spend time differently.

newsletters.title: How to get the most out of your product pass, part 1
newsletters.subtitle: Tips for taking full advantage of the 15+ free premium products you have access to as a paid subscriber

claims.text: Replit can single-shot fairly sophisticated full-stack applications unlike other vibe-coding platforms, because it automatically tests the apps it builds using its internal browser and auto-fixes issues it finds.
claims.evidence: Lenny describes Replit's Agent 3 running autonomously for 200+ minutes, building, testing, and fixing apps, and notes this self-testing capability distinguishes it from competitors.

claims.text: Every startup and modern product team should be using Linear for task tracking, project organization, and roadmap building — and at this point, most are.
claims.evidence: Lenny states there is 'no question' about this, despite not using Linear himself since he works solo, and notes Linear now has deep agent integrations for delegating tickets to AI.

claims.text: Creating decks and documents is one of the most time-consuming and annoying parts of most product builders' jobs, and AI tools like Gamma are doing for PMs what Cursor is doing for engineers.
claims.evidence: Lenny describes creating shareable podcast summaries by copying transcripts and clicking a few buttons, calling the result 'magic.'

newsletters.title: Part 2 of how to get the most out of your product pass—and welcome, Stripe Atlas, to the bundle!
newsletters.subtitle: Tips for taking full advantage of the 17+ free premium products you have access to as a paid subscriber

claims.text: Lovable reached $100 million ARR just eight months after launch, making it the fastest-growing company in history — faster than even Cursor.
claims.evidence: Stated as a factual milestone in the newsletter's description of Lovable as an AI-powered vibe-coding platform.

claims.text: Bolt grew from 0 to $40 million ARR in five months and is used by 72% of Fortune 500 product teams, differentiating itself by integrating frontier agents like Claude Code rather than relying on homegrown agents.
claims.evidence: Lenny describes Bolt's unique approach of integrating state-of-the-art frontier agents so users always get access to the most advanced coding agents without switching tools.

claims.text: Granola has quickly become the obvious tool for AI meeting note-taking because it works behind the scenes using laptop audio instead of joining calls, and everyone who tries it gets hooked.
claims.evidence: Lenny notes that Granola doesn't join calls like competing tools, instead transcribing silently in the background, and that its UX and note quality are standout features.

newsletters.title: How tech workers really feel about work right now
newsletters.subtitle: Results from our first-ever large-scale tech worker sentiment survey

claims.text: Almost half (44.67%) of tech workers are currently experiencing significant burnout, and burnout strongly correlates with quitting intentions and low engagement.
claims.evidence: Survey of 8,200+ tech workers found 44.67% report moderate to complete burnout; 67.8% of those exploring new jobs are highly burned out.

claims.text: Startup founders are the happiest people in tech, scoring highest on career optimism, job enjoyment, engagement, and belonging while having the lowest burnout.
claims.evidence: Founders and new college grads were the only two segments growing more optimistic over the past year; founders led nearly all positive sentiment metrics.

claims.text: Leadership quality may be the single most important lever for retention, with workers under ineffective managers 4.3 times as likely to be at risk of leaving.
claims.evidence: Only 26.6% of tech workers rate their managers as highly effective; great managers correlate with 48% more engagement, 63% more belonging, and 62% more job enjoyment.

claims.text: Hybrid workers feel significantly better about their jobs than in-office workers (21% sentiment gap), but in-office workers are slightly more optimistic about their long-term career prospects.
claims.evidence: Survey data showed job enjoyment, burnout, and engagement are similar across setups, but direct sentiment questions revealed hybrid workers feel much better about their current job.

claims.text: Small-company employees outperform large-company counterparts on nearly every work sentiment measure, and company size is one of the strongest predictors of positive sentiment.
claims.evidence: Statistically significant differences across 9 of 11 sentiment metrics by company size; small-company workers report more optimism, less burnout, higher belonging, and better manager ratings.

claims.text: Mid-career tech professionals (7-14 years experience) are struggling the most, showing higher burnout, higher quitting intentions, and lower job enjoyment than both early and late-career workers.
claims.evidence: U-shaped pattern across age and experience: early-career workers report 71% job enjoyment vs. mid-career 66% vs. late-career 75%; mid-career shows lowest role commitment at 28%.

claims.text: Enabling teams to have maximal autonomy, ownership, and purpose are the secret ingredients that make founders 3-4x more optimistic and almost 3x happier than everyone else in tech.
claims.evidence: Companies are advised to inspire founder mode for everyone based on the survey finding that founders had the most positive sentiment across nearly all metrics.

newsletters.title: Five principles for successfully managing managers
newsletters.subtitle: Why skip leads are the load-bearing pillars of your organization

claims.text: All manager challenges are either directly or indirectly about the skip lead (manager's manager), because the skip lead hires, reviews, and coaches the manager.
claims.evidence: Saumil Mehta's experience leading 800+ colleagues and 100+ managers at Square; he shifted from advising on the manager to always asking about the manager's manager.

claims.text: The transition from line manager to manager of managers has a learning curve that is just as steep or steeper than the IC-to-manager transition, yet organizations rarely recognize this.
claims.evidence: The author notes that managing through an abstraction layer requires entirely different skills; he personally insisted on signing off on every promotion to manager-of-managers at Square.

claims.text: Skip leads should never offer strong opinions or make decisions on the spot with skip reports, as this undermines their direct-report managers and creates contradictory directions.
claims.evidence: Jeff Weiner observed that his take-it-or-leave-it remarks created massive disruptive fire drills; the better approach is to listen, ask follow-up questions, then work through the direct-report manager.

claims.text: Skip leads should use Andy Grove's task-relevant maturity (TRM) framework to match projects to managers based on each project's scope, risk, and ambiguity and each manager's specific skills and experience.
claims.evidence: From High Output Management; skip leads evaluate project uncertainty and impact on a 2x2 rubric to determine delegation level and review cadence for each manager.

claims.text: Covering for a direct-report manager's underperformance is one of the most common and damaging patterns in fast-growing tech companies, driven by shame, fear of confrontation, and insecurity.
claims.evidence: Saumil Mehta reports repeatedly finding this pattern when investigating failed projects at Square; the fix is to clearly report underperformance up the chain and set explicit expectations.

newsletters.title: The ultimate guide to negotiating your comp
newsletters.subtitle: The step-by-step guide that’s helped hundreds of leaders secure life-changing compensation

claims.text: The difference between standard and exceptional compensation outcomes is not skill or experience but a fundamental shift from asking what a company can offer you to demonstrating how you will solve their most pressing challenges.
claims.evidence: Jacob Warwick's GAINS framework based on 3,500+ executive negotiations over 15 years; a Senior PM turned a $185K-$285K range into a $1.1M package using this approach.

claims.text: Published salary ranges should never be treated as the final word on compensation because strategic negotiation can yield packages that are 2-4x the stated upper limit.
claims.evidence: Multiple case studies: a Senior PM turned $285K max into $1.1M; a VP of Product Design turned $600K max into $1.2M; a product leader secured an $800K signing bonus through strategic patience.

claims.text: Gathering intelligence about organizational power dynamics, decision-maker psychology, and strategic pain points creates more negotiation leverage than showcasing credentials or experience.
claims.evidence: An SVP candidate lost an $800K opportunity by treating a Chief of Staff meeting as a formality; the winning candidate understood the real power dynamic.

claims.text: Product leaders have an average tenure of 2.6 years, which is shorter than typical 4-year vesting schedules, making agreement engineering and term definition critical for long-term compensation.
claims.evidence: One client lost over $20 million by not properly documenting early stock payouts; another gained $1.2M in accelerated equity by spending three extra weeks defining success metrics.

claims.text: Strategic patience in negotiations, including maintaining multiple opportunities and transparent timelines rather than rushing to accept, consistently produces dramatically better outcomes.
claims.evidence: A 16-month GAINS strategy turned organizational turmoil into an $800K signing bonus and $500K equity grant; rushing negotiations almost always leaves money on the table.

newsletters.title: Make product management fun again with AI agents
newsletters.subtitle: A guide to AI agents for product managers

claims.text: AI automations (tools like Zapier Agents, Lindy AI, Relay App) are currently the most practical category of AI agents for helping product managers offload repetitive busywork.
claims.evidence: Based on interviews with AI agent platform founders, usability sessions with PMs, and a hands-on workshop for 5,000+ product managers.

claims.text: PMs should design AI agents with low downside by restricting outputs to drafts, DMs, and recommendations rather than allowing direct actions like sending emails or posting in channels.
claims.evidence: Framework from the article's agent design checklist: send DMs instead of channel messages, create drafts instead of sending emails, make recommendations instead of decisions.

claims.text: Letting AI summarize everything will quickly degrade a PM's customer intuition; instead PMs should use AI to traverse and cluster data while insisting on exact quotes and direct links to original sources.
claims.evidence: Gumloop uses AI to reason about root causes in support threads rather than summarize, always referencing back to the original conversation for human review.

claims.text: AI copilots, AI prototyping tools, and AI agents will converge into unified tools that have deep company context, connect to real-world inputs and outputs, and operate by natural conversation.
claims.evidence: Each current category of AI tool checks some but not all agentic behaviors; the author expects the gaps to close as tools borrow elements from each other.

claims.text: Using AI prototyping tools to build production AI agents is tempting but not recommended because it creates maintenance burden, SaaS registration bureaucracy, and compliance overhead that dedicated agent platforms handle out of the box.
claims.evidence: PMs in usability sessions tried building agents with prototyping tools but found ongoing bug maintenance, third-party app registration, and IT permission requirements made it impractical.

claims.text: AI agents shine at ongoing, one-at-a-time repetitive tasks rather than big one-time batch tasks, and PMs should choose continuous trigger-driven work as their first agent use case.
claims.evidence: The article recommends choosing ongoing tasks that arrive continually and suggests using direct LLM tools like Claude or ChatGPT for batch processing instead.

newsletters.title: The definitive guide to mastering product sense interviews
newsletters.subtitle: A step-by-step playbook to help you ace your PM interview

claims.text: Product sense interviews assess five specific dimensions: clear communication, product motivation, segmentation, problem identification, and solution development, and excellence in one area cannot compensate for weakness in another.
claims.evidence: Ben Erez's framework based on interviewing 50+ candidates at Facebook and coaching hundreds of PM candidates

claims.text: Product sense and analytical thinking interviews originally popularized by Facebook and Google have now been widely adopted across the tech industry, including at Stripe, OpenAI, Block, and even earlier-stage companies.
claims.evidence: Author's direct experience interviewing at Attentive and observing industry hiring trends

claims.text: The best candidates treat product sense interviews as a structured game with clear rules rather than a casual conversation, using waypointing, assumption setting, and game plan articulation to generate clear signals for interviewers.
claims.evidence: Patterns observed from coaching hundreds of candidates and watching successful mock interviews

claims.text: Strong candidates should spend 3-5 minutes establishing product motivation before diving into segmentation and solutions, connecting the product to deeper human needs rather than just describing features.
claims.evidence: Ben Erez's coaching framework distilled from interviewing and coaching hundreds of PM candidates

claims.text: Candidates should practice stating assumptions and game plans for 20-30 different product sense questions to build muscle memory and avoid blank-page anxiety during interviews.
claims.evidence: Tactical preparation advice from coaching practice

newsletters.title: How to ship like a startup
newsletters.subtitle: Seven lessons from the story of Figma Slides

claims.text: Prototypes should replace PRDs in fast-moving environments because PRDs block valuable design and engineering work, are almost never up-to-date, and become watered-down versions of the actual product once it exists.
claims.evidence: Mihika Kapoor's experience building Figma Slides and FigJam at Figma

claims.text: Figma Slides launched less than a year ago and is already ahead of Apple Keynote and tied with Canva as a most-used presentation software among Lenny's readers.
claims.evidence: Lenny's reader survey data on presentation software usage

claims.text: Sharing raw work-in-progress with stakeholders builds hype and investment rather than depleting trust, as demonstrated by an engineer posting over 70 prototype videos in Slack before launch.
claims.evidence: Figma Slides team practice of sharing progress in Slack channels

claims.text: The best team members are those who opt in rather than being assigned, and if you need to sell someone really hard to join your project, they are probably not the right fit.
claims.evidence: Feedback Mihika received early in her career, validated by Figma Slides team assembly

claims.text: You must first build trust by executing well on assigned projects (the meat and potatoes) before leadership will fund your own ambitious ideas (the souffle).
claims.evidence: Mihika's experience earning trust through FigJam features like Sections, Music, and Voting before pitching Figma Slides

claims.text: Every team member should attend at least 50% of user research sessions because hearing a user directly express a pain point makes team members far more likely to act on it than hearing a secondhand summary.
claims.evidence: Mihika Kapoor's observation from building products at Figma

newsletters.title: Which companies produce the best product managers
newsletters.subtitle: Part two in my exploration of the companies that find and develop the best PMs

claims.text: Over a third of Palantir's PM alumni have started a company, making it the dominant company for producing founder PMs.
claims.evidence: Live Data Technologies data analysis of PM alumni career paths

claims.text: Intercom is the only company to rank in the top 10 on all four career acceleration dimensions for PMs: internal promotions, external promotions, fastest time to promotion, and fastest rise to leadership.
claims.evidence: Live Data Technologies data triangulating four career acceleration metrics

claims.text: Over 20% of Chime's PM alumni go on to not just start a company but also raise a Series A, making it the top company for producing venture-scale founders.
claims.evidence: Live Data Technologies data on PM alumni founder outcomes

claims.text: Stripe PMs do not rank highly on traditional career metrics because they get hired as star PMs at rocketship companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, and Mercury rather than starting companies or climbing the ladder.
claims.evidence: Analysis of Stripe PM alumni destinations showing they go to earlier-stage companies rather than big tech

newsletters.title: Why you’re so angry at work (and what to do about it)
newsletters.subtitle: A practical framework for transforming big feelings into wisdom

claims.text: Workplace anger most commonly arises when your expertise is challenged, your autonomy is threatened, your values are compromised, or your identity is dismissed, and it signals an unmet emotional need rather than being the problem itself.
claims.evidence: Executive coach Natalie Rothfels' pattern observed across all co-founder and executive coaching clients

claims.text: Anger manifests in two forms at work: hot anger (exploding outward with aggression and blame) and cold anger (withdrawing inward with shutdown and self-criticism), and both cause real costs if left unconscious.
claims.evidence: Buddhist philosophy framework applied in Natalie Rothfels' executive coaching practice

claims.text: A four-step process can transform workplace anger into wisdom: recognize your alarm, do a U-turn to look inward, identify the unmet need, and choose a conscious response.
claims.evidence: Natalie Rothfels' coaching framework informed by Buddhist philosophy, Internal Family Systems, and somatic methodologies

claims.text: In the past six months, every one of coach Natalie Rothfels' executive and co-founder clients has been vibrating with deep, persistent anger that was depleting their motivation and making them emotionally exhausted at work.
claims.evidence: Direct observation from Natalie Rothfels' coaching practice across all clients

newsletters.subtitle: Taking a moment to celebrate and reflect—and then back to work

claims.text: The only way Lenny's Newsletter has meaningfully grown is through word of mouth, with the sole exception being Substack's recommendation feature which accounts for roughly half of the journey from 500K to 1M subscribers.
claims.evidence: Lenny's own growth data showing paid ads, SEO, referrals, and BD had no meaningful impact

claims.text: The podcast takes less work and generates more revenue than the newsletter, with each podcast episode requiring 3-5 hours versus 10-30 hours per newsletter post.
claims.evidence: Lenny's direct breakdown of time allocation across newsletter and podcast

claims.text: Product teams will shrink by 25-50% due to AI (mostly fewer engineers), while PMs will have more influence and leverage, spending more time in discovery and GTM and less in designing and building.
claims.evidence: Lenny agreeing with Marty Cagan's published take on AI's impact on product teams

claims.text: For creator-driven businesses, prioritizing stamina over virality is essential because one viral post is easy but publishing weekly for five years is what builds a sustainable audience.
claims.evidence: Lenny's personal experience growing to 1M subscribers over 5 years by writing 100% about what he is curious about

claims.text: Roughly half of Lenny's Newsletter audience are PMs, over a quarter are founders, and the fastest-growing segment is non-PMs including engineers, designers, and growth/marketing professionals.
claims.evidence: Lenny's subscriber demographic data at the 1M milestone

newsletters.title: Strategy Blocks: An operator’s guide to product strategy
newsletters.subtitle: Five steps to map out and build alignment around the future of your company

claims.text: Product strategy sits between the mission/vision and the roadmap, and a good strategy articulation includes 3-5 focus areas (strategic pillars), explicit areas to deprioritize, and clear explanations for why those choices were made.
claims.evidence: Chandra Janakiraman's Strategy Blocks framework used at Headspace, Meta, and VRChat over 10 years

claims.text: Crafting a high-quality 2-year product strategy requires 8-12 weeks through five steps: preparation, strategy sprint, design sprint, document writing, and rollout.
claims.evidence: Chandra Janakiraman's process tested in six high-stakes contexts including at Meta and Headspace

claims.text: Opportunity areas should be scored along four dimensions: expected impact, certainty of impact, clarity of levers, and uniqueness of levers, with a simple sum and sort to identify the top three strategic pillars.
claims.evidence: Core scoring methodology from the Strategy Blocks strategy sprint process

claims.text: The most effective strategists are those who can move fluidly between process and content rather than being married to either, wielding each as tools to achieve objectives.
claims.evidence: Chandra Janakiraman's experience navigating strategy tensions across multiple organizations

claims.text: Strategy and execution have a circular symbiotic relationship where each makes the other stronger, and execution should not be gated by strategy work since no-regrets work can proceed in parallel.
claims.evidence: Chandra Janakiraman's approach to resolving strategy vs. execution debates

newsletters.title: How much product managers make in the U.S., Europe, and Canada
newsletters.subtitle: Comprehensive salary, equity, and total comp benchmarks for product managers

claims.text: The median starting base salary for a PM in the U.S. is $112,000 at a public company and $96,000 at a private company, with a 90th-percentile senior IC PM hitting close to $1,000,000 in total comp.
claims.evidence: Pave compensation data from HRIS integrations at 7,500+ companies covering 23,000+ PMs globally

claims.text: New PM managers make less than senior ICs in total comp ($265,000 vs $425,000), and you have to reach M5 level to out-earn the most senior median IC PM.
claims.evidence: Pave compensation benchmarks comparing IC and manager tracks

claims.text: U.K. PMs make 65% of what the average U.S. PM makes, Canadian PMs make 60%, and the average European PM makes less than 50% of U.S. PM compensation.
claims.evidence: Pave global compensation comparison data

claims.text: PM base salaries are 15-20% lower at private companies versus public companies, and over 25% lower for product executives, but this is offset by significantly higher equity at private companies.
claims.evidence: Pave data comparing public vs private company PM compensation structures

claims.text: The median chief product officer makes $1,425,000 in total comp, and the 90th percentile makes nearly $5,000,000.
claims.evidence: Pave compensation data for executive-level product roles

newsletters.title: Pulling back the curtain on the magic of Y Combinator
newsletters.subtitle: A first-of-its-kind deep dive into the data to see what’s really working for the industry’s biggest incubator

claims.text: Roughly 4.5% of YC companies become unicorns compared to the 2.5% rate for other venture-backed seed-stage startups, and around 45% go on to raise a Series A versus the 33% industry average.
claims.evidence: Analysis of 4,939 YC companies using data from Y Combinator, Harmonic, and Crunchbase

claims.text: YC has shifted from being primarily a consumer investor to a B2B investor, with over 75% of recent batch companies being B2B, driven by the fact that most large consumer apps have already been invented.
claims.evidence: Analysis of YC batch composition trends over 20 years across 4,939 companies

claims.text: The top four YC companies (Airbnb, DoorDash, Coinbase, Instacart) account for more than 84% of all market value created, demonstrating extreme power law distribution in startup outcomes.
claims.evidence: Market capitalization analysis of YC portfolio companies

claims.text: More than 50% of YC companies are still alive after 10 years, compared to the average startup lifetime of about five years, with only 13% having gone out of business.
claims.evidence: Survival analysis of YC batch W15 and subsequent batches

claims.text: Solo founders are at a growing disadvantage at YC, with the proportion accepted declining to around 10% in the latest batches despite YC officially saying it is not a deal-breaker.
claims.evidence: YC batch composition data showing declining solo founder acceptance rates

claims.text: 99% of YC's financial returns have come from U.S.-founded companies despite accepting startups from many countries, with the most valuable European YC company (GoCardless) valued at only $2.1 billion.
claims.evidence: Geographic analysis of YC company returns and valuations

newsletters.title: Introducing the Foundation Sprint: From the creators of the Design Sprint
newsletters.subtitle: An exclusive first look at Jake Knapp and John Zeratsky’s new book, Click, and the new 2-day process for jump-starting big projects right

claims.text: The Foundation Sprint is a two-day workshop that helps teams define their Founding Hypothesis by clarifying customer, approach, and differentiation before building anything.
claims.evidence: Jake Knapp and John Zeratsky created the Foundation Sprint based on lessons from working with over 300 companies in the past 20 years.

claims.text: Products click with customers when they make a simple, compelling promise that combines a clear target customer, their problem, a differentiated approach, and why it beats competitors.
claims.evidence: Reverse-engineered from successful products like Gmail, Blue Bottle Coffee, Flatiron Health, and Slack, each of which made a simple promise that clicked.

claims.text: If you ask three co-founders to write down their startup's target customer, you typically get three different answers, revealing that obvious strategic alignment is rarely obvious.
claims.evidence: Jake Knapp's direct experience working with startup founders, where basic questions about customers and differentiation often led to 60-minute debates.

claims.text: Teams should use the Note-and-Vote technique for fast decision-making: silent individual brainstorming, silent voting, brief debate, then the Decider makes the final call.
claims.evidence: Core technique used throughout the Foundation Sprint process developed by Jake Knapp and John Zeratsky.

claims.text: Google Meet succeeded only after the team stripped away complex features like 3D virtual conference rooms and focused on the core hypothesis of being the fastest and easiest video call software.
claims.evidence: Jake Knapp's firsthand account of spending two years failing with a complex pitch before a one-week Stockholm sprint produced a working prototype that spread across Google.

newsletters.title: What’s in your stack: The state of tech tools in 2025
newsletters.subtitle: Insights from 6,500 tech professionals on their favorite and least favorite tools

claims.text: ChatGPT is used by 90% of tech professionals surveyed, making it more widely used than Gmail (76%) or Slack (71%), representing the most significant shift in the product team tool stack in recent memory.
claims.evidence: Survey of over 6,500 tech professionals conducted in early 2025.

claims.text: Cursor is already used by 17% of all respondents and 21% of engineers despite launching just two years ago, signaling rapid adoption of AI-native development environments.
claims.evidence: Survey data from 6,500 tech professionals; v0 and Replit at ~10%, Bolt at 5%.

claims.text: Jira dominates project management with 53% market share but simultaneously tops the 'please let us switch' list, while Linear is the fastest-growing alternative already used by over 10% of participants.
claims.evidence: Survey respondents described Jira as 'overly complex' and 'full of feature creep,' while praising Linear's modern interface; Linear was founded in 2019 and is already on par with Asana (founded 2008).

claims.text: Bundling is a powerful distribution strategy but can only get you so far before well-crafted alternatives eat your lunch, as seen with Jira, Microsoft Teams, and Google Slides facing insurgent competitors.
claims.evidence: Survey shows bundled tools like Jira, Teams, and Google Slides top both usage and 'least valued' lists, while craft-focused tools like Linear, Notion, and Figma Slides are rising fast.

claims.text: When choosing tools, user experience now trumps features, as teams are increasingly willing to sacrifice deep functionality for tools that are actually pleasant to use.
claims.evidence: Tools like Linear, Notion, Slack, and Figma are praised for craft, flexibility, and workflow fit; they top the 'adjusted most valued' metric while feature-heavy incumbents top 'least valued' lists.

claims.text: Over 50% of tech professionals combine multiple AI assistants for specific use cases, such as ChatGPT plus Claude for thought partnership and ChatGPT plus Perplexity for deep research.
claims.evidence: Survey of 6,500 tech professionals showing people mix and match AI tools based on each assistant's strengths.

claims.text: Figma is used by 97% of designers as their primary design tool, while Canva is democratizing design for non-designers like PMs, marketers, and engineers.
claims.evidence: Survey data from 6,500 respondents; 90% of overall participants use Figma.

newsletters.title: Introducing Core 4: The best way to measure and improve your product velocity
newsletters.subtitle: A unified developer productivity framework from the creators of DORA, SPACE, and DevEx

claims.text: Core 4 is a unified developer productivity framework with four dimensions — Speed, Effectiveness, Quality, and Impact — that unifies the principles behind DORA, SPACE, and DevEx.
claims.evidence: Co-authored by Abi Noda and Laura Tacho in collaboration with Nicole Forsgren (DORA founder), Margaret-Anne Storey (SPACE co-author), and Michaela Greiler (DevEx co-author), informed by field experience at over 300 companies.

claims.text: Product velocity is about speed and direction; when teams focus only on moving fast without alignment on what to work on, trying to move faster can actually slow you down.
claims.evidence: Common disagreement between engineering leaders (focused on deployment frequency) and product leaders (focused on deadlines and customer value); Core 4 designed to resolve this tension.

claims.text: Improving the developer experience score (DXI) by one point saves 13 minutes per week per developer, equivalent to 10 hours annually per developer.
claims.evidence: Ongoing research from DX looking at data from 40,000 developers across 800 organizations correlating developer experience with efficiency.

claims.text: The three most common friction points slowing developer teams down are poor build and test processes, lack of time for deep work, and poor support for production debugging.
claims.evidence: Data from hundreds of companies already using Core 4 to identify top friction points in the developer experience.

claims.text: PR throughput per engineer is a controversial but useful metric when used as a system health metric alongside other metrics, as validated by companies like Meta, Microsoft, and Uber.
claims.evidence: Companies like Meta, Microsoft, and Uber have successfully used PRs per engineer as a central part of their productivity measurement; Will Larson (CTO of Carta) endorses comparing this metric across peer companies.

claims.text: Core 4 balances qualitative and quantitative measurements because quantitative data shows what is happening while qualitative insights reveal why, which is necessary to actually change team behavior.
claims.evidence: Design principle of Core 4 framework, supported by Drew Houston (Dropbox CEO) stating it gives a 'much more cohesive picture of what's happening in your organization.'

newsletters.title: Which companies accelerate PM careers most
newsletters.subtitle: A first-ever deep dive into the impact 50+ companies have had on alumni trajectories

claims.text: Revolut, N26, eBay, Plaid, and Intercom are the top five companies that most accelerate PM careers based on promotions, rise to leadership, founder rate, and first-PM rate.
claims.evidence: Analysis of hiring, firing, and promotion data for 160 million people from Live Data Technologies, examining six career trajectory metrics across 50+ companies.

claims.text: Fintech companies dominate the top 10 PM career accelerators with 5 out of 10 spots, likely because fintech work is on hard mode with complex risk, regulatory, and compliance challenges.
claims.evidence: Data analysis showed Revolut, N26, Plaid, Intuit, and Deel in top 10; Dennis Yang suggests fintech nurtures strong leaders due to difficult stakeholders and complicated tradeoffs.

claims.text: FAANG companies lag behind companies like eBay, Intuit, and LinkedIn in accelerating PM careers, possibly because PMs learn to operate well within that specific company but are less successful elsewhere.
claims.evidence: Data shows eBay, Intuit, and LinkedIn far ahead of Microsoft, Google, Apple, Meta, and Amazon across promotion rate, leadership trajectory, and founder rate metrics.

claims.text: Palantir is the standout company for fostering founders, with almost a quarter of its PM alumni going on to start their own company.
claims.evidence: Palantir ranked first by far in founder rate among all companies analyzed, based on Live Data Technologies career trajectory data.

newsletters.title: How GiveDirectly increased donations by over $3 million/year through experimentation
newsletters.subtitle: Wins, misses, and lessons from GiveDirectly’s donation-optimizing journey

claims.text: Adding one-click PayPal and mobile wallet checkout options increased GiveDirectly's online donations by 14%, raising an incremental $1.3 million per year.
