claims.evidence: Segment's advisor told them 'double your price, if they say yes, keep doubling'; Stytch regretted not revisiting their simple pricing sooner

claims.text: Databricks' revenue flatlined when they tried a pure zero-touch PLG motion in 2015, and their CEO now believes PLG without a sales force does not work for enterprise products.
claims.evidence: Ali Ghodsi, CEO of Databricks, described revenue flatlining in Q2-Q3 2015 after telling sales to stop engaging customers

newsletters.title: How to build a killer sales pitch
newsletters.subtitle: And why your real competition is “no decision”

claims.text: 40-60% of B2B purchase processes result in 'no decision' not because the status quo won, but because buyers could not figure out how to confidently make a decision.
claims.evidence: Analysis of over 2 million sales calls from The JOLT Effect by Matthew Dixon and Ted McKenna

claims.text: A sales pitch should be structured as a setup (market insight, alternative approaches, perfect world) followed by a follow-through (differentiated value), rather than a feature walkthrough.
claims.evidence: April Dunford's pitch structure used with 200+ B2B companies from seed-stage to Google and Epic Games

claims.text: Your real competition in B2B sales is not other vendors but buyer indecision, because B2B buyers are more worried about messing up than missing out.
claims.evidence: JOLT Effect research finding that most no-decisions stem from inability to make confident choices, not from preferring status quo

newsletters.title: Hiring your early team
newsletters.subtitle: Part six of my seven-part series on kickstarting and scaling a B2B business

claims.text: Over two-thirds of top B2B companies hired an engineer as employee number one, and 100% hired at least one engineer among their first three hires.
claims.evidence: Survey of first hires across Segment, Coda, Vanta, Sprig, Amplitude, and 20+ other companies

claims.text: Every B2B founder should start with founder-led sales and wait until $300-500K ARR before hiring their first full-time salesperson.
claims.evidence: Consistent pattern across Vanta ($500K), Amplitude (~$300K), Stytch (low six figures), Hex ($250K), and others

claims.text: Your first sales hire should be a 'hungry senior AE' who can break new ground rather than someone who excels only at repeatable processes from later-stage companies.
claims.evidence: Recurring theme across interviews with founders of Vanta, Stytch, Zip, Sprig, Census, and Gusto

claims.text: Cold outreach through LinkedIn and GitHub is the second most common channel for finding early startup employees, and is accessible to any founder regardless of network size.
claims.evidence: Multiple founders described finding key early hires through cold LinkedIn messages and GitHub open-source contributions

newsletters.title: How Linear builds product
newsletters.subtitle: Karri Saarinen, co-founder and CEO, on taste over metrics, product teams over product managers, strategy over OKRs—and the importance of craft, profitability, and good judgment

claims.text: Linear operates without dedicated product managers for each team; instead PM duties are distributed across engineering and design, because distributing product thinking across the team produces higher quality than outsourcing thinking to a single PM.
claims.evidence: Linear CEO Karri Saarinen describes their model with only one Head of Product for the entire 50-person company

claims.text: Linear uses no A/B tests and no metrics-based goals for individual projects; decisions are based on taste and opinions, validated through beta testing and conviction rather than data.
claims.evidence: Karri Saarinen explains they have only a North Star company-level metric and use taste over data for product decisions

claims.text: Linear avoids durable cross-functional teams and instead assembles project teams that disperse once the project is done, which prevents people from getting trapped in their product area and losing broader context.
claims.evidence: Karri Saarinen describes how project leads can be engineers or designers, and teams reform for each project

claims.text: Linear uses paid work trials of 1-5 days as the final hiring step, where candidates join the team and work on a real project, which gives both sides a much better signal than traditional interviews.
claims.evidence: Karri Saarinen describes the work trial process where candidates get codebase access and join meetings

newsletters.title: The Magic Loop
newsletters.subtitle: A framework for rapid career growth

claims.text: The Magic Loop for career growth is a five-step process: do your current job well, ask your manager how you can help, do what they ask, ask for work that grows your skills toward a goal, then repeat.
claims.evidence: Ethan Evans used this framework to rise to VP at Amazon over 15 years, and his direct reports have become executives across the industry

claims.text: Managers are very rarely offered help by their direct reports, so simply offering to help makes you stand out and creates a collaborative relationship that leads to career growth.
claims.evidence: Ethan Evans confirmed with many managers that offers of help are unusual and deeply appreciated

claims.text: The advanced form of the Magic Loop is to progress from asking what needs to be done, to suggesting ideas, to simply seeing what is needed and doing it autonomously.
claims.evidence: Ethan Evans describes three stages of evolution, with the most senior people blending all three styles

newsletters.title: A guide for finding product-market fit in B2B
newsletters.subtitle: Part five of my seven-part series on kickstarting and scaling a B2B business

claims.text: The median time from idea to feeling product-market fit in B2B is roughly 2 years, with 9-18 months of iteration after launching a working product.
claims.evidence: Data from 24 top B2B startups showing timelines to first product, first customer, and first feeling of PMF

claims.text: Product-market fit is never a binary yes-or-no moment but a gradual process of finding fit with larger and larger market segments, and many founders never fully feel they have achieved it.
claims.evidence: Quotes from founders of Coda, Retool, Databricks, Gusto, Hex, and Persona all describing PMF as ongoing and never fully achieved

claims.text: The PMF journey in B2B follows five steps: get one company to love your product, get one to pay meaningful money, get multiple companies paying, notice a shift from push to pull, then grow consistently.
claims.evidence: Synthesis of interviews with 20+ B2B founders organized into a progressive framework

claims.text: B2B founders should spend 50% of their time talking to customers and 50% building; most technical founders over-index on building and waste time solving problems without real traction behind them.
claims.evidence: Amplitude CEO Spenser Skates describes wasting a year building when he should have spent half that time with customers

newsletters.subtitle: Ten lessons learned from building this newsletter

claims.text: Quality plus consistency is all that matters for newsletter growth; design, title, strategy, and growth plans are secondary to publishing valuable content people want to share repeatedly.
claims.evidence: Lenny Rachitsky's experience growing to 500K subscribers on Substack with no growth hacks beyond consistent quality

claims.text: There is a strong correlation between time spent on a post and how well it performs; Lenny spends a median of 10 hours per post with some taking hundreds of hours.
claims.evidence: Lenny's personal data correlating time invested with post performance across years of publishing

claims.text: Before going all-in on content creation, spend at least 10 years doing the actual work first, because real-life experience is what allows you to contribute something new to the conversation.
claims.evidence: Lenny's advice based on his own path from 7 years at Airbnb to newsletter, plus Elena Verna's concept of career optionality

newsletters.title: How to find and win your first 10 B2B customers
newsletters.subtitle: Part four of my seven-part series on kickstarting and scaling a B2B business

claims.text: None of the seven strategies for finding your first 10 B2B customers scale, and that is precisely why they work: in B2B it always starts with hand-to-hand combat.
claims.evidence: Synthesis from 20+ B2B founder interviews showing personal networks, cold outbound, investor networks, communities, content, press, and launches

claims.text: Trust is the secret weapon for winning early B2B customers; start with channels that have the most innate trust (personal network) and work outward to cold outbound.
claims.evidence: Pattern across Figma, Ramp, Census, Gong, and Salesforce where earliest customers came through trusted personal connections

claims.text: Cold outbound works for finding early B2B customers if done creatively; Figma's Dylan Field built a custom script to find influential designers on Twitter, and Retool used filtered Crunchbase data to target operationally heavy companies.
claims.evidence: Specific tactics from Figma, Retool, and Zip that led to their earliest customers through cold outreach

newsletters.title: How to identify your ideal customer profile (ICP)
newsletters.subtitle: Part three of my seven-part series on kickstarting and scaling a B2B business

claims.text: Every successful B2B startup landed on at least three attributes to describe their ICP, and founders should aim to get super-specific and almost comically narrow with their initial ICP definition.
claims.evidence: ICP data from Gusto (6 attributes), Gong (3), Snyk (3), Linear, Vanta, and 15+ other companies

claims.text: Outbound sales signal is the cleanest data for identifying your ICP; leads from investors and friends muddy the signal because people may take meetings out of relationship obligation rather than genuine interest.
claims.evidence: Retool CEO David Hsu describes how investor-sourced leads created fake interest versus pure cold outbound which gave clean signal

claims.text: Four signs you are converging on the right ICP are: a significant increase in conversion rate, a significant increase in enthusiasm, a stronger desire from prospects to take action now, and 'the nod' when you describe the pain.
claims.evidence: Patterns from Stytch (5-10% vs 40-50% conversion), Census (data persona got more enthused), Sprig (Robinhood installed immediately), Hex (the knowing nod)

newsletters.title: How to hire your first growth team
newsletters.subtitle: Practical advice from 12 world-class growth experts, including Lauryn Isford, Casey Winters, Melissa Tan, Adam Fishman, Laura Schaffer, John Egan, Hila Qu, Adam Grenier, and Ben Williams

claims.text: Growth teams should not be hired before product-market fit is validated; growth is about increasing distribution of core product value, and if PMF does not exist, there is nothing to grow.
claims.evidence: Consensus from 12 growth experts including leaders from Lyft, Pinterest, Uber, Airtable, Notion, Snyk, and Dropbox

claims.text: A growth leader should not be your first growth hire; instead hire a builder-profile IC focused on a specific growth lever like acquisition or activation, because even experienced leaders take 6-12 months to understand the local problem.
claims.evidence: Elena Verna and Andrea Wang's synthesis from 12 top growth practitioners across multiple companies

claims.text: 90% of the time, acquisition is the first goal new growth teams should take on, because there is more volume for experimentation and the biggest drop-offs occur in the acquisition stage.
claims.evidence: Recommendation from growth experts citing higher user volume and biggest funnel drop-offs at acquisition stage

newsletters.title: How to validate your B2B startup idea
newsletters.subtitle: Part two of my seven-part series on how to kickstart and scale a B2B business

claims.text: About 40% of top B2B startups pivoted at least once before finding their winning idea, a much higher pivot rate than the approximately 20% in B2C.
claims.evidence: Data from 20+ B2B companies including Retool (started as UK Venmo), Amplitude (voice recognition app), Segment (classroom tool), Vanta (B2B Alexa)

claims.text: Founders spoke to a median of 30 potential customers before feeling confident their idea was solid, with some like Zip speaking to 75 people in just 2-3 weeks.
claims.evidence: Survey data across 20+ B2B founders; Zip did 75 interviews producing 110 pages of notes, Gusto did exactly 30

claims.text: The strongest validation signal is deep emotional reaction from potential customers; when half the people you talk to start cursing about their current solution unprompted, you know you have found real pain.
claims.evidence: Gusto co-founder Tomer London found more than half of the 30 people he talked to started cursing about their payroll provider

newsletters.title: How the most successful B2B startups came up with their original idea
newsletters.subtitle: Part one of my seven-part series on kickstarting and scaling a B2B business

claims.text: There are three reliable paths for discovering a great B2B startup idea: past pain from a previous company, pondering and probing a space while talking to dozens of customers, or identifying present pull from something you already built.
claims.evidence: Analysis of idea origins across Gong, Retool, Linear, Persona, Hex, Figma, Notion, Canva, Segment, and 15+ other companies

claims.text: Every prosumer collaboration product including Figma, Notion, Coda, Airtable, Miro, and Slack spent three to four years wandering in the dark before finding something that clicked.
claims.evidence: Timeline data from 6 prosumer collaboration tools showing extended periods before PMF

claims.text: A great B2B startup idea requires three ingredients: the problem is important to many people willing to spend money, existing solutions are doing a subpar job (Delta-4 framework), and you are personally excited about spending years solving it.
claims.evidence: Synthesis from 20+ founder interviews combined with frameworks from Dylan Field and Kunal Shah

newsletters.title: What is good free-to-paid conversion
newsletters.subtitle: Benchmarks of good and great free-to-paid conversion rates based on a survey of 1,000+ products

claims.text: A good free-to-paid conversion rate for freemium self-serve B2B products is 3-5% and great is 6-8%; for freemium with sales-assist it is 5-7% good and 10-15% great; for free trial products it is 8-12% good and 15-25% great.
claims.evidence: Survey of 1,000+ products conducted with OpenView Ventures and Pendo

claims.text: Reverse trials, which give users full premium access then downgrade to free, convert at twice the rate of classic freemium while maintaining similar sign-up rates.
claims.evidence: Early data from the product benchmarks survey showing 2x conversion for reverse trials; examples include Airtable and Krisp

claims.text: Having sales reach out to free accounts is the single biggest driver of increasing free-to-paid conversion; nearly half of free-trial companies have sales contact more than half of sign-ups.
claims.evidence: Data from 1,000+ product survey showing sales outreach doubles conversion rates compared to pure self-serve

newsletters.title: How Shopify builds product
newsletters.subtitle: VP of Product Glen Coates on how Shopify plans around yearly themes, organizes around jobs to be done, tracks using a homegrown tool, why they shifted away from a GM structure, and much more

claims.text: Shopify organizes teams around merchant jobs to be done rather than product features, and each team must think about the full spectrum from first-time seller to enterprise brands like Supreme.
claims.evidence: VP of Product Glen Coates describes 11 teams in Core organized by JTBD with no separate Enterprise Team or Small Business Team

claims.text: Shopify's CEO Tobi sets yearly themes written from the merchant's perspective, which become the basis for six-month roadmaps and six-week sprint cycles, replacing traditional annual planning that always got torn up by March.
claims.evidence: Glen Coates describes Shopify's evolution from annual plans to theme-driven planning with Editions releases

claims.text: Shopify avoids formal OKRs because metrics-driven optimization can lead to local maxima where the product feels incohesive, and they will approve investments even without measurable metrics if the outcome improves craft and quality.
claims.evidence: Glen Coates describes Shopify redesigning the Admin for no measurable reason other than it will look better and be rad

claims.text: Shopify's internal priorities mantra is: first make the best product for merchants, second make money to do more of number one, and third never reverse priorities one and two.
claims.evidence: Internal Shopify motto described by Glen Coates as the central operating principle

newsletters.title: How today’s top consumer brands measure marketing’s impact
newsletters.subtitle: Lessons from how 40+ brands measure their marketing impact, including McDonald’s, H&M, TikTok, Amazon, Airbnb, and Uber

claims.text: Over 40% of top consumer brands use at least two marketing measurement methods (MTA, MMM, CLS) together, and about 20% use all three to triangulate the truth of what is working.
claims.evidence: Database of 42 top consumer brands including Uber, Amazon, Airbnb, McDonald's, and DoorDash

claims.text: Marketing mix modeling (MMM) has surged in popularity after iOS 14 because it only needs aggregate data and has no privacy concerns, with 82% of the top brands studied talking about MMM in case studies.
claims.evidence: Database analysis showing 35 out of 42 top brands discussing MMM, driven by Apple's ATT limiting cookie-based tracking

claims.text: The recommended triangulation approach is to use MTA for daily ad optimizations, MMM for channel budget allocation forecasting, and conversion lift studies every few months to calibrate the MMM model with causal ground truth.
claims.evidence: Framework from Michael Kaminsky (Recast) and Mike Taylor (Vexpower) based on research into 42 top brands

newsletters.title: How Snowflake builds product
newsletters.subtitle: An interview with Jeff Hollan, Director of Product for Snowflake’s Developer Platform and Ecosystem

claims.text: Snowflake aligns the entire company around 6-10 'big boulders' each year and uses customer scenarios rather than feature-area metrics for quarterly planning, with 85% of ideas coming bottom-up from PMs.
claims.evidence: Jeff Hollan, Director of Product, describes the planning process including annual six-pagers reviewed by founders

claims.text: Snowflake has zero tolerance for organizational politics and embeds data scientists directly into each product team so that data-driven decisions are part of the DNA rather than an afterthought.
claims.evidence: Jeff Hollan describes SVP Christian Kleinerman messaging about dashboard changes within 24-48 hours and no-politics culture

claims.text: Snowflake hires 'drivers' instead of 'passengers' and starts almost every product interview with a candidate presentation on any topic they choose to assess communication, passion, and product sense.
claims.evidence: Snowflake's interview process described by Jeff Hollan, including presentation format and values-based evaluation

newsletters.title: How to pass any first-round interview (even in a terrible talent market)
newsletters.subtitle: Learn how to get that strong yes without burning yourself out with interview prep

claims.text: For interview prep, pick 3-5 recent major projects and recall every detail about them rather than writing answers to hundreds of behavioral questions, because deep recall of a few projects prevents brain overload and blackouts.
claims.evidence: Coach Erika's method used by 200+ coached job seekers with 93% landing at Google, Meta, Uber, Airbnb, or Stripe

claims.text: Use STAR++ for behavioral questions: after stating Situation, Tasks, Actions, and Results, add what you learned and how you evolved your approach in a future situation to demonstrate growth mindset.
claims.evidence: Coach Erika's enhanced STAR framework derived from coaching 200+ tech professionals

claims.text: Interviews are often won or lost by the questions you ask the interviewer at the end; formulating high-signal questions that show you have done your research gets interviewers thinking and makes you stand out.
claims.evidence: Coach Erika's observation from 200+ coaching engagements about impact of candidate questions on interview outcomes

newsletters.title: What 5 years at Reddit taught us about building for a highly opinionated user base
newsletters.subtitle: A framework, from the front lines of Reddit, for turning a sometimes combative relationship with users into a strong, productive partnership

claims.text: The Trust Vault is a metaphor for how much trust your user base has in you; it can be filled and depleted, and you should measure it with periodic surveys asking users to rate how much they trust your staff to do the right thing.
claims.evidence: Framework developed over 5+ years at Reddit, where trust scores informed launch timing and product decisions

claims.text: Public forums are the wrong place for nuanced product discussions; instead form a private advisory council of representative users who can give candid feedback without the incentive to 'win' the conversation.
claims.evidence: Reddit's experience where public comment threads devolved into grandstanding but private advisory council calls produced constructive feedback

claims.text: If you ask users for feedback but do not act on it, you burn more trust than if you had never asked; the golden rule is to ask for feedback only if you will genuinely consider it.
claims.evidence: Reddit's experience seeing trust depletion when feedback was collected but ignored, and trust gains when feedback was visibly acted on

newsletters.title: How to create an exceptional coverage plan for your parental leave
newsletters.subtitle: A step-by-step guide and plug-and-play template for going on leave with peace of mind

claims.text: Start your parental leave coverage plan at least 3-4 months before your due date, assign a single DRI per project or focus area, and finalize the plan one month before the due date in case the baby comes early.
claims.evidence: Tamara Hinckley's template used at Pinterest, referred to as the 'gold standard' in their product org

claims.text: Do not share your contact information broadly during parental leave; plan for very limited or no availability especially in the first few weeks, and let your manager be the single point of contact for emergencies.
claims.evidence: Tamara Hinckley's experience helping many people prepare for leave and seeing that over-availability undermines DRI empowerment

newsletters.title: The unconventional Palantir principles that catalyzed a generation of startups
newsletters.subtitle: How the principles we learned at Palantir spawned nine unicorns and 100+ venture-backed companies

claims.text: Forward-deploying engineers to literally do the customer's job with your product for months at a time produces an order of magnitude more customer understanding than interviews or phone calls.
claims.evidence: Palantir's Forward Deployed Engineering team embedded in customer operations centers for months, leading to $100M+ deals and spawning practices at Anduril

claims.text: Hiring the literal world's number one person in each critical discipline creates cascading advantages because top people have disproportionately more access to knowledge, opportunity, and other top talent.
claims.evidence: Palantir hired the most decorated Navy SEAL commanders; alumni went on to build 9 unicorns and 100+ venture-backed companies

claims.text: Delay building a PM organization as long as possible; putting great engineers directly in front of customer problems often produces better results than adding a PM layer that introduces lossy communication.
claims.evidence: Palantir operated for a decade without formal PM titles; the first CPO was an engineer who never had a product title

claims.text: Build features that magnify value over time by creating growing data assets or network effects where each user's contribution makes the product more valuable for the next user.
claims.evidence: Palantir's Gotham platform 'tagging' feature where each user's structured data creation made the system exponentially more valuable for subsequent users

newsletters.title: How a traumatic brain injury made me a better PM—and person
newsletters.subtitle: Takeaways from a period of extreme adversity

claims.text: Viewing your calendar as an energy management tool and mapping energy levels across the week reveals obvious killers like meeting-heavy Mondays and back-to-back meetings that cause progressive burnout.
claims.evidence: Amol Avasare's recovery from TBI forced him to restructure his calendar and discovered he became a more effective operator with fewer hours

claims.text: Setting a daily work-hour limit raises the bar for what is worth your time and naturally leads to better prioritization; reduced hours combined with empowering teammates improved both team performance and satisfaction.
claims.evidence: Amol's experience working 2 hours less per day post-TBI while using p0/p1/p2 priorities led to better outcomes

claims.text: Tactical deep breathing with a 2:1 exhale-to-inhale ratio stimulates the parasympathetic nervous system and can calm you down before product reviews or after difficult meetings in as little as 20-30 seconds.
claims.evidence: Amol's TBI recovery practice, grounded in autonomic nervous system science showing exhaling activates rest-and-digest response

newsletters.title: What working at Figma taught me about customer obsession
newsletters.subtitle: VP of Product Sho Kuwamoto on the importance of feel, service, and staying close to customers

claims.text: Engineers spending 20% of their time on customer support actually makes product development faster, not slower, because teams figure out what matters to customers quickly.
claims.evidence: Figma VP of Product Sho Kuwamoto found that despite engineers spending ~20% of time helping customers, the team moved faster because they built a reputation for responsiveness and focused on what mattered.

claims.text: A service-oriented mindset where the product is just a tool to solve user problems produces better outcomes than a product-oriented mindset where user problems are tools to improve the product.
claims.evidence: Sho Kuwamoto describes inverting jobs-to-be-done so the user's needs are the primary focus, treating SaaS literally as providing a service.

claims.text: The feel of software is vital to product-led growth adoption, and launching with a polished but feature-limited product is better than launching with all features but poor feel.
claims.evidence: Figma cut multiplayer from its initial release to ensure the core felt great, reasoning that starting with a bad feel creates a credibility problem that's hard to recover from.

claims.text: The more frequently a piece of software is used, the more important its feel becomes as a factor in adoption.
claims.evidence: Sho Kuwamoto argues that daily-use tools like communication and productivity apps are where feel is make-or-break, drawing from Figma's focus on performance, familiarity, and consistency.

newsletters.title: How Notion builds product
newsletters.subtitle: Chief Product and Technology Officer Michael Manapat on Notion’s evolving internal processes, product reviews, planning cadences, and increasing shift to synchronous communication

claims.text: Notion has fewer than 15 PMs out of 550 people and waited until they had 50-60 engineers before hiring their first product manager.
claims.evidence: Michael Manapat, Notion's CPTO, shared the team size and noted that while engineering-driven culture is great, the lack of PMs early on meant missing dedicated user feedback and strategy work.

claims.text: Notion uses a four-checkpoint product review process covering problem statement, direction options, full solution with designs, and ship candidate quality check.
claims.evidence: Michael Manapat described how these four async checkpoints replaced unstructured working sessions, ensuring feedback comes at known moments rather than days before launch.

claims.text: Async product check-ins don't work well for exploring design directions because documenting options in writing is too time-consuming, making synchronous in-person discussions more effective for that stage.
claims.evidence: Notion found people spent too much time documenting exploration options and the async back-and-forth dragged out, so they switched direction exploration to 30-minute in-person discussions.

claims.text: Having data, design, research, engineering, PM, UXR, and security all report to the same leader increases proactive information sharing across functions.
claims.evidence: Michael Manapat noted that having a mixed leadership group enables more crosstalk, with people surfacing issues proactively when they hear about plans from other functions.

claims.text: EMs and PMs should have joint responsibility for everything including product and business outcomes rather than operating in disjointed spheres of accountability.
claims.evidence: Notion wants EMs and PMs to be 'joined at the hip,' both owning outcomes, with PMs doing one-on-ones with engineers if needed and EMs working with Customer Success if needed.

newsletters.title: How Ramp builds product
newsletters.subtitle: Geoff Charles, VP of Product, on Ramp's unique culture of velocity, efficiency, and empowerment

claims.text: Ramp reached $100 million ARR with fewer than five PMs and fewer than 50 engineers, demonstrating that extreme efficiency is possible by restricting headcount.
claims.evidence: Geoff Charles, VP of Product, shared that Ramp deliberately kept teams small and restricted PM hiring until it was obvious product was the bottleneck to velocity.

claims.text: Product strategy should start bottom-up with teams closest to the problem developing their own roadmaps, then co-developing the top-level strategy.
claims.evidence: Ramp's planning process empowers pod-level teams to develop bottom-up roadmaps using a seven-part strategy template (Goal, Hypothesis, Right to Win, Metric, Initiatives, Risks, Long-Term Outcomes).

claims.text: If your speed of shipping is extremely high, the cost of being wrong is much lower, making velocity itself a competitive advantage.
claims.evidence: Ramp's entire culture is organized around velocity, from decision-making to team sizing to process design, and Geoff Charles identifies speed as the core competitive strategy.

claims.text: PMs should find, pitch, and close the first 5-10 customers for their products themselves before handing off to sales.
claims.evidence: Ramp requires PMs to sell their own products initially, forcing them to iterate on positioning and pitch until they get it right, and if they can't find 10 customers, the product hasn't found demand.

claims.text: OKRs should not be used for performance management because failing to hit a cross-functional OKR doesn't mean everyone tied to that goal underperformed.
claims.evidence: Ramp focuses OKRs on things they want to improve vs. maintain, and each team is assessed by different function-specific metrics like quota attainment or customer satisfaction.

claims.text: Hiring people with high slope (learning ability) over high intercept (current domain expertise) is critical at fast-growing companies because scope will be much bigger in six months.
claims.evidence: Ramp greatly discounts domain expertise in favor of intrinsic curiosity and learning ability, and hires many former and future founders.

newsletters.title: Your startup idea probably isn’t venture-scale
newsletters.subtitle: And that’s OK

claims.text: A venture-scale startup must have a path to $100 million per year in revenue and $1 billion+ valuation within 10 years.
claims.evidence: Multiple VCs including Sequoia's Stephanie Zhan and Index Ventures' Nina Achadjian confirm this threshold as the minimum for VC fund economics to work.

claims.text: When a great team meets a lousy market, market wins, making market size more important than team quality.
claims.evidence: Classic advice from Andy Rachleff (co-founder of Benchmark Capital) cited by Lenny as a foundational insight for evaluating venture-scale ideas.

claims.text: Making something people want is necessary but not sufficient for a venture-scale business; you also need enough people who want it and the ability to make enough money from them.
claims.evidence: Lenny lists 10 examples of beloved products (Trello, Basecamp, DuckDuckGo, etc.) that likely generate tens of millions but will never be venture-scale.

newsletters.title: How Miro builds product
newsletters.subtitle: Varun Parmar, Chief Product Officer at Miro, shares what they've learned about OKRs, planning, design reviews, prioritization, rituals—plus tons of plug-and-play templates you can start using today

claims.text: Miro's AMPED structure (Analytics, Marketing, Product, Engineering, Design) brings all required functions into product teams from the start, resulting in more effective product development.
claims.evidence: Varun Parmar describes how AMPED ensures diverse perspectives and includes Product Marketing from the beginning to think about positioning and differentiation alongside building.

claims.text: Miro's general rule of thumb is to devote 60% of resources to product innovation, 20% to running the business and maintenance, and 20% to reducing tech debt.
claims.evidence: Varun Parmar shared this as Miro's default resource allocation, noting it varies based on product maturity with some teams needing more tech debt reduction or product innovation.

claims.text: Having OKRs at every organizational level creates overlapping initiatives, excessive process time, and too many priorities, so reducing OKR layers and frequency improves clarity.
claims.evidence: Miro removed extra OKR layers, moved to semi-annual frequency, and limited objectives to 3-4 with 2-3 KRs each after finding quarterly multi-level OKRs consumed too much time and created duplication.

claims.text: Monthly design reviews where the team reviews everything shipped and identifies what is and isn't high quality is more effective for raising the quality bar than abstract principles.
claims.evidence: Miro iterated from the Mona Lisa principle (ship things you'd be proud to put your name on) to concrete monthly reviews with real examples of high and low quality shipped work.

newsletters.title: How to do linear regression and correlation analysis
newsletters.subtitle: Steps, methods, tools, and use cases for locating predictable user actions and improving retention

claims.text: Always run a correlation analysis before running a linear regression, because if variables aren't correlated, the regression will produce inconclusive patterns.
claims.evidence: Olga Berezovsky recommends running correlation first to confirm whether X and Y are related before expanding to prediction with regression analysis.

claims.text: Neither correlation nor linear regression proves causation, but correlation confirms the relationship between variables while regression shows how much one variable affects another and can predict behavior.
claims.evidence: Olga Berezovsky distinguishes between correlation (relationship and connection) and regression (prediction and estimation), using MyFitnessPal food logging and retention as examples.

claims.text: Product analytics tools like Amplitude Compass and Mixpanel Signal can quickly identify which features correlate most strongly with user retention.
claims.evidence: The post demonstrates using Amplitude and Mixpanel to find that food logging correlates strongly with retention at MyFitnessPal while other features do not.

newsletters.title: What jury duty taught me about product management
newsletters.subtitle: Unexpected parallels and hidden lessons about leadership and product management from my experience on a jury

claims.text: The most effective presentation structure is to tell them what you'll tell them, then tell them, then tell them what you told them.
claims.evidence: Lenny found opening statements in trials unexpectedly impactful because knowing key points upfront prevented jumping to conclusions, connecting to the Aristotelian triptych and Minto pyramid principle.

claims.text: Don't go into important decision meetings without knowing how key stakeholders will feel; do the pre-work through 15-minute pre-meetings to get alignment in low-stakes one-on-one discussions.
claims.evidence: Lawyers never ask questions they don't know the answer to by doing extensive pre-trial preparation, and the same principle applies to PM stakeholder meetings.

claims.text: Influence fundamentally boils down to two things: how convincing your evidence is and how much people trust you and your sources.
claims.evidence: Jury instructions focused solely on evidence and witness trustworthiness while excluding attorney arguments, speculation, and emotion, yet produced the correct verdict.

newsletters.title: How to use ChatGPT in your PM work
newsletters.subtitle: Real-life examples (and actual prompts) of how PMs are already using ChatGPT day-to-day

claims.text: Learning to work alongside AI will quickly become table stakes for product managers, just as Grammarly became for writing and Copilot for engineering.
claims.evidence: Lenny draws a parallel between AI tools becoming essential for PMs and how Grammarly, Copilot, Firefly, and Midjourney have become standard for their respective fields.

claims.text: The best way to prepare for the AI future in product management is to dive in and get your hands dirty rather than waiting for the technology to mature.
claims.evidence: Dan Shipper's advice emphasized in the post, with 12 concrete PM use cases including summarizing feedback, roadmap ideation, PRDs, market research, and technical questions.

newsletters.title: Product-led marketing
newsletters.subtitle: How to get traffic and users for your product at a near-$0 CAC

claims.text: Standout PLG products disproportionately attract users through organic search/SEO (40% of new users) and product virality (16% of new users).
claims.evidence: Kyle Poyar cites data from OpenView showing that companies like Airtable, Miro, Snyk, Webflow, and Zapier get 56% of new users from these two channels.

claims.text: Building free sidecar products specifically designed to attract users through SEO is one of the most effective product-led marketing strategies.
claims.evidence: Examples include Snyk's Open Source Vulnerability Database, Similarweb's Top Websites Ranking, HubSpot's Website Grader, and Ahrefs's Free Backlink Checker.

claims.text: For PLG products, the target marketing KPI should be product activation (activated signups), not website traffic or monthly signups.
claims.evidence: Kyle Poyar argues activated signups are the best north star for PLG marketers because they measure users who reach the aha moment, not just top-of-funnel volume.

claims.text: PLG companies need to keep customer acquisition cost below $1 per unique visitor because the average PLG product generates only $1-2 in first-year revenue per visitor.
claims.evidence: OpenView benchmark data shows a typical PLG funnel converts 6% of visitors to free signups and 5% of those to paid, yielding $1-2 per visitor before churn and costs.

newsletters.title: How Duolingo builds product
newsletters.subtitle: Cem Kansu on Duolingo’s unique team structure, steady planning cadence, design review process, OKR templates, team rituals, and more

claims.text: Duolingo uses a co-lead structure with PM and engineering leads jointly heading each team, which provides complementary skills and divides leadership responsibilities.
claims.evidence: Cem Kansu describes how the co-lead model proved more effective than single-function leads, combining business acumen with technical expertise.

claims.text: Duolingo runs over 200 A/B tests at any given time, testing every product change as an experiment before rolling it out.
claims.evidence: Cem Kansu emphasizes that Duolingo is experimentation-driven and data-obsessed, with PMs becoming experts in designing good A/B tests and analyzing massive datasets.

claims.text: For mature product teams, balancing resources as a 50/50 portfolio between big features and incremental improvements is optimal because incremental changes compound but you must avoid local maxima.
claims.evidence: Duolingo treats resource allocation as an investment portfolio varying by team maturity: 50/50 for mature products, 90/10 for pre-PMF products like Duolingo Math.

claims.text: Feature-based teams without clear success metrics can measure progress through a combination of internal dogfooding buzz, user research, public forum sentiment, and long-term holdout experiments.
claims.evidence: Duolingo's social features team uses Duo employee usage enthusiasm, Reddit/Twitter reactions, and 3+ month holdout experiments since social features take time to show effects.

newsletters.title: Growth ideas
newsletters.subtitle: A long and growing list of ideas to inspire your team’s thinking on increasing top-of-funnel, monetization, conversion, retention, and velocity

claims.text: Letting users pause or snooze a subscription instead of canceling is an effective retention strategy.
claims.evidence: Listed as a concrete retention improvement tactic in Lenny's curated list of growth ideas from practitioners.

claims.text: Narrowing your team's focus by removing one goal or project is one of the most effective ways to improve team velocity.
claims.evidence: Listed as the first velocity improvement tactic, alongside no-meeting days and assigning a single DRI for each outcome.

claims.text: Investing in resurrecting churned users is a high-leverage growth tactic since growth equals new users plus resurrected users minus churned users.
claims.evidence: Listed as a retention improvement strategy with the growth equation framing that highlights resurrection as an often-overlooked growth lever.

newsletters.title: How to be prepared for layoffs
newsletters.subtitle: What you can do today to avoid getting caught off guard in case layoffs come for you

claims.text: The single most important thing to do to prepare for potential layoffs is to document your accomplishments with metrics, screenshots, and stories while you still have access.
claims.evidence: The most common piece of advice from recently laid-off professionals was saving accomplishment details, since you immediately lose access to supporting evidence after layoff.

claims.text: Creating distance between your work identity and your personal identity is essential preparation for career disruptions like layoffs.
claims.evidence: Multiple laid-off professionals emphasized not letting work define you, with some reporting that being laid off opened new opportunities they hadn't explored.

newsletters.title: Growth inflections
newsletters.subtitle: Stories behind growth unlocks for Figma, Facebook, Airbnb, YouTube, Tinder, DoorDash, and others

claims.text: The majority of growth inflections at successful companies sprang from a product improvement rather than external events or growth tactics.
claims.evidence: Research into two dozen successful products found product improvements (Figma Team Libraries, Snap Stories, Netflix subscription model, Tinder Android app) as the most common inflection source.

claims.text: The most durable growth inflections come from doubling down on a company's primary growth engine like SEO or virality rather than from one-off events.
claims.evidence: Pinterest 100-1000x'd SEO traffic, Airtable's templates accounted for a third of site traffic, and Dropbox's Samsung partnership drove 100M+ signups by leaning into their core channels.

claims.text: Long-term sustainable growth requires all three pieces: ongoing product improvements, ongoing events that spread the word, and a well-oiled growth engine.
claims.evidence: Lenny synthesizes the research finding that while single moments can ignite growth, durable businesses need continuous improvement across all three dimensions.

claims.text: Figma's biggest growth inflection was the release of Team Libraries in 2017, which provided so much leverage to design teams that it changed the competitive conversation.
claims.evidence: Badrul Farooqi, Figma's first PM, identified Team Libraries as the feature that made Figma an obvious winner against Sketch and others.

newsletters.title: I built a Lenny chatbot using GPT-3. Here’s how to build your own.
newsletters.subtitle: A step-by-step guide to building an AI chatbot

claims.text: GPT-3 tends to hallucinate confident but incorrect answers, especially when it lacks the right training data, making context injection via embeddings essential for accurate chatbots.
claims.evidence: Dan Shipper demonstrated that GPT-3 gave wrong answers about Lenny's newsletter data but answered correctly when relevant article chunks were injected into the prompt.

claims.text: Every newsletter, book, blog, and podcast used as evergreen reference information can now be repackaged as a chatbot, creating a new content format and monetization opportunity for creators.
claims.evidence: Dan Shipper built a functioning Lenny chatbot in a couple hours using GPT-3 and embeddings, arguing this will become a standard way to interact with creator content.

newsletters.title: How Coda builds product
newsletters.subtitle: Lane Shackleton, CPO at Coda, shares his team’s templates, day-to-day processes, and hard-earned lessons

claims.text: Planning should take up no more than 10% of the execution time period, so quarterly planning should take less than nine days.
claims.evidence: Coda CPO Lane Shackleton states this as a general rule their teams follow, evolving from two-week sprints to Quarterly Plus planning as the company scaled.

claims.text: OKRs are not a strategy and should not be treated as a replacement for a strategy document that describes broader challenges with a clear why and how.
claims.evidence: Lane Shackleton emphasizes strategy planning happens independent of OKR planning, and warns it's easy to fall into the trap of using OKRs as a strategy substitute.

claims.text: The job of PMs and designers is to turn ambiguity into clarity, first for themselves, then for their teams, and ultimately for customers.
claims.evidence: Shishir Mehrotra, Coda CEO, instilled this principle that Lane Shackleton considers core to product development philosophy at Coda.

claims.text: Product teams should distinguish between input metrics they can control and output metrics like activation that are lagging indicators, and goal themselves on input metrics.
claims.evidence: Lane Shackleton notes activation is hard to move as an OKR because it's a lagging output metric, so teams should goal on metrics they control and ask whether inputs will move the output.

claims.text: If you want to be great at building product, your preparation should look more like a professional athlete's than someone winging it.
claims.evidence: Lane Shackleton advocates practicing hard conversations 20 times, iterating on pitches 10 times, and doing 4-5 editing passes on communications.

newsletters.title: Five steps to starting your product-led growth motion, part 2
newsletters.subtitle: A guide to setting up your PLG infrastructure, tool stack, and team

claims.text: Product-led growth is essentially data-led growth because without usage data infrastructure, you cannot activate or convert free users.
claims.evidence: Hila Qu identifies data infrastructure (product analytics, customer 360 database) as an absolute must-have for PLG, noting many B2B companies lack sufficient product analytics.

claims.text: Companies commonly skip buying an experimentation platform and jump to building their own, which is a mistake because it requires not just engineering but also data science and statistical expertise.
claims.evidence: Hila Qu recommends smaller teams use tools like Optimizely, VWO, or Eppo rather than reinventing the wheel with homegrown platforms.

claims.text: Heads of growth most commonly report to product leaders (CPO), but sometimes roll up into sales/GTM organizations to better align PLG incentives with revenue targets.
claims.evidence: Hila Qu describes two common org structures: growth under CPO for product-native alignment, or under CRO to minimize conflict between growth and sales teams on quotas.

newsletters.title: Five steps to starting your product-led growth motion
newsletters.subtitle: An in-depth guide to getting started with PLG

claims.text: For B2B PLG products, there are four distinct aha moments for different personas: user, team, buyer, and paid-customer, and most companies stop strategizing after the first.
claims.evidence: Hila Qu describes GitLab's experience where the pivotal activation moment was team-based (2+ users and 2+ features in 14 days), not individual user behavior.

claims.text: Product-led activation should be product-led, not product-only, using a multi-channel approach including lifecycle marketing, human assistance, documentation, and forums alongside product onboarding.
claims.evidence: Hila Qu emphasizes that while product onboarding touches 100% of users and is most scalable, the ideal PLG activation is led by product, powered by data, and supported by automated and human channels.

claims.text: The biggest challenge when layering PLG onto an existing sales-led company is resistance from sales teams worried about quota impact and marketing teams worried about PQLs replacing MQLs.
claims.evidence: Hila Qu identifies five common pitfalls including internal resistance, noting sales teams fear small-potato customers while marketing worries about workflow disruption.

newsletters.title: Virality is a myth (mostly)
newsletters.subtitle: And why you are probably under-investing in influencers and PR

claims.text: True virality is mostly a myth; what we call viral growth is almost always driven by one-to-many broadcasts from people with large audiences rather than many-to-many friend-to-friend spread.
claims.evidence: Yahoo researchers found 95% of news on Twitter comes from original sources or one degree of separation, and Derek Thompson's Hit Makers shows popularity is driven by the size of the largest broadcast.

claims.text: Even the most viral products like Dropbox, Facebook, Snapchat, and Instagram show roughly linear growth when you zoom out, with viral moments appearing as short-lived S-curves.
claims.evidence: Growth charts of Dropbox, Facebook, Snapchat, Slack, and Instagram all show approximately linear trajectories despite reputation for viral growth.

claims.text: To sustain growth, you need both mechanisms of virality (word of mouth, invites, referrals) and a strategy of ongoing one-to-many broadcasts (PR, influencers, TV) to keep reigniting spread.
claims.evidence: Lenny argues virality and turbo boosts go together like peanut butter and jelly; higher k-factor amplifies each broadcast, but you get no juice without large-audience exposure.

newsletters.title: How to activate your investor network
newsletters.subtitle: Examples and tactics for getting the most out of your investors

claims.text: Founders should optimize their investor network for eigenvector centrality (how many people your connections know) rather than degree centrality (how many people you know directly).
claims.evidence: Sam Corcos of Levels explains that knowing the most connected person in diverse networks gives vastly more surface area than having many connections in one dense network.

claims.text: It's almost always the founder's fault when they aren't getting value from their investors, because investors have little context and need targeted, time-bounded, specific asks.
claims.evidence: Sam Corcos sent 2,626 requests to investors over 3.3 years with a 63% response rate and 44% conversion rate by making asks targeted, time-bounded, and specific.

claims.text: Investor requests are not like precious gems with limited uses; people like being helpful, and the failure mode is making bad asks, not making too many asks.
claims.evidence: Sam Corcos found that investors often don't respond because asks are bad, not because they've been asked too many times, and maintains an abundance mindset about requests.

newsletters.title: Startup to exit: Lessons from a first-time founder
newsletters.subtitle: Why VC funding is a drug, when building in stealth is a mistake, what to do when you lose conviction, how to approach selling your company, and more

claims.text: Building a startup in stealth mode is always a mistake because it inhibits frequent market signal and greatly increases time to product-market fit.
claims.evidence: Suril Kantaria estimates stealth mode delayed key learnings about Savvy's market fit by six months, and observed the same pattern in other stealth startups.

claims.text: Pre-PMF, you want either overwhelming market pull or no sales at all; steady sales with no acceleration is a dangerous riptide that can trap you.
claims.evidence: Savvy experienced steady but non-accelerating sales that felt like winning but prevented them from recognizing poor product-market fit, and early large customers churned when cracks showed.

claims.text: VC funding creates pressure that can delay honest assessment of product-market fit because founders tell themselves that if investors backed them, they must be on the right path.
claims.evidence: Savvy raised $2.5M based on a vision rather than metrics, which committed them to a path and delayed pivoting even as the pandemic changed the landscape.

claims.text: Headcount is a cost center, not a sign of progress, and the only metric that merits congratulations is product traction and product-market fit.
claims.evidence: Savvy accepted accolades for growing the team when there was no underlying traction, and headcount later constrained their ability to pivot.

newsletters.title: How Figma builds product
newsletters.subtitle: Yuhki Yamashita, Chief Product Officer at Figma, shares lessons learned, plug-and-play templates, and fresh insights into how Figma builds product

claims.text: Figma deprecated traditional OKRs in favor of headlines, which are claims teams want to make by end of a time period, evaluated by a mix of quantitative and qualitative signals.
claims.evidence: Yuhki Yamashita replaced a spreadsheet of hundreds of task-like OKRs with headlines that captured philosophical alignment and allowed creative measurement approaches.

claims.text: PMs are responsible for making crystal clear why the team is solving a particular problem and why it's important over anything else, because clarity on the why empowers everyone to make great decisions.
claims.evidence: Yuhki Yamashita insists that when the why isn't clear, PMs should invest in internal storytelling rather than jumping to solutions.

claims.text: Using alignment widgets for anonymous voting on decisions in product reviews is more powerful than allowing the loudest people in the room to dictate outcomes.
claims.evidence: Yuhki Yamashita describes how FigJam alignment widgets let people share and vote on sentiments toward specific decisions, improving product review quality.

newsletters.title: How to determine your activation metric
newsletters.subtitle: Including a deep dive into multi-player B2B SaaS products featuring Figma, Linear, Snyk, Airtable, Slack, Asana, and Sprig

claims.text: The three-step process for finding your activation metric is: brainstorm potential aha moments, run regression to find correlation with retention, then experiment to prove causation.
claims.evidence: Six real-world examples from companies show this process in action, from initial hypothesis through correlation analysis to structured experimentation.

claims.text: Figma's core activation metric is collaboration in the same file with someone else within 24 hours, which allowed them to focus on winning design teams rather than solo users.
claims.evidence: Badrul Farooqi, Figma's first PM, shared that users who experienced real-time collaboration were much more likely to become paying customers.

claims.text: Slack's early activation metric was a team with 2+ users having 50+ messages within the first seven days, focusing on team success rather than individual user actions.
claims.evidence: Merci Grace, former Head of Growth at Slack, explained the metric captures real collaborative behavior since email between two people isn't broken the way it is for groups.

newsletters.title: What is a good activation rate
newsletters.subtitle: Benchmarks and advice for increasing your activation rate, based on a survey of 500+ products

claims.text: The average activation rate across 500+ products is 34% with a median of 25%, and for SaaS products specifically the average is 36% with a median of 30%.
claims.evidence: Based on a global benchmarking survey of over 500 responses, the largest survey of its kind, co-conducted with Yuriy Timen.

claims.text: The most common and impactful ways to increase activation are simplifying onboarding UI/UX and reducing onboarding friction, not adding more features.
claims.evidence: Survey of 500+ products found the top tactics were simpler onboarding, reduced friction, follow-up emails, optimized copy, and smarter targeting, with dozens of specific examples.

claims.text: The most common mistake when setting activation milestones is setting them too early (just completing signup) or too late (multiple purchases), when the milestone should be a leading indicator of habit formation.
claims.evidence: The survey found many companies define activation as signup completion or multiple transactions, both of which defeat the purpose of activation as a predictive and actionable metric.

claims.text: A good activation metric should be predictive of long-term retention at a rate at least 2x better for users who hit it versus those who don't.
claims.evidence: The benchmark survey defines a good activation metric as both highly predictive (2x retention difference) and highly actionable (something growth teams can directly impact).

newsletters.title: Mission → Vision → Strategy → Goals → Roadmap → Task
newsletters.subtitle: As taught to us by Oceans Eleven

claims.text: A product team's strategic hierarchy flows from Mission to Vision to Strategy to Goals to Roadmap to Task, with each level informing the next.
claims.evidence: Provides definitions and examples from Tesla, TED, Stripe, and others showing how mission drives vision drives strategy drives goals drives roadmap drives tasks

claims.text: You need goals before you can align around a roadmap because when prioritizing ideas you look at ROI, and the impact you're prioritizing against is based on how much a project will impact your goals.
claims.evidence: Explains that goals measure progress of strategy, and impact in ROI calculations is measured against goals

claims.text: A strategy is essentially a short description of your plan to win, with 3 to 5 concrete investments that, if you get right, will bring you closer to winning.
claims.evidence: References Tesla's multi-step strategy, Uber's staged approach, and Substack's three-part strategy as examples

newsletters.title: The Racecar Growth Framework—expanded and illustrated
newsletters.subtitle: The culmination of all my GTM and growth research in one image

claims.text: The Racecar Growth Framework identifies five components of startup growth: growth engine, kickstarts, turbo boosts, lubricants, and mid-stage accelerants.
claims.evidence: Comprehensive framework developed with Dan Hockenmaier showing how each component maps to a stage of growth

claims.text: There are only four common growth engines: SEO, paid ads, sales, and virality, and the growth engine is the most important component because it is the only one that can be self-sustaining.
claims.evidence: Maps growth engines to loops where output (revenue, users, content) feeds back into more growth

claims.text: When just starting out, focus energy on kickstarts and turbo boosts until your growth engine begins to drive the majority of growth, then invest in lubricants.
claims.evidence: Provides a five-stage sequencing guide for when to focus on each component of the framework

claims.text: The nine most common kickstart tactics for getting your first 1,000 users include sharing with friends, reaching out to targeted strangers, pitching where your audience hangs out, enlisting influencers, getting press, creating viral content, physical placement, hosting events, and being first on a platform.
claims.evidence: Lists tactics with examples from Cameo, YouTube, Airbnb, DoorDash, Snapchat, Tinder, and others

claims.text: The primary growth engine for nearly all of today's biggest B2B products is sales, though some have a product-led or self-service element.
claims.evidence: References a comprehensive table of B2B products and their growth engines

newsletters.title: How to get your marketing team to drive more impact
newsletters.subtitle: GACCS: A framework for aligning your marketing, product, and sales teams

claims.text: The GACCS framework (Goals, Audience, Creative, Channels, Stakeholders) is a marketing brief that keeps teams from doing busywork and drives cross-team alignment before work begins.
claims.evidence: Emily Kramer created and refined this at Asana, Carta, Ticketfly, and Astro; endorsed by marketing leaders who adopted it

claims.text: Much marketing work misses the mark because of six common failures: no goal, no prioritization, created for no one, work that does not stand out, no distribution plan, and experts excluded from the process.
claims.evidence: Emily Kramer's analysis from leading marketing teams at multiple companies

claims.text: Distribution is just as important as, if not more important than, what you are creating in marketing; if no one will see it, all the time spent making it is wasted.
claims.evidence: Emily Kramer advises that if you have no distribution plan, you should wait on creating the asset

claims.text: Areas of Responsibility (AORs) where each responsibility has exactly one DRI eliminate wasted time figuring out who to collaborate with and create extreme clarity on ownership.
claims.evidence: Learned at Asana, used successfully at Carta; template provided with marketing-specific responsibilities

newsletters.title: How to get better at influence
newsletters.subtitle: What Frodo Baggins from Lord of the Rings can teach us about influence

claims.text: The seven strategies for improving influence are: make their goals your goals, charge your trust battery, help them see what you see, show success, bring evidence, leverage authority, and be likable.
claims.evidence: Drawn from PM experience at Airbnb, supported by examples from the Superhost program launch and review system redesign

claims.text: Trust in the workplace works like a battery that you charge by doing things that build trust and tap into when you need people to trust you.
claims.evidence: Concept popularized by Tobi Lutke of Shopify; charged by delivering on promises, listening, helping, and assuming good intentions

claims.text: Before asking someone to do something for you, take 10 seconds to reflect on what they want, then frame your ask in a way that highlights how it helps them hit their goals.
claims.evidence: Demonstrated through Airbnb Superhost badge example where reframing the ask around guest-side conversion won over the search team

claims.text: The more you rely on authority to get things done, the more your own authority fades because you become just a middle person without your own voice or decision-making powers.
claims.evidence: Presented as a warning when discussing leveraging authority from executives and influential people

newsletters.title: When to sunset a feature
newsletters.subtitle: A handy checklist to help decide when it’s time to sunset a product or feature

claims.text: If a product or feature checks three of five criteria it is probably time to shut it down: less than 5% user engagement, more than 10% team resources to maintain, degrades user experience, misaligned with strategy, and very few vocal users.
claims.evidence: Checklist based on research and Airbnb experience sunsetting Guidebooks, Wish Lists, Neighborhoods, Match, Apple Watch app, and Stories

claims.text: Teams almost always wait too long to kill products; in the author's experience at Airbnb, not once did anyone regret a product or feature being sunset.
claims.evidence: Reflecting on multiple products sunset at Airbnb including Milestones, Guidebooks, Neighborhoods, and others

claims.text: A feature with low measurable impact can still be worth keeping if it supports the company's long-term strategy, as was the case with Airbnb's Superhost program which initially showed little measurable impact but was aligned with strategy.
claims.evidence: Contrasts Milestones (killed after no measurable impact and strategy misalignment) with Superhost (kept because of strategic alignment despite low initial impact)

newsletters.title: How to measure cohort retention
newsletters.subtitle: A deep dive into the formulas, visualizations, and SQL you need to accurately measure and report on cohort retention—a guest post by Olga Berezovsky

claims.text: The recommended event to use for defining 'active users' in retention measurement is the main user action rather than visits, sessions, or logins, because it gives the cleanest sample of truly active users.
claims.evidence: Olga Berezovsky's 10+ year experience as analyst at Change.org and MyFitnessPal; visits and sessions overcount while main actions provide precise data

claims.text: SaaS companies commonly make the mistake of reporting one blended retention metric mixing free and paid users, which is misleading because paid users use the product far more than free users.
claims.evidence: Analyst experience showing that blending hides the true activity of free users and the opportunity to convert them

claims.text: Retention is an output metric and should not be used as a baseline for A/B tests because user activity is only one component of retention, and changes in activity often do not lead to measurable changes in retention.
claims.evidence: Olga's experience seeing product analytics teams wonder why increased user activity didn't change retention metrics in experiments

claims.text: X-day (bounded) retention is more appropriate for SaaS products tied to specific time bounds like trial or subscription length, while unbounded retention is better for B2C consumer transactional or social businesses with chaotic engagement patterns.
claims.evidence: Olga Berezovsky's rule of thumb based on comparing SaaS subscription models with consumer social products

claims.text: At Change.org, monitoring and slicing Day 7 and Day 30 retention of petition starters helped grow their 'petition starters per day' KPI by 450%.
claims.evidence: Direct experience of Olga Berezovsky on the Petition Starters squad at Change.org

newsletters.title: All the ways to grow your product
newsletters.subtitle: Growth engines, turbo boosts, lubricants, kickstarts, and mid-stage accelerants

claims.text: Channel partnerships and geographic expansion are two mid-stage growth accelerants that are rarely useful in early stages but can significantly accelerate growth when used effectively at the right moment.
claims.evidence: Examples from Kayak/AOL, Netflix/DVD manufacturers, Spotify/telcos, and geographic expansion stories from Instacart, DoorDash, and Grubhub

claims.text: Geographic expansion is one of the biggest growth accelerants a startup can have but is something you can only really do once, and normally happens post-PMF but before the growth engine is at full steam.
claims.evidence: GTM sequences from Instacart, Tinder, Facebook, Spotify, and DoorDash all show geo-expansion between kickstart and growth engine phases

claims.text: Channel partnerships are game-changing when they work but normally go nowhere or take far too long to make a dent; founders should treat them like any high-risk/high-reward opportunity.
claims.evidence: Kayak/AOL, Netflix/DVD manufacturers, and OpenTable/Citysearch examples show both massive wins and the rarity of success

newsletters.title: Kickstarting and scaling a consumer business—Step 6: SCALE: Build your growth engine
newsletters.subtitle: How products grow, how your product will grow, and the GTM motions of today’s biggest consumer apps

claims.text: The majority of startups grow primarily through just one growth engine, and a common pitfall is trying to invest in too many engines at once without nailing any.
claims.evidence: Nearly 100% of Tinder's growth came through WOM, Calm's through paid growth, and Thumbtack's through SEO

claims.text: For consumer startups there are only three feasible growth engines since relying on sales is rarely economical: SEO, paid ads, and virality.
claims.evidence: Analysis of growth engines across dozens of consumer businesses; sales only works as a growth engine for marketplace/platform supply-side

claims.text: For marketplace and platform startups, supply driving demand is an incredibly powerful growth engine where suppliers market your platform to their own customers, as seen at DoorDash, Etsy, and Eventbrite.
claims.evidence: DoorDash restaurants printed placards and stickers, Etsy sellers did grassroots marketing, Eventbrite organizers brought built-in demand

claims.text: You will likely grow primarily through virality if you have to share the product to use it, the product is only fun with friends, or the product is super-fun to share.
claims.evidence: Examples include Dropbox/PayPal (share to use), Snapchat/Facebook (fun with friends), Tinder/Airbnb/TikTok (fun to share)

claims.text: At Airbnb, word of mouth was the biggest growth driver early on, accounting for over 50% on the guest side and over 70% on the host side.
claims.evidence: Direct quote from Gustaf Alstromer, Airbnb's first head of growth

newsletters.title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 5: RETAIN: Iterate until enough people stick around
newsletters.subtitle: How to know if you’ve got product-market fit, how to iterate toward product-market fit, and how long it usually takes

claims.text: The four clearest signs of product-market fit in decreasing confidence are: cohort retention curves flatten, explosive growth through word of mouth, users feel the product is a must-have, and strong unit economics.
claims.evidence: Research across dozens of successful consumer products including Tinder, Dropbox, Instacart, and Superhuman

claims.text: Good six-month user retention benchmarks for consumer products are approximately 25% for social, 30% for transactional, and 40% for subscription, with great being roughly 45%, 50%, and 70% respectively.
claims.evidence: Based on Lenny's prior research published in 'What is good retention'

claims.text: Product-market fit should be thought of as a spectrum of confidence that changes over time rather than a binary you-have-it or you-don't one-way door.
claims.evidence: Presented as a reframing of PMF, supported by the observation that PMF signs vary in intensity

newsletters.title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 4: Find your early adopters by doing things that don’t scale
newsletters.subtitle: Also, how today’s biggest consumer products got their first 1,000 users

claims.text: There are only seven reliable ways to kickstart consumer growth and acquire your first 1,000 users: friends and colleagues, targeted strangers, going where your audience hangs out, enlisting influencers, getting press, creating viral content, and getting physical placement.
claims.evidence: Research across dozens of consumer startups including DoorDash, Tinder, Snapchat, and Netflix

newsletters.title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 3: Craft your pitch
newsletters.subtitle: Lessons from Pinterest, Netflix, Tinder, Dropbox, DoorDash, Robinhood, Amazon, WhatsApp, and dozens of today’s most successful consumer businesses

claims.text: There are four strategies for crafting a remarkable hook: identify what is unique about your product versus alternatives, listen to how your most obsessed users describe it, determine what job your product does for people, and find what about your product grabs attention.
claims.evidence: Examples from DoorDash (selling revenue), Behance (exposure), Netflix (no late fees), Robinhood ($0 commission), Dropbox (throw away USB drive)

claims.text: If your pitch is not resonating, one of three things is happening: your pitch is bad, you are pitching the wrong people, or your product is not something people want.
claims.evidence: Netflix iterated for 18 months to find the right pitch; Pinterest founder pitched tech people first before finding female bloggers were the right audience

claims.text: DoorDash's Tony Xu spent weeks pitching restaurants on food delivery technology with slow results until he reframed the pitch as 'we are selling revenue' which changed the company's trajectory.
claims.evidence: Direct quote from Tony Xu describing the pivot from pitching technology to pitching revenue

newsletters.title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 2: Identify your super-specific who
newsletters.subtitle: Lessons from Substack, Netflix, Yelp, Discord, TikTok, Instagram, Cameo, DoorDash, and dozens of others

claims.text: When deciding who to target early on, the target customer definition must be almost comically narrow, aiming for three very specific characteristics of your ideal early adopter.
claims.evidence: Andy Johns (Facebook, Twitter, Wealthfront), Andy Rachleff (Benchmark), and Paul Graham all emphasize super-narrow targeting; examples include Wealthfront targeting 25-35-year-old engineers at pre-IPO tech companies

claims.text: Pinterest initially failed by targeting tech friends, but when Ben Silbermann found that 30-something female bloggers at Alt Summit were the right audience, the product reached over a million users within a year.
claims.evidence: Ben Silbermann's direct account of trying Apple stores, emailing tech friends, before collaborating with Victoria Smith of SFGirlByBay

claims.text: TikTok's early growth strategy treated the platform like a new country where initial wealth should be distributed to a small number of creators to make them role models that attract migration from established platforms.
claims.evidence: Direct quote from Alex Zhu, co-founder of Musical.ly and ex-president of TikTok, using the America/Europe metaphor

newsletters.title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business
newsletters.subtitle: Step 1: Come up with an idea

claims.text: There are three common approaches to coming up with a startup idea: market first (investigate a space then find a problem), experience ripe for improvement (find a broken consumer experience), and problem first (start with a problem you have experienced firsthand).
claims.evidence: Todd Jackson's research into Vanta, Flexport, Rec Room, LaunchDarkly, Pinwheel, Snackpass, Good Dog, and Cocoon

claims.text: Sitting on a couch brainstorming startup ideas in a bubble is counterproductive because all ideas seem flawed without market feedback; founders should instead get out into the market and have real conversations.
claims.evidence: Christina Cacioppo of Vanta spent months brainstorming before realizing it was unproductive; only found Vanta by interviewing founder friends about their pain points

newsletters.title: How to build trust in a marketplace
newsletters.subtitle: Lessons from Lyft, Thumbtack, Rover, Snackpass, Peerspace, Shef, Good Dog, and Udemy

claims.text: There are six effective ways to build trust in a new marketplace: reviews, verifying supply, leaning on social proof, creating a perception of quality, providing a safety net, and delivering magic.
claims.evidence: Research across Lyft, Thumbtack, Rover, Snackpass, Peerspace, Shef, Good Dog, and Udemy

claims.text: High-quality photos are instrumental for building trust with customers in marketplaces, and Airbnb's professional photography program along with host profile photos contributed to immediate trust for new visitors.
claims.evidence: Joe Gebbia confirmed design-for-trust mantra at Airbnb; Shef co-founder reported high-quality food photos instrumental for conversion; academic study showed host photos significantly impact guest decision-making

claims.text: Udemy purposely had instructors set prices very high at $199 then discounted to $10 because high prices signal quality and trust to students while the low actual price drives conversion.
claims.evidence: Archie Abrams, early growth leader at Udemy, describing their pricing strategy

claims.text: The magic moment for marketplace trust is when both sides experience something better than expected: for Thumbtack pros it was getting hired and paid for their first job, and for consumers it was finding a pro better than through word of mouth.
claims.evidence: Sander Daniels, co-founder of Thumbtack, describing their focus on liquidity to accelerate trust-building

newsletters.title: Product management career ladders
newsletters.subtitle: Examples, templates, titles, and attributes from 20+ companies, including Facebook, Google, DoorDash, Airbnb, Instacart, Lyft, Uber, and many more

claims.text: Two-thirds of companies have both an IC track and a manager track for product managers, with the split typically occurring at the Senior Product Manager level (L6).
claims.evidence: Analysis of career ladders from 20+ companies including Facebook, Google, DoorDash, Airbnb, Instacart, Lyft, and Uber

claims.text: The most common PM title sequence is APM to PM to Senior PM to Principal PM on the IC track, and GPM to Director to Senior Director to VP on the management track.
claims.evidence: Data from 20+ companies aggregated into a public spreadsheet

claims.text: The most common attributes evaluated in PM career ladders are leadership, impact, scope, execution, communication, vision, strategy, collaboration, planning, and technical skills in that order.
claims.evidence: Analysis across 20+ company career ladders

newsletters.title: How to win in consumer subscription
newsletters.subtitle: Learnings from Noom, Duolingo, Grammarly, Calm, Spotify, Future, Flo, and others

claims.text: The three patterns of winning B2C subscription businesses are an obsession with efficiency, alignment between product strategy and acquisition strategy, and a singular focus on building a magical sticky product through rapid iteration.
claims.evidence: Research into Noom, Duolingo, Grammarly, Calm, Spotify, Future, and Flo

claims.text: Duolingo built a massive user base through word of mouth and no paid marketing by offering all learning content for free, because limiting free experience heavily slows organic growth since only payers can tell friends.
claims.evidence: Cem Kansu, VP of Product at Duolingo, explaining their free-first business model creating an organic growth engine

claims.text: Noom targeted one-month payback on paid acquisition which allowed immediate reinvestment into their performance marketing engine, making them less reliant on raising money to grow.
