moments.summary: AI models have outgrown generalist data labelers and now need domain experts.

moments.speaker: Garrett Lord
moments.text: Model builders care about three things: quality first, then volume, then speed.
moments.summary: AI labs prioritize quality above all else, then volume in expert domains, then speed.

moments.speaker: Garrett Lord
moments.text: We started to see direct outreach from the Frontier Labs trying to cut out the middleman. Five months later we were working with arguably the number one lab.
moments.summary: Handshake went from exploration to working with the top lab in five months by going direct.

moments.speaker: Garrett Lord
moments.text: Separate engineering team, separate design team, separate finance team. People only had one job — making Handshake AI successful.
moments.summary: Handshake succeeded by making their AI business fully separate with dedicated teams and founder-mode intensity.

moments.speaker: Garrett Lord
moments.text: We had this motto: leave nothing to chance. There will never be a time like this. I've never seen anything like it.
moments.summary: In moments of unlimited demand, maximize execution intensity because these windows close fast.

moments.speaker: Garrett Lord
moments.text: That's not what we're hearing from employers. Being AI native, young people are at a huge advantage.
moments.summary: Being AI-native is the new generational advantage, like growing up with Google Search.

podcasts.title: How Intercom rose from the ashes by betting everything on AI | Eoghan McCabe (founder and CEO)
podcasts.description: How Intercom rose from the ashes by betting everything on AI, covering AI product work, pricing decisions, and product design.

moments.speaker: Eoghan McCabe
moments.text: We were about to hit $0 net new ARR, which means we would've been in negative growth territory.
moments.summary: Intercom was on the verge of negative growth before their AI pivot.

moments.speaker: Eoghan McCabe
moments.text: I said, we need to become a wartime company. I rewrote the values designed to be a sharp knife to cut out the parts of the company that wouldn't be effective.
moments.summary: McCabe declared wartime mode and rewrote company values as a tool to remove ineffective parts of the organization.

moments.speaker: Eoghan McCabe
moments.text: Fin is our AI agent who will pass 100 million ARR in less than three quarters.
moments.summary: Intercom's AI agent Fin is on track to exceed $100M ARR in under three quarters.

moments.speaker: Eoghan McCabe
moments.text: People abhorred our pricing. It was a meme. Part of the problem was our strategy, super unfocused.
moments.summary: Unfocused product strategy directly causes complex, hated pricing.

moments.speaker: Eoghan McCabe
moments.text: You don't have a choice. AI is going to disrupt in the most aggressive violent ways. If you're not in it, you're about to get kicked out.
moments.summary: AI disruption is not optional — companies that don't embrace it will be displaced.

podcasts.title: The one question that saves product careers | Matt LeMay
podcasts.description: The one question that saves product careers, covering team leadership, product design, and product strategy and execution.

moments.speaker: Matt LeMay
moments.text: The PM role is evolving faster than any other role in tech. The PMs who survive are the ones who keep redefining their own job description every six months.
moments.summary: PM role evolves faster than any tech role — survival requires redefining your job description every 6 months.

moments.speaker: Matt LeMay
moments.text: The best question a PM can ask is 'What would have to be true for this to work?' It saves you from building the wrong thing and from political landmines.
moments.summary: The most powerful PM question: 'What would have to be true for this to work?' — prevents wrong builds and political issues.

podcasts.title: Inside ChatGPT: The fastest-growing product in history | Nick Turley (Head of ChatGPT at OpenAI)
podcasts.description: The fastest-growing product in history, covering AI product work, product design, and go-to-market execution.

moments.speaker: Nick Turley
moments.text: This is a pattern with AI, you won't know what to polish until after you ship. My dream is that we ship daily.
moments.summary: In AI products, you cannot know what to polish before shipping — speed beats craft early on.

moments.speaker: Nick Turley
moments.text: If we're shipping a feature and it doesn't get 2X better as the model gets 2X smarter, it's probably not a feature we should be shipping.
moments.summary: Features should scale with model intelligence, otherwise they may not be worth building.

moments.speaker: Nick Turley
moments.text: I started writing evals before I knew what an eval was because I was just outlining clearly specified ideal behavior.
moments.summary: Evals are simply articulating success criteria and bridge the gap between product and research teams.

moments.speaker: Nick Turley
moments.text: You're going to be polishing the wrong things in this space. You won't know what to polish until after you ship.
moments.summary: Traditional product craft can be counterproductive in AI because emergent properties mean you'll polish the wrong things.

moments.speaker: Nick Turley
moments.text: It is a small team running ChatGPT. I take inspiration from WhatsApp. You have to treat hiring like executive recruiting.
moments.summary: ChatGPT runs on a small team using executive-style recruiting to fill specific skill gaps.

moments.speaker: Nick Turley
moments.text: We would have immense regret if you had a model that was state-of-the-art on health bench and didn't use that to help people.
moments.summary: OpenAI chose to lean into high-stakes use cases rather than disabling them.

moments.speaker: Nick Turley
moments.text: Curiosity is an attribute that we think matters so much more than your ML knowledge.
moments.summary: OpenAI values curiosity over ML experience when hiring.

podcasts.title: Brian Chesky's secret mentor who died 9 times, started the Burning Man board, and built the world's first midlife wisdom school | Chip Conley (founder of MEA)
podcasts.description: Brian Chesky's secret mentor who died 9 times, started the Burning Man board, and built the world's first midlife wisdom school, covering team leadership, product design, and startup building.

moments.speaker: Chip Conley
moments.text: I was mentoring Brian, but he was also my boss. I was 52, the average age was 26. I had to be both wise and curious.
moments.summary: Conley describes the paradox of being a senior mentor reporting to a younger CEO.

moments.speaker: Chip Conley
moments.text: If what AI cannot do is the human wisdom piece, is it possible that older managers with more emotional intelligence can be a value?
moments.summary: AI may make human wisdom and emotional intelligence — often found in older workers — more valuable.

moments.speaker: Chip Conley
moments.text: Show up with curiosity and a passionate engagement. People won't notice your wrinkles as much as they'll notice your energy.
moments.summary: The key to thriving as an older person in tech is showing up with curiosity and energy.

moments.speaker: Chip Conley
moments.text: We are moving from the era of specialists to generalists. AI is accelerating this.
moments.summary: AI is accelerating the shift from specialists to generalists.

moments.speaker: Chip Conley
moments.text: The employee model is simple: compensation at the base, recognition in the middle, meaning at the top.
moments.summary: Conley's Maslow-inspired employee pyramid: compensation, recognition, meaning.

moments.speaker: Chip Conley
moments.text: Culture is what happens around here when the boss is not around. The more distributed, the more culture is important.
moments.summary: Culture matters most in distributed companies because it guides decisions when leadership is absent.

podcasts.title: He saved OpenAI, invented the “Like” button, and built Google Maps: Bret Taylor on the future of careers, coding, agents, and more
podcasts.description: The future of careers, coding, agents, and more, covering product design, team leadership, and product strategy and execution.

moments.speaker: Bret Taylor
moments.text: I think the PM of the future looks more like a product engineer. Someone who can think strategically but also build and test ideas rapidly. The line between PM and engineer is blurring.
moments.summary: Future PMs will be product engineers — strategic thinkers who also build and test rapidly as the PM/engineer boundary blurs.

podcasts.title: Pricing your AI product: Lessons from 400+ companies and 50 unicorns | Madhavan Ramanujam
podcasts.description: Lessons from 400+ companies and 50 unicorns, covering pricing decisions, strategic decision-making, and product design.

moments.speaker: Madhavan Ramanujam
moments.text: When we talk about pricing, many people gravitate to dollar figures. But price is a measure of value.
moments.summary: Price should be understood as a measure of value, and willingness-to-pay conversations should happen earlier.

moments.speaker: Madhavan Ramanujam
moments.text: They moved the price from 79 to 99, and built a decoy at 299. It was a 30+ percent increase in MRR.
moments.summary: Using a decoy product at a higher price tier drove 30%+ MRR increase with zero product changes.

moments.speaker: Madhavan Ramanujam
moments.text: The Panini effect — show products as a puzzle and 40 to 50% of people start taking more products because there's a compulsion to finish.
moments.summary: The Panini effect leverages collection-completion compulsion, driving 40-50% higher attach rates.

moments.speaker: Madhavan Ramanujam
moments.text: Don't give the farm away in your entry level product. Use the compromise effect so people avoid the extremes.
moments.summary: Reserve features for higher tiers and use the compromise effect in packaging.

moments.speaker: Madhavan Ramanujam
moments.text: Before you discount, think about what value can you exchange. Use non-pricing levers: give more product, change contract terms, change payment terms.
moments.summary: Never just cut price — use non-pricing levers like giving more product or adjusting terms.

moments.speaker: Madhavan Ramanujam
moments.text: 90% of customers who claim to have a land and expand strategy are only landing. They're not expanding because they gave their farm away.
moments.summary: Most companies fail at land-and-expand because they give away too much value in the initial land.

podcasts.title: Benjamin Mann
podcasts.description: Ultimately, people think about this as probably going to hit a wall because if the model isn't good enough to see its own mistakes, then how could it improve, covering AI product work, product design, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Benjamin Mann
moments.text: I think 50th percentile chance of hitting some kind of superintelligence is now like 2028.
moments.summary: Anthropic co-founder estimates 50% chance of superintelligence by 2028.

moments.speaker: Benjamin Mann
moments.text: We felt like safety wasn't the top priority there. The case for safety has gotten a lot more concrete.
moments.summary: Mann left OpenAI because safety was not the top priority.

moments.speaker: Benjamin Mann
moments.text: Once we get to superintelligence, it will be too late to align the models. My best forecast for an extremely bad outcome is 0-10%.
moments.summary: AI alignment must be solved before superintelligence arrives.

moments.speaker: Benjamin Mann
moments.text: In a world of abundance where labor is almost free, what do jobs even look like?
moments.summary: Post-singularity economics will look nothing like today.

moments.speaker: Benjamin Mann
moments.text: The talent wars are real. The expected value of a top researcher is just astronomical.
moments.summary: Massive AI researcher compensation reflects the astronomical expected value of a single brilliant researcher.

podcasts.title: The AI-native startup: 5 products, 7-figure revenue, 100% AI-written code | Dan Shipper (co-founder/CEO of Every)
podcasts.description: 5 products, 7-figure revenue, 100% AI-written code, covering AI product work, engineering tradeoffs, and product design.

moments.speaker: Dan Shipper
moments.text: 100% of our code is AI-written. Five products, seven-figure revenue. The quality bar has crossed the threshold where AI code is production-ready for most use cases.
moments.summary: Every (Dan's company) runs 5 products on 100% AI-written code with 7-figure revenue, proving production viability.

moments.speaker: Dan Shipper
moments.text: The gap between demo and production is real. AI can write code fast, but making it reliable requires iteration. We spend about 30% of our time on polish and reliability.
moments.summary: Demo-to-production gap is real — 30% of time goes to polish and reliability even with AI-written code.

podcasts.title: I’ve run 75+ businesses. Here’s why you’re probably chasing the wrong idea. | Andrew Wilkinson (co‑founder of Tiny)
podcasts.description: I’ve run 75+ businesses. Here’s why you’re probably chasing the wrong idea., covering product design, startup building, and AI product work.

moments.speaker: Andrew Wilkinson
moments.text: You don't want to walk into the gym on day one and try and deadlift 300 pounds.
moments.summary: First-time founders should start with simpler businesses rather than trying to build the next great AI company.

moments.speaker: Andrew Wilkinson
moments.text: The biggest mistakes I've made have been going into business models where other people have repeatedly failed.
moments.summary: Entrepreneurs should avoid business models with high failure rates.

moments.speaker: Andrew Wilkinson
moments.text: It's like having the world's most reliable employee who costs $200 a month. Do all jobs just become a single prompt?
moments.summary: AI is the most reliable and cheapest employee — the key question is whether knowledge work reduces to a single prompt.

moments.speaker: Andrew Wilkinson
moments.text: Charlie Munger says, 'Fish where the fish are.' Find a small fishing hole with lots of fish and very little competition.
moments.summary: The best business opportunities are in unsexy niches with low competition.

moments.speaker: Andrew Wilkinson
moments.text: In order to know what problems are valuable to solve, you kind of need to have valuable problems.
moments.summary: Understanding how much people will actually pay to solve a problem requires real-world business experience.

moments.speaker: Andrew Wilkinson
moments.text: What you want is problems where the pain level is really high and the existing solutions are really bad.
moments.summary: The best startup ideas target problems with high pain levels and poor existing solutions.

podcasts.title: Naming expert shares the process behind creating billion-dollar brand names like Azure, Vercel, Windsurf, Sonos, Blackberry, and Impossible Burger | David Placek (Lexicon Branding)
podcasts.description: Naming expert shares the process behind creating billion-dollar brand names like Azure, Vercel, Windsurf, Sonos, Blackberry, and Impossible Burger, covering strategic decision-making, team leadership, and product design.

moments.speaker: David Placek
moments.text: Your brand name, nothing's going to be used more often or for longer than that name. Design will change, messaging will change, products will change, but that name is there.
moments.summary: A brand name outlasts every other element of a company and is the most enduring asset.

moments.speaker: David Placek
moments.text: When we presented Sonos, it was rejected because it's not entertainment-like. We argued about that because I said, 'This is outside looking in, but I don't see you as an entertainment company.' Humans do like to be comfortable. Part of our job here is to help people to give the confidence going bigger and being uncomfortable.
moments.summary: Great names are initially uncomfortable; the naming expert's job is helping clients push past comfort to find bold names.

moments.speaker: David Placek
moments.text: Just draw a shape of a diamond on a piece of paper. On the top put the word win. How do you define winning? On the next corner, what do you have to win? On the bottom, what do you need to win? And then on the left-hand side, what do you have to say to win?
moments.summary: Placek's diamond framework helps founders clarify their naming strategy by defining winning, assets, needs, and messaging.

moments.speaker: David Placek
moments.text: Andy Grove said, 'Because I see the polarization here amongst people. That tells me there's energy for Pentium here.' And he said, 'That's why I think we should go with it.' We do look for that polarization. That's what you want. You don't want to go out in the marketplace with something that doesn't have a level of boldness or intensity.
moments.summary: Internal polarization over a name is a sign of strength, as Andy Grove recognized when choosing the Pentium name.

moments.speaker: David Placek
moments.text: Forget about the word, think about behavior and experience. I'm a big believer in synchronicity. If someone says we make sailboats, I would say forget about sailboats. Go pick out some magazines about hunting or flying. I would bet you $5 that out of those two magazines, you will get a word that you never would've thought of, but somehow it would relate to sailing.
moments.summary: The best names come from cross-domain inspiration rather than literal descriptions of your product.

moments.speaker: David Placek
moments.text: The .com or URL address has become an area code. And whether you're in 415 or 615, it doesn't really matter to people. And now with AI, SEO is going to be less important. The principle in play is let's get the right name first.
moments.summary: Domain names are far less important than they used to be; focus on finding the right name first.

podcasts.title: From ChatGPT to Instagram to Uber: The quiet architect behind the world’s most popular products | Peter Deng
podcasts.description: The quiet architect behind the world’s most popular products, covering AI product work, product design, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Peter Deng
moments.text: Sometimes your product actually doesn't matter. At Uber, I learned this because, really, the price and the ETA at Uber was the product. Looking at it from a holistic perspective, we humans consume the entirety of the product. It's not to say that you shouldn't fix the bug, but it doesn't have as much of an impact as something that is more important to people.
moments.summary: At Uber, the pixels on screen mattered far less than price and ETA, teaching that the holistic experience is the real product.

moments.speaker: Peter Deng
moments.text: AGI is just necessary but not sufficient. A lot of the value is still going to require a bunch of hustle from a lot of builders to really turn that new source of energy and channel it into something that we humans want to use that solves some of our problems.
moments.summary: AGI alone won't solve everything; builders still need to do the hard work of channeling AI into useful products.

moments.speaker: Peter Deng
moments.text: Language actually affects the way you think. Once I heard that and read that, I couldn't stop thinking about it because it just rang so true. When I make slide decks, there were probably a total of 20 words on the entire slide deck. And I spent hours obsessing over them because if you're not intentional about the words you use, those have downstream effects.
moments.summary: Language shapes thought, so product leaders must obsess over every word in vision docs and presentations.

moments.speaker: Peter Deng
moments.text: When you go from one to 100, you have to plan your chess moves out in advance and build systems that are going to let you go sustainably faster. Sometimes you have to go slow to go fast. I like to think that Newsfeed has stood the test of time because we thought very carefully about how people wanted to interact.
moments.summary: In the scaling phase, investing in systems thinking and architecture pays off far more than moving fast and breaking things.

moments.speaker: Peter Deng
moments.text: One of the first things I did was always to build a growth team. When you build a growth team and you hire the right growth leader, they start asking all the right questions because the archetype of person who is a growth PM will be like, 'Well, wait. Why is this happening? Let's get the data.' That's when you realize you don't have X, Y, and Z thing logged.
moments.summary: Building a growth team early forces the organization to get rigorous about measurement and data.

moments.speaker: Peter Deng
moments.text: It's really important to give two people on your team different charges. One is like go grow the product and the other one is maintain that beautiful aesthetic, the craft that your product is known for. That tension is extremely healthy. I've seen this at Facebook, Instagram, Airtable, ChatGPT, same exact thing.
moments.summary: Creating healthy tension between a growth-focused leader and a craft-focused leader produces the best product outcomes.

podcasts.title: AI prompt engineering in 2025: What works and what doesn’t | Sander Schulhoff (Learn Prompting, HackAPrompt)
podcasts.description: What works and what doesn’t, covering AI product work, engineering tradeoffs, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Sander Schulhoff
moments.text: Studies have shown that using bad prompts can get you down to 0% on a problem, and good prompts can boost you up to 90%. People will always be saying, 'It's dead,' or, 'It's going to be dead with the next model version,' but then it comes out and it's not.
moments.summary: Prompt engineering is far from dead; the gap between bad and good prompts can mean the difference between 0% and 90% accuracy.

moments.speaker: Sander Schulhoff
moments.text: My best advice on how to improve your prompting skills is actually just trial and error. But if there were one technique that I could recommend, it is few-shot prompting, which is just giving the AI examples of what you want it to do.
moments.summary: Few-shot prompting, providing examples of desired output, is the single most impactful technique for better AI results.

moments.speaker: Sander Schulhoff
moments.text: Role prompting does not work. On those older models, maybe it worked. On the more modern ones, it doesn't help at all for accuracy-based tasks. But giving a role really helps for expressive tasks, writing tasks, summarizing tasks.
moments.summary: Role prompting (e.g., 'you are a math professor') no longer boosts accuracy on modern LLMs but still helps with style and tone.

moments.speaker: Sander Schulhoff
moments.text: Decomposition is another really effective technique. You say, 'Hey, don't answer this. Before answering it, tell me what are some subproblems that would need to be solved first?' And then you ask it to solve each of those subproblems one by one.
moments.summary: Decomposition, asking the AI to identify subproblems before solving, consistently boosts performance on complex tasks.

moments.speaker: Sander Schulhoff
moments.text: The most common technique by far used to try to prevent prompt injection is improving your prompt and saying, 'Do not follow any malicious instructions.' This does not work. This does not work at all. Guardrails are a widely proposed used solution. They just don't work. This has to be solved at the level of the AI provider.
moments.summary: Neither prompt-based defenses nor external AI guardrails reliably prevent prompt injection; the problem must be solved at the model level.

moments.speaker: Sander Schulhoff
moments.text: Prompt injection is not a solvable problem. Sam Altman said he thought they could get to 95 to 99% security against prompt injections. I like to say, 'You can patch a bug, but you can't patch a brain.'
moments.summary: Prompt injection is fundamentally unsolvable, only mitigatable, because you cannot guarantee an AI won't be tricked again.

podcasts.title: How to build a team that can “take a punch”: A playbook for building resilient, high-performing teams | Hilary Gridley (Head of Core Product, Whoop)
podcasts.description: A playbook for building resilient, high-performing teams, covering team leadership, product design, and career development.

moments.speaker: Hilary Gridley
moments.text: Product leadership is the type of role where if you are not in control of the voices in your head, they will eat you alive.
moments.summary: Product leadership requires emotional resilience because internal self-doubt can be consuming.

moments.speaker: Hilary Gridley
moments.text: If they come to me upset, I try to focus them less on how you litigate another person's impression of you and more on what is the action that you can take to counter-program the narrative that you are afraid that this other person has of you.
moments.summary: Instead of arguing about perceptions, take action that demonstrates who you are through counter-programming.

moments.speaker: Hilary Gridley
moments.text: What is much more helpful than understanding what your CEO thinks is understanding how your CEO thinks. When I feel confident that people on my team understand how I think, I don't need to read their emails, I don't need to approve things.
moments.summary: Teaching your team how leaders think, not just what they think, eliminates bottlenecks and empowers autonomous decision-making.

moments.speaker: Hilary Gridley
moments.text: I talk about what I call the magic questions, but the thing about magic questions is they're not actually questions, they're statements and they end with, 'Do you agree?' or 'Is that right?' You're teasing out the mental model rather than asking them to explain it to you.
moments.summary: Use declarative statements ending with 'is that right?' to extract mental models from leaders and calibrate your own judgment.

moments.speaker: Hilary Gridley
moments.text: You can be the protagonist in the story of your family, but in the story of your company, you are probably not the protagonist. Some of the best advice I've gotten in my life is: you're not special. Your job is to understand what the CEO's vision is and figure out how to operationalize that.
moments.summary: As a product leader, your job is not to impose your vision but to operationalize the CEO's vision as effectively as possible.

moments.speaker: Hilary Gridley
moments.text: I have a 30 days of GPT list of 30 things to do, one every single day. I don't know anyone who has gone through this and not come out the other side feeling a hundred times more confident in their skills. It's built as a habit formation tool, not an education tool.
moments.summary: Driving AI adoption on teams works better as a daily habit-building exercise than as a training program.

podcasts.title: 35 years of product design wisdom from Apple, Disney, Pinterest, and beyond | Bob Baxley
podcasts.description: 35 years of product design wisdom from Apple, Disney, Pinterest, and beyond, covering product design, team leadership, and startup building.

moments.speaker: Bob Baxley
moments.text: Design is trying to imagine the future you want to live in and then take the steps to make it real. Saying a company is design-led does not mean it's designer-led. I've never seen somebody graft it on after the fact. It's there at the beginning in the root DNA or it doesn't exist.
moments.summary: Design-led culture must be baked into a company's founding DNA; it cannot be grafted on later.

moments.speaker: Bob Baxley
moments.text: I went to Pinterest and did not have a successful time. I came in thinking I was supposed to behave the way I behaved at Apple, which is very direct, fighting hard. I didn't give myself time to recalibrate to the Pinterest culture.
moments.summary: Transitioning between strong company cultures requires a recalibration period; jumping straight in can lead to failure.

moments.speaker: Bob Baxley
moments.text: I've very explicitly been using AI as a life coach. One prompt was, 'What's an outdated mindset that I'm holding onto that's not still serving me?' It came back with a very polite response about being wedded to the idea of control. It suggested: try to focus on choreography over control.
moments.summary: Using ChatGPT as a life coach surfaced patterns from his subconscious, reflecting back insights his conscious mind hadn't articulated.

moments.speaker: Bob Baxley
moments.text: John Hobolt risked his whole career sending a memo to champion Lunar Orbit Rendezvous when no one at NASA believed in it. Ideas need champions. They need champions willing to put themselves on the line for them. Have the courage of your convictions and fight as hard as you can.
moments.summary: The Apollo story of John Hobolt shows that great ideas need passionate champions willing to risk their careers.

moments.speaker: Bob Baxley
moments.text: When you show up with an iPhone, you're thinking about sharing. When you show up with a film camera, you're thinking about saving film. When you show up with a digital SLR, you just take a whole bunch of pictures. Be conscious about how the tools you pick are going to impact the thing that you produce.
moments.summary: Different tools shape different behaviors and outputs; choosing Figma vs. a sketchbook is itself a design decision.

moments.speaker: Bob Baxley
moments.text: There's nothing worse than a brilliant image of a fuzzy concept. We live in a time when it's very easy to produce things at incredibly high production values, but they don't mean anything. They're just fancy potato chips. There's no nourishment there.
moments.summary: High-fidelity outputs without clear concepts are empty; vibe coding and AI prototypes can mask the absence of real thinking.

podcasts.title: Anthropic’s CPO on what comes next | Mike Krieger (co-founder of Instagram)
podcasts.description: Anthropic’s CPO on what comes next, covering AI product work, product design, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Mike Krieger
moments.text: The team that works in the most futuristic way is the Claude Code team. They're using Claude Code to build Claude Code in a very self-improving kind of way. Over half of our pull requests are Claude Code generated. Probably at this point it's over 70%.
moments.summary: At Anthropic, over 70% of pull requests are AI-generated, with the Claude Code team building itself in a self-improving loop.

moments.speaker: Mike Krieger
moments.text: The biggest role shift is prototyping happening earlier in the process. PMs and designers that have an idea will use Claude and maybe even Artifacts to put together an actual functional demo. That has been very, very helpful.
moments.summary: AI is shifting prototyping earlier in the product process, with PMs and designers building functional demos before engineering begins.

moments.speaker: Mike Krieger
moments.text: Instead, look yourself in the mirror and embrace who you are and what you could be rather than who others are. We have a super strong developer brand. Can we lean into the fact that builders love using Claude? Those builders aren't all just engineers.
moments.summary: As CPO of Anthropic, Krieger chose to lean into the builder brand identity rather than chase ChatGPT's consumer dominance.

moments.speaker: Mike Krieger
moments.text: For utility of AI products, it's three parts: model intelligence, context and memory, and applications and UI. You need all three to converge. MCP tried to tackle the middle one. The difference between the right context and not is entirely the difference between a good answer and a bad answer.
moments.summary: AI product utility requires convergence of model intelligence, context/memory (which MCP solves), and application UI.

moments.speaker: Mike Krieger
moments.text: Being willing to build at the edge of capabilities and basically break the model and then be surprised by the next model. Those are the companies where I'm like, 'Yep, they get it.' They were trying it beforehand and then hitting a wall and being like, the models are almost good enough.
moments.summary: The best AI startups build at the frontier of model capabilities, pushing until they break, then benefit when the next model arrives.

moments.speaker: Mike Krieger
moments.text: I think you know when your product is really serving people. So much of when you get really metrics obsessed is when you're trying to convince yourself that it is when it's not. That's our north star: do we repeatedly hear from people that Claude is unlocking their own creativity?
moments.summary: Over-optimizing metrics often signals self-deception; the real measure is whether people say the product genuinely unlocks their creativity.

podcasts.title: Growth tactics from OpenAI and Stripe’s first marketer | Krithika Shankarraman
podcasts.description: Growth tactics from OpenAI and Stripe’s first marketer, covering strategic decision-making, AI product work, and B2B products.

moments.speaker: Krithika Shankarraman
moments.text: Everyone knew of ChatGPT, but when you clicked one zoom level further, the thing that came up was, 'I don't know what to use it for.' The work of marketing ended up becoming creating this use case epiphany where people could say, 'I had no idea ChatGPT can do that.'
moments.summary: As OpenAI's first marketer, the core challenge was not awareness but creating 'use case epiphanies' that showed people what ChatGPT could actually do.

moments.speaker: Krithika Shankarraman
moments.text: A lot of marketing metrics tend to be vanity metrics about the number of clicks, views, and impressions. I think those are all bullshit numbers. What is that experience that you want your customers to come away with when they interact with your brand?
moments.summary: Traditional marketing metrics like clicks and impressions are vanity metrics; focus on the customer experience instead.

moments.speaker: Krithika Shankarraman
moments.text: Capital-M Marketing, the marketing team, is responsible for channels and artifacts driving the funnel. But lowercase-m marketing is what do you stand for as a company? It's a whole company motion where the product team, the sales team, and everyone joins to make that happen.
moments.summary: Capital-M Marketing is the team function; lowercase-m marketing is company-wide storytelling and positioning that everyone owns.

moments.speaker: Krithika Shankarraman
moments.text: The conventional wisdom for many CMOs is to be a T-shaped marketer. I think this chameleon CMO concept means modern marketing leaders have to be really good at a bunch of different things. Maybe going from T-shaped to comb-shaped is probably the right approach.
moments.summary: Modern CMOs need to be comb-shaped, going deep in multiple marketing disciplines, rather than the traditional T-shaped specialist.

moments.speaker: Krithika Shankarraman
moments.text: Taste is going to become a distinguishing factor in the age of AI because there's going to be so much drivel that is generated by AI. The companies that are going to distinguish themselves are the ones that show their craft and their true understanding of their product and customer.
moments.summary: As AI floods the market with mediocre content, taste and craft will become the ultimate differentiator for companies.

moments.speaker: Krithika Shankarraman
moments.text: We launched Stripe Relay back in 2014 for social commerce buy buttons. It launched to a lot of fanfare, but eventually failed. The learning was we hadn't gone deep enough into the market dynamics. We hadn't done enough user research on whether people really wanted this.
moments.summary: Stripe Relay failed despite excitement because the team didn't validate market readiness or user demand deeply enough.

podcasts.title: Unconventional product lessons from Binance, N26, Google, more | Mayur Kamat (CPO at N26, ex-Binance Head of Product)
podcasts.description: Unconventional product lessons from Binance, N26, Google, more, covering team leadership, product strategy and execution, and AI product work.

moments.speaker: Mayur Kamat
moments.text: Strategy is a little bit overrated for product. For most product managers, your strategy should be, 'How fast can I go from hypothesis to data?'
moments.summary: For most PMs, strategy is overrated; the real strategy is minimizing the time from hypothesis to validated data.

moments.speaker: Mayur Kamat
moments.text: My most spectacular failure was being the first PM on Hangouts. We had thousands of people, the entire power of Google, Larry literally sitting with us saying we can do anything we want Chrome to do, and we still didn't manage to build a great messaging product.
moments.summary: Google Hangouts failed despite unlimited resources, teaching that company DNA can work against you in certain product categories.

moments.speaker: Mayur Kamat
moments.text: At Binance, we had this ability to just put 500 people in and solve the problem. It's less about having the 500 people and being able to maneuver them that quickly. I don't think I've been able to do that at any other company.
moments.summary: Binance's extraordinary speed came not from headcount but from the ability to rapidly redeploy hundreds of people to solve urgent problems.

moments.speaker: Mayur Kamat
moments.text: Alex Allgood had a floating desk. He would move his desk to the department which had the highest leverage opportunity. He would sit there until that problem was solved. You could literally visualize him working on the highest leverage problem by his desk moving.
moments.summary: The CEO of White Pages physically moved his desk to whichever department had the highest leverage problem, embodying founder mode.

moments.speaker: Mayur Kamat
moments.text: A full calendar is a badge of shame, not a badge of honor. If you have hundreds of meetings, hundreds of one-on-ones, daily standups, a lot of recurring meetings, you just can't find time to go work on high leverage problems.
moments.summary: Keeping your calendar deliberately free is essential to working on the highest leverage problems.

moments.speaker: Mayur Kamat
moments.text: There's no right or wrong decisions. There's just slow and fast decisions. If you make a wrong decision fast enough, you would know it was wrong and you would correct it faster than thinking months for the right decision.
moments.summary: For reversible decisions, speed matters more than correctness because fast failures provide data faster than slow deliberation.

podcasts.title: Microsoft CPO: If you aren’t prototyping with AI, you’re doing it wrong | Aparna Chennapragada
podcasts.description: If you aren’t prototyping with AI, you’re doing it wrong, covering AI product work, product design, and B2B products.

moments.speaker: Aparna Chennapragada
moments.text: If you're not prototyping and building to see what you want to build, I think you're doing it wrong. It becomes even more important to have taste-making at the heart of it because otherwise you just have a Frankenstein product.
moments.summary: In the AI era, prototyping is mandatory to discover what to build, but taste must guide the process or the result is a Frankenstein.

moments.speaker: Aparna Chennapragada
moments.text: NLX is the new UX. Conversations also have grammars. They have structures. They have UI elements. They're invisible. What are the new principles, new constructs in natural language as an interface?
moments.summary: Natural language interfaces (NLX) are the new UX frontier, with their own invisible grammars and design principles to discover.

moments.speaker: Aparna Chennapragada
moments.text: Three inflection points for great products: technology shift, consumer behavior shift, and business model shift. At least two out of three should be true for any good product. With AI, the monetization is a whole different story -- we've just barely scratched the surface.
moments.summary: Great products require at least two of three inflection points: new technology, shifted behavior, and new business models.

moments.speaker: Aparna Chennapragada
moments.text: Excel is proof that non-coders also have to program. Programming is really powerful and it's the tool that gives all of the non-coders a really powerful programming ability. The learning curve initially might be tricky, but it is because there's so much power and depth in the tool.
moments.summary: Excel's endurance proves that non-coders need programming power, and depth of capability outweighs initial learning friction.

moments.speaker: Aparna Chennapragada
moments.text: I was working on this idea that should just work and it didn't. I tried to make personalization work in Google Search. Then I started working on Google Now, which was a twist: on the phone, we should push content. That was a pivotal moment. Being early is the same as being wrong.
moments.summary: Google Now was a pivotal career moment that taught Chennapragada that being early with a product idea is often the same as being wrong.

moments.speaker: Aparna Chennapragada
moments.text: We had the opposite problem with voice assistants. We overshot the interface and the intelligence wasn't there. Today, these things have amazing intelligence and the interface we have largely is like the AOL Dial-Up Modem Chatbot.
moments.summary: Voice assistants had great UI but weak AI; today's AI chatbots have the reverse problem of amazing intelligence trapped in primitive interfaces.

podcasts.title: How Revolut trains world-class product managers: The “local CEO” model, raw intellect over experience, and a cultural obsession with building wow products | Dmitry Zlokazov (Head of Product)
podcasts.description: Dmitry Zlokazov is the head of product at Revolut, the $45 billion fintech giant operating in over 50 countries, serving more than 50 million customers, and producing some of the world’s top product leaders.

moments.speaker: Dmitry Zlokazov
moments.text: Everyone is striving for talented, skillful, smart people. Revolut values way more raw intellect and this unquenched hunger to build things rather than experience.
moments.summary: Revolut prioritizes raw intellect and hunger to build over years of experience when hiring product people.

moments.speaker: Dmitry Zlokazov
moments.text: It's not getting traction. Is it because the underlying idea is wrong? Or maybe your product just sucks? By forcing everyone to build a product that people will love, we kind of cut out this part of uncertainty. We will never compromise on the quality and UX and the aesthetics.
moments.summary: Revolut eliminates the 'is the idea wrong or does the product suck?' ambiguity by never compromising on quality and UX.

moments.speaker: Dmitry Zlokazov
moments.text: If something is 99% done, it's closer to 0% rather than 100%.
moments.summary: A product that's 99% complete but unshipped is closer to zero value because the last mile of launch, marketing, and adoption matters most.

moments.speaker: Dmitry Zlokazov
moments.text: I would add an obsession with building wow product. The product owner is set to this objective that I need to build a product that would make people say wow. Practically, the way we do it is we align a product vision with a reference product.
moments.summary: Revolut's product owners are charged with building 'wow' products, benchmarked against the best reference products in any industry.

moments.speaker: Dmitry Zlokazov
moments.text: We started building a website that allows people to buy cinema tickets online. We built end-to-end hardware, stainless steel scanners, hacked the SMS standard to send QR codes to Nokia phones. We spent all our investment on hardware without any proven business model. We were just enjoying building a product we would love to use.
moments.summary: A student startup that built beautiful cinema ticket hardware failed because they spent everything on hardware before validating the business model.

moments.speaker: Dmitry Zlokazov
moments.text: If you're excited to build certain things, never hesitate to do it. The best way is probably your own startup, which will give you the steepest possible learning curve, but if you want to join a company, try to choose the one that has the highest entrepreneurial spirit and will allow you to work as close to a mode of a founder as you possibly can.
moments.summary: For the steepest career growth, either start your own company or join one where you can operate like a founder.

podcasts.title: How Palantir built the ultimate founder factory | Nabeel S. Qureshi (founder, writer, ex-Palantir)
podcasts.description: How Palantir built the ultimate founder factory, covering product design, startup building, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Nabeel S. Qureshi
moments.text: They were extremely careful about only making people PMs who had first proven themselves out as forward deployed engineers. You basically could not become a PM any other way.
moments.summary: Palantir only promoted forward deployed engineers into PM roles, never hiring external PMs, ensuring deep customer empathy.

moments.speaker: Nabeel S. Qureshi
moments.text: When you started a new project, you basically had to organize what they called a murder board. You write up a two-page plan for the project. You invite three or four smart folks who don't know anything about the project and their job is just to tear apart your plan.
moments.summary: Palantir used 'murder boards' where uninvolved colleagues would rigorously challenge new project plans before they started.

moments.speaker: Nabeel S. Qureshi
moments.text: As soon as we have titles, you have a thing that people are competing for and then you get these very unproductive conflicts. You get people optimizing to game the system. You get Goodhart's law everywhere.
moments.summary: Palantir eliminated titles to prevent internal competition and gaming, forcing people to continuously earn their place through merit.

moments.speaker: Nabeel S. Qureshi
moments.text: Every week, you would have a cadence where Monday you go in, do your meetings. Monday night, you build something. Tuesday, you show it to somebody. Tuesday, you get the feedback. Tuesday night, you iterate on it. You get four or five of these cycles every single week.
moments.summary: Forward deployed engineers achieved rapid iteration cycles of four to five feedback loops per week by working on-site with customers.

moments.speaker: Nabeel S. Qureshi
moments.text: I think it's very hard to not be anchored to your own experience and your conceptions of a problem. One thing I've seen in really strong founders is they're able to drop a bunch of those assumptions and almost treat a new opportunity as a completely blank slate.
moments.summary: The best founders can shed their assumptions and treat new opportunities as blank slates rather than staying anchored to their original vision.

moments.speaker: Nabeel S. Qureshi
moments.text: When you actually feel that difference between somebody who's just checking the boxes and somebody who's kind of an animal in this way, they'll actually go and pursue and accomplish the end outcome, that difference is very, very big and it matters so much for your first 20 people.
moments.summary: For early-stage startups, the biggest hiring differentiator is finding people who obsessively pursue outcomes versus those who just check boxes.

podcasts.title: How have I been complicit in creating the conditions I say I don’t want? | Jerry Colonna (CEO of Reboot, executive coach, former VC)
podcasts.description: How have I been complicit in creating the conditions I say I don’t want?, covering product design, team leadership, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Jerry Colonna
moments.text: The question that I often ask is how have I been complicit in creating the conditions I say I don't want. The purpose of this question is actually to evoke your own agency.
moments.summary: The radical self-inquiry question of complicity in unwanted conditions is designed to restore personal agency rather than assign blame.

moments.speaker: Jerry Colonna
moments.text: When we become attached to the outcome, we inadvertently fuel our own suffering. The deeper attachment is see, I'm not nothing. See, I'm not a nobody, I'm a somebody, and that's the source of the suffering.
moments.summary: Buddhist teaching on attachment: tying your self-worth to business outcomes creates suffering because the real need is proving you matter.

moments.speaker: Jerry Colonna
moments.text: Teams are groups, and there are group dynamics that always happen. Until you make conscious the unconscious patterns operating in the group, the group will continue to repeat those patterns and you will blame somebody in the group.
moments.summary: Dysfunctional teams often mirror the unresolved personal patterns of their leaders; fixing the team starts with the leader doing their own inner work.

moments.speaker: Jerry Colonna
moments.text: When we get too fixed on the proper way to do things, we're setting ourselves up for attachment and therefore suffering. Stay attached to the growth and hold mindset a little loosely.
moments.summary: A growth mindset becomes counterproductive when it becomes rigid; the key is to stay attached to growth while holding the mindset itself loosely.

moments.speaker: Jerry Colonna
moments.text: Why is it that nobody on my team can make a decision without me? Who hired them? How can you hire people whom you expect to make decisions without running them through you if you can't tolerate them making a decision that you disagree with?
moments.summary: Leaders who complain their team cannot make decisions must examine whether they can actually tolerate decisions they disagree with.

podcasts.title: The rise of Cursor: The $300M ARR AI tool that engineers can’t stop using | Michael Truell (co-founder and CEO)
podcasts.description: Michael Truell is the co-founder and CEO of Anysphere, the company behind Cursor—the fastest-growing AI code editor in the world, reaching $300 million in annual recurring revenue just two years after its launch.

moments.speaker: Michael Truell
moments.text: Our goal with Cursor is to invent a new type of programming, a very different way to build software. A world kind of after code. More and more being an engineer will start to feel like being a logic designer, and it will be about specifying your intent.
moments.summary: Cursor's vision is to transform programming from writing code to specifying intent, making engineering more about logic design.

moments.speaker: Michael Truell
moments.text: We definitely didn't expect to be doing any of our own model development when we started. And at this point, every magic moment in Cursor involves a custom model in some way.
moments.summary: Cursor unexpectedly moved into custom model development, and now every key product moment relies on their own trained models.

moments.speaker: Michael Truell
moments.text: I truly just think that the ceiling is so high that no matter what entrenchment you build, you can be leapfrogged. It is incumbent on us to continue to try to build the best thing.
moments.summary: In AI-powered tools, traditional moats are weak because the ceiling for improvement is so high that any company can be leapfrogged.

moments.speaker: Michael Truell
moments.text: I would bias less toward trying in one go to tell the model exactly what to do. Instead chop things up into bits. You're specifying a little bit, getting a little bit of work. At the same time, explicitly try to fall on your face and discover the limits of what the models can do.
moments.summary: The best way to use Cursor is to break tasks into small chunks rather than one big prompt, while also pushing model limits in safe environments.

moments.speaker: Michael Truell
moments.text: Many people you hear hired too fast, I think we actually hired too slow to begin with. Getting the right group of people into the company was the thing that maybe more than anything else, apart from building the product, we really, really fussed over.
moments.summary: Cursor's biggest hiring lesson was that they hired too slowly early on, though obsessing over team quality was critical to their success.

moments.speaker: Michael Truell
moments.text: We're in the middle of a technology shift that's going to be more consequential than the internet. And I think it's going to take a while, it's going to be a multi-decade thing, and many different groups will be consequential in pushing it forward.
moments.summary: AI will be more consequential than the internet but will play out over decades, not months, with many different companies driving progress.

podcasts.title: Inside monday.com’s transformation: radical transparency, impact over output, and their path to $1B ARR | Daniel Lereya (Chief Product and Technology Officer)
podcasts.description: Radical transparency, impact over output, and their path to $1B ARR, covering team leadership, product design, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Daniel Lereya
moments.text: We really have an approach of very radical transparency about everything. Before we went public, we actually shared every bit of information with our employees. Instead of demoralizing people, it gives them a sense of deep partnership.
moments.summary: Monday.com practices radical transparency by sharing all company information with employees, creating deep partnership rather than demoralization.

moments.speaker: Daniel Lereya
moments.text: We had the daily numbers concept. You buy a TV, put it on the wall, and when you had a new paying account, you had the Simpson saying the same with the $1 million. For new signups, you had a tick. Suddenly, everyone is living it.
moments.summary: Monday.com made metrics visceral by displaying real-time dashboards with sound effects for key events, making the whole company feel connected to growth.

moments.speaker: Daniel Lereya
moments.text: We actually announced five different new products simultaneously. People said we're going to confuse our current users. But we managed to transform the company in a very short amount of time. Not taking bold risks is a risk for itself.
moments.summary: Monday.com launched five products at once despite internal resistance, learning that not taking bold moves is itself a major risk.

moments.speaker: Daniel Lereya
moments.text: More time creates more questions. It creates more complications. We really encourage people to get really fast to production, to put traps for themselves that are called by time and not by effort.
moments.summary: Setting time-based deadlines rather than effort-based ones forces teams to focus on core value and ship faster instead of over-building.

moments.speaker: Daniel Lereya
moments.text: There were personally times when suddenly I saw how my biggest strength, like mastering all the details and having everything in my head, created damage by continuing to do the same thing. Don't be afraid to let go of things that you think are superpowers.
moments.summary: As a leader scales, their former superpowers can become liabilities; letting go of what made you successful is essential for continued growth.

podcasts.title: Building a magical AI code editor used by over 1 million developers in four months: The untold story of Windsurf | Varun Mohan (co-founder and CEO)
podcasts.description: Building a magical AI code editor used by over 1 million developers in four months: The untold story of Windsurf, covering engineering tradeoffs, product design, and AI product work.

moments.speaker: Varun Mohan
moments.text: We should be cannibalizing the existing state of our product every six to 12 months. Every six to 12 months, it should make our existing product look silly. It should almost make the form factor of existing product look dumb.
moments.summary: Windsurf's philosophy is to cannibalize their own product every six to twelve months, making the previous version look obsolete.

moments.speaker: Varun Mohan
moments.text: I want the company to almost be like this dehydrated entity. Every hire is like a little bit of water, and we only go back and hire someone when we're back to being dehydrated.
moments.summary: Windsurf keeps the team intentionally lean, treating each hire like water for a dehydrated organism, only adding when truly needed.

moments.speaker: Varun Mohan
moments.text: You don't win by doing 10 things well. You win by doing one thing really well and maybe you fail nine things. In school you optimize for your total GPA. But for companies, I just need to get an A+ on the one class that matters.
moments.summary: Startups win by excelling at one thing, not by being good at everything; it is the opposite of school where you optimize your total GPA.

moments.speaker: Varun Mohan
moments.text: We hired our VP of sales over a year ago. The go-to-market team is now over 80 people. Technical founders sometimes think sales is negative. I think enterprise sales is really valuable.
moments.summary: Windsurf embraced enterprise sales early with 80+ go-to-market staff, rejecting the technical founder bias against sales teams.

moments.speaker: Varun Mohan
moments.text: We tasked everyone at our company to build an app with Windsurf. We've saved over half a million dollars of SaaS products we were going to buy because our go-to-market team has now built apps instead of buying them.
moments.summary: Windsurf's non-technical employees built their own internal tools using the product, saving over half a million dollars in SaaS purchases.

podcasts.title: Everyone’s an engineer now: Inside v0’s mission to create a hundred million builders | Guillermo Rauch (founder and CEO of Vercel, creators of v0 and Next.js)
podcasts.description: Inside v0’s mission to create a hundred million builders, covering product design, AI product work, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Guillermo Rauch
moments.text: People could be more full stack. Imagine a designer that can ship a fully baked product, a product manager that can prototype and ship to production. We shouldn't put limits on ourselves and what we can build.
moments.summary: AI tools like v0 enable designers and PMs to become full-stack builders who can prototype and ship production products directly.

moments.speaker: Guillermo Rauch
moments.text: Now we have 600 engineers. Some of the best things created with v0 have not come from our engineering team. They've come from the marketing team, the sales team, the product management team. The product management team is fascinating, because now they're actually building the product.
moments.summary: At Vercel, the best v0 creations come from non-engineering teams; PMs are now building live interactive product specs instead of static PRDs.

moments.speaker: Guillermo Rauch
moments.text: You can be as ambitious as you want. If you have technical skills, have some suspension of disbelief. Focus more on the product description, what do you want the end user to experience. Try to be open-minded about how well the tool can implement it.
moments.summary: The key to succeeding with v0 is being ambitious about what you ask, focusing on user experience descriptions rather than implementation details.

moments.speaker: Guillermo Rauch
moments.text: Think about LLMs as Oracles that can write software for you, but there's a limit. It's not writing the compiler from scratch. The engineers that learn foundational infrastructure are probably going to be extremely empowered still, for years to come.
moments.summary: LLMs orchestrate existing tools and infrastructure but do not replace foundational engineering; engineers who build infrastructure will remain vital.

moments.speaker: Guillermo Rauch
moments.text: My number one guidance I would give to any startup founder is create a lot of opportunities for people to give you feedback inside the product. When you're building AI products, it's a constant stream of user feedback. For people thinking about not building AI products, it's going to be hard to compete with something that has such a tight feedback loop.
moments.summary: AI products have an inherent advantage of constant user feedback loops; founders should embed feedback opportunities deeply into their products.

podcasts.title: OpenAI’s CPO on how AI changes must-have skills, moats, coding, startup playbooks, more | Kevin Weil (CPO at OpenAI, ex-Instagram, Twitter)
podcasts.description: How AI changes must-have skills, moats, coding, startup playbooks, more, covering AI product work, product design, and go-to-market execution.

moments.speaker: Kevin Weil
moments.text: The AI models that you're using today is the worst AI model you will ever use for the rest of your life. Every two months, computers can do something they've never been able to do before and you need to completely think differently about what you're doing.
moments.summary: Today's AI model is the worst you will ever use; capabilities leap forward every two months requiring constant rethinking of what is possible.

moments.speaker: Kevin Weil
moments.text: We have this philosophy of model maximalism. Our general mindset is in two months there's going to be a better model and it's going to blow away whatever the current set of limitations are. If you're building right on the edge of the capabilities, keep going because you're doing something right.
moments.summary: OpenAI's model maximalism philosophy means building at the frontier of model capabilities, trusting that the next model will unlock what barely works today.

moments.speaker: Kevin Weil
moments.text: You're trying to figure out how some product should work with AI, you can often reason about it the way you would reason about another human and it works. If I asked you something that I needed to think for 20 seconds to answer, I wouldn't just go mute. I also wouldn't start babbling every single thought.
moments.summary: The most counterintuitive AI product design insight is that reasoning about how a human would behave in the same situation often gives the right UX answer.

moments.speaker: Kevin Weil
moments.text: My personal belief is that you want to be pretty PM light as an organization. Too many PMs causes problems. We'll fill the world with decks and ideas versus execution. It's a good thing when a PM is working with slightly too many engineers.
moments.summary: OpenAI keeps PMs intentionally scarce so they cannot micromanage; slightly overstretched PMs empower product-focused engineers to make decisions.

moments.speaker: Kevin Weil
moments.text: I actually think chat is an amazing interface because it's so versatile. It is the way we talk. If I had some more rigid interface, I would be able to speak to you about far fewer things and it would get in the way of maximum communication bandwidth.
moments.summary: Chat is not a placeholder interface for AI but a fundamentally powerful one because it matches how humans naturally communicate with zero restrictions.

moments.speaker: Kevin Weil
moments.text: We use ensembles of models much more internally than people might think. If we have 10 different problems, we might solve them using 20 different model calls, some using specialized fine-tuned models, different sizes for different latency or cost requirements.
moments.summary: OpenAI internally uses ensembles of specialized, fine-tuned models of different sizes rather than one generic model for everything.

podcasts.title: Become a better communicator: Specific frameworks to improve your clarity, influence, and impact | Wes Kao (coach, entrepreneur, advisor)
podcasts.description: Specific frameworks to improve your clarity, influence, and impact, covering product design, startup building, and career development.

moments.speaker: Wes Kao
moments.text: You can continue to think that way and your career might be fine, but if you embrace that if you manage your boss, they're going to appreciate you much more, you're going to get more opportunities, you're going to have more trust with them.
moments.summary: Managing up is not about sucking up; it is a strategic skill where proactively keeping your boss informed builds trust and creates more opportunities.

moments.speaker: Wes Kao
moments.text: As you move up the triangle of the content hierarchy of BS, there's less and less room for BS. In a cohort-based course where your students are right there with you, if you're saying something that doesn't really make sense, there could be a whole conversation happening in Zoom chat.
moments.summary: The content hierarchy of BS framework shows that interactive formats like courses expose weak thinking, while tweets and short posts can hide behind clever phrasing.

moments.speaker: Wes Kao
moments.text: The idea behind the state change method is that you should punctuate your monologues with state changes. State changes are anything that shakes your audience awake and adds variety. Every three to five minutes, go ahead and put in a state change.
moments.summary: The state change method combats audience fatigue by inserting engagement shifts like polls, chat prompts, or screen shares every three to five minutes.

moments.speaker: Wes Kao
moments.text: When you're talking to someone and explaining something, there's usually a moment where their eyes light up because they are genuinely interested. Make note of those moments because their face can't lie. Cut out all the parts that make people go dead in the eyes.
moments.summary: The eyes-light-up method teaches you to watch for genuine reactions during conversations and double down on what truly resonates.

moments.speaker: Wes Kao
moments.text: Alex would always talk about trade-offs. He'd say, 'Yes, I can design this PDF for you. That means the thing I was going to work on today will have to wait.' It went from a conversation about yes or no into how do we make sure the right things get done.
moments.summary: The best way to say no at work is to frame it as trade-offs, letting the requester help you prioritize rather than making it a binary yes or no.

podcasts.title: A better way to plan, build, and ship products | Ryan Singer (creator of “Shape Up,” early employee at 37signals)
podcasts.description: A better way to plan, build, and ship products, covering product design, team leadership, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Ryan Singer
moments.text: If you're doing a home renovation, you can have the most beautiful rendering of the new bedroom. But if you haven't checked if there's electricity in that wall, it's going to drastically change the cost and the time and everything.
moments.summary: Shape Up's core insight is that beautiful plans are worthless without checking technical feasibility first, just like a renovation rendering without checking for wiring.

moments.speaker: Ryan Singer
moments.text: We are not going to start something unless we can see the end from the beginning. We're going to go the other way around and say, what is the maximum amount of time we're willing to go before we actually finish something? Six weeks is the maximum we can see into the future.
moments.summary: Shape Up inverts traditional planning: instead of estimating how long a feature takes, you fix the time budget at six weeks maximum and vary the scope.

moments.speaker: Ryan Singer
moments.text: Instead of 'here's your ticket' or 'here's your user story,' it's 'here's the thing you understand, that makes sense, and now you're going to have freedom to figure out how to actually make this a reality.' We see way more engagement, especially from the technical team.
moments.summary: Giving teams the whole shaped idea rather than pre-shredded tickets dramatically increases engineer engagement and ownership.

moments.speaker: Ryan Singer
moments.text: Those sessions with Jason, they were short, very intense sessions where you're trying to crack the nut together. It wasn't sitting alone writing a document, it wasn't making a bunch of requirements. It wasn't making a beautiful Figma file. It was this super intense collaborative 'what about this? what about that?'
moments.summary: Shaping sessions are intense collaborative whiteboard sessions to crack an idea's core, not solo document writing or polished Figma files.

moments.speaker: Ryan Singer
moments.text: Six weeks is only a maximum. If we think of six weeks as a maximum, that's going to force us to ask some really good questions about what piece of this do we really think we can land. If you try to say in six months we're going to ship this thing, you can't get your arms around all the problems.
moments.summary: The six-week maximum is a ceiling that forces teams to surface unknowns and complexity before building, not an arbitrary sprint length.

podcasts.title: How to win in the AI era: Ship a feature every week, embrace technical debt, ruthlessly cut scope, and create magic your competitors can't copy | Gaurav Misra (CEO and co-founder of Captions)
podcasts.description: Gaurav Misra is the co-founder and CEO of Captions, an AI-powered video creation company and one of the most successful consumer AI products in the world today. Previously he was a product leader at Snap, where he created the design engineering function and spent years helping develop features used by hundreds of.

moments.speaker: Gaurav Misra
moments.text: Our engineering goal is every engineer should ship a marketable product every week.
moments.summary: Captions sets the extreme pace of every engineer shipping a marketable product feature every single week.

moments.speaker: Gaurav Misra
moments.text: Every piece of debt that you take on you have to pay interest on. 1% or 2% of your time every day. If you take on enough debt, you'll be paying 80 or 90% interest and you won't have time to do anything new. You have a technical debt runway.
moments.summary: Technical debt works like financial debt with daily interest payments; too much debt means all your time goes to maintenance with nothing left for building.

moments.speaker: Gaurav Misra
moments.text: We have a second roadmap which we think of as a secret roadmap. Nobody has ever asked for anything on it. Given our unique vantage point, we've come up with special ideas that will completely revolutionize how something is used where we can truly change the behavior of the user.
moments.summary: Captions maintains a secret roadmap of ideas no user has asked for, based on the team's unique insight, which produces their biggest wins.

moments.speaker: Gaurav Misra
moments.text: The design team was 10, 12 people at 5,000-6,000 employees. For a long time there were no PMs at all. These weren't your average designers. These were designers who were actually PMs as well. That's what the secret sauce was.
moments.summary: Snap's innovation engine was a tiny design team of 10-12 designer-PMs who controlled product at 6,000 employees, giving the CEO granular control over every user-facing change.

moments.speaker: Gaurav Misra
moments.text: As the company gets bigger, you can actually create alignment by causing internal virality. We would create prototype products, share the build, and it would explode inside the company. Day after day we would hear from engineers, then managers, then VPs, then eventually from Evan.
moments.summary: At Snap, the design engineering team created internal virality with prototypes that spread organically through the company, achieving instant alignment without formal processes.

moments.speaker: Gaurav Misra
moments.text: At Captions, we're going even one step further. Why shouldn't the PM understand marketing? I actually think PMs should own all the way to marketing. Search marketing is just placing a button to your product in Google. That's almost a product surface.
moments.summary: PMs should own the full funnel including marketing because ad placements and acquisition are product surfaces that require the same user understanding.

podcasts.title: Superhuman's secret to success: Ignoring most customer feedback, manually onboarding every new user, obsessing over every detail, and positioning around a single attribute: speed | Rahul Vohra (CEO)
podcasts.description: Rahul Vohra is the founder and CEO of Superhuman. Prior to Superhuman, Rahul founded Rapportive, the first Gmail plug-in to scale to millions of users, which he sold to LinkedIn in 2012.

moments.speaker: Rahul Vohra
moments.text: You have to deliberately not act on the feedback of many of your early users, and this is at the same time as listening to people intensely and building what people want. That's what we're here to do, is to make something that people want, but it can't be all people.
moments.summary: Achieving product-market fit requires deliberately ignoring feedback from users who are not your target audience, even while listening intensely.

moments.speaker: Rahul Vohra
moments.text: This is a technique that I call the switch lock. It's born out of the observation that your calendar says what you thought you were going to do, but it's really only your trail of work that describes what you actually did. How can we capture that? So I came up with the following idea. What if I just did whatever the heck I wanted?
moments.summary: The 'switch lock' technique means tracking what you actually do rather than what your calendar says, letting you focus on what matters most.

moments.speaker: Rahul Vohra
moments.text: You can classify anything that you build in a company into one of two categories, solution deepening and market widening. Solution deepening means making your product better for its existing users, but not making it available to more users. Whereas market widening means making your product available to more users, but not making the product itself any better.
moments.summary: Every company effort falls into either solution deepening (better product for existing users) or market widening (reaching new users), and balancing the two is critical to sustained growth.

moments.speaker: Rahul Vohra
moments.text: The biggest secret of Superhuman, which is something that nobody else does, is that we would manually onboard every single new user for the first five years. People would sign up on the wait list, and when we were ready, our growth team would reach out, schedule a call, get on Zoom, and walk the person through every feature, every shortcut, and every workflow for Superhuman.
moments.summary: Superhuman manually onboarded every user one-on-one for five years, which drove deep engagement and word-of-mouth growth.

moments.speaker: Rahul Vohra
moments.text: I believe that every product should be positioned around a single primary attribute. In our case, that attribute is speed. Now, lots of products try to be good at everything. That tends to be a mistake, because if you try to stand for everything, you stand for nothing.
moments.summary: Successful product positioning means choosing a single primary attribute and committing fully to it rather than trying to be good at everything.

moments.speaker: Rahul Vohra
moments.text: If you haven't tried Superhuman, then gosh, what are you doing? Getting through your email twice as fast, responding one to two days sooner, saving four hours or more every single week, they're all real.
moments.summary: Superhuman's core value proposition is measurable: users get through email twice as fast and save four-plus hours per week.

podcasts.title: Inside Bolt: From near-death to ~$40m ARR in 5 months—one of the fastest-growing products in history | Eric Simons (founder and CEO of StackBlitz)
podcasts.description: Eric Simons is the founder and CEO of StackBlitz, the company behind Bolt—the #1 web-based AI coding agent and one of the fastest-growing products in history. After nearly shutting down, StackBlitz launched Bolt on Twitter and exploded from zero to $40 million ARR and 1 million monthly active users in about five.

moments.speaker: Eric Simons
moments.text: The company was on the verge of going under when we launched Bolt, and what ended up happening is, in the first two months it went from zero to 20 million of ARR. And we've already crossed 30 million of ARR, with the current rate we're on, our forecast for the year is we want to get to 100 million of ARR.
moments.summary: Bolt went from near-death to $20M ARR in two months, becoming one of the fastest-growing products in startup history.

moments.speaker: Eric Simons
moments.text: Most importantly, it's been the people. It's rare to find startups where you have the core group of five, six, seven people that have been there for five years plus.
moments.summary: Bolt's explosive growth was enabled by a rare core team of five to seven people who had worked together for over five years.

moments.speaker: Eric Simons
moments.text: Sonnet was really the first model that flipped the equation. We actually tried building Bolt almost exactly a year ago, with the frontier models at the time. Spent a week or two building it. It just didn't work. The output, the code output was not reliable enough. And then we got a sneak peek of the Sonnet stuff in May and we were like, 'Oh. Okay, we should take that project back off the shelf.'
moments.summary: Anthropic's Sonnet model was the breakthrough that made Bolt possible -- earlier frontier models produced code that was not reliable enough.

moments.speaker: Eric Simons
moments.text: Software is deterministic. When you write code and you hit run, it either runs or it doesn't. And that's the key insight Anthropic really had. They just went deep. This is what they're doing, is just reinforcement learning on basically permutating every type of app you could ever build. I'm extremely bullish. It makes technical sense why, of anything, LLMs are going to get insanely better at writing code than probably most other types of applications.
moments.summary: Code is uniquely suited for AI because it is deterministic and verifiable, enabling massive reinforcement learning at scale.

moments.speaker: Eric Simons
moments.text: I would say, talk to this thing like you do a Linear ticket, or a JIRA ticket. That would be my advice. Talk to this like you're talking to one of the developers on your team. Be specific on things that matter. And on things where you can let it be creative, you can just say, 'Hey, make it prettier.' And it does a really good job when you give it just, vibes.
moments.summary: The best way for PMs to use AI coding tools is to treat them like a developer teammate: be specific on requirements and give creative freedom on aesthetics.

moments.speaker: Eric Simons
moments.text: On any Figma URL, when you're looking at a design that you've made, if you just put bolt.new in front of that URL and hit enter, it's going to suck that design into Bolt, and turn it into a full stack app or mobile app, just out of the box.
moments.summary: Bolt's Figma integration lets users convert any Figma design URL into a working full-stack application with one click.

moments.speaker: Eric Simons
moments.text: It's always good to just have the perspective of, you should start companies to keep the mindset that you're doing it to have fun. So, stoked to see where this goes, one way or the other. It's going to be interesting.
moments.summary: The right mindset for founders is to build companies to have fun, not just to chase outcomes.

podcasts.title: Building Lovable: $10M ARR in 60 days with 15 people | Anton Osika (co-founder and CEO)
