podcasts.description: Anton Osika is the co-founder and CEO of Lovable, which is building what they call “the last piece of software”—an AI-powered tool that turns descriptions into working products without requiring any coding knowledge.

moments.speaker: Anton Osika
moments.text: Lovable is your personal AI software engineer. You describe an idea and then you get a fully working product. The reason is to enable those who have had such a hard time finding people who are good at creating software that's been their absolute bottleneck and let them take their ideas and their dreams into reality.
moments.summary: Lovable removes the bottleneck of finding engineering talent by letting anyone turn an idea description into a working product.

moments.speaker: Anton Osika
moments.text: The best word for a great product is that it's lovable. A lot of jargon that I like to use to emphasize what we should be striving for is building a minimum lovable product and then building a lovable product and then building an absolutely lovable product. So I took that jargon with me in the company name.
moments.summary: The concept of 'minimum lovable product' rather than minimum viable product became the company's identity and north star.

moments.speaker: Anton Osika
moments.text: I think the number one most important skill when AI can do more and more is taste, like having really good taste for what to build and what is a great product. And so in some ways, the people that are like the best product people, like you, I actually think they're going to become more and more important over time.
moments.summary: As AI handles more execution, taste -- knowing what to build and what makes a great product -- becomes the most important human skill.

moments.speaker: Anton Osika
moments.text: Just start simple and then what you get is that the AI says, okay, I can go through what does a beautiful Airbnb clone look like and it goes through a bit of design decisions and then I'll zoom out to see more of it. We have this just UI that is... I mean it has all the nice things you would expect from Airbnb clone where you see different categories and you can see two listings from Airbnb with login buttons and everything.
moments.summary: A two-word prompt ('Airbnb clone') produces a full UI with categories, listings, and login buttons in 30 seconds.

moments.speaker: Anton Osika
moments.text: The big learning is that you have to start with how is this product working end-to-end and then add AI or think where should we add AI. You really want to see what does the big picture of the user experience look like, and then add something with AI to solve specific problems.
moments.summary: Start with the end-to-end user experience first, then add AI to solve specific problems -- not the other way around.

moments.speaker: Anton Osika
moments.text: I think if you spend a full week on trying to reach an outcome, the best way to learn is I want to do this thing and then I want to use AI to do that thing. And you've spent a full week, you are in the top 1% in the global population. And if you surround yourself with friends who have this obsession or they also care a lot about this, then you'd be quickly in the top 0.1%.
moments.summary: Spending just one focused week using AI tools to solve a real problem puts you in the top 1% of AI fluency globally.

podcasts.title: Notion’s lost years, its near collapse during Covid, staying small to move fast, the joy and suffering of building horizontal, more | Ivan Zhao (CEO and co-founder)
podcasts.description: Ivan Zhao is the co-founder and CEO of Notion. Ivan shares the untold story of Notion, from nearly running out of database space during Covid to finding product-market fit after several “lost years,” and the hard-won lessons along the way.

moments.speaker: Ivan Zhao
moments.text: We called it sugar-coated broccoli. People don't want to eat the broccoli but people like sugar, so it gave them the sugar then hide your broccoli inside of it.
moments.summary: Notion disguised its ambitious vision of everyone creating software inside a familiar productivity tool to achieve product-market fit.

moments.speaker: Ivan Zhao
moments.text: I think one element is we have to be very honest about what we don't know and we don't know users. It took us two years to realize we need to build a productivity tool.
moments.summary: Radical honesty about what you do not understand about your users is essential to finding product-market fit.

moments.speaker: Ivan Zhao
moments.text: We were 10 people when COVID hit. We nearly ran out of database space. We had to scramble. That near-collapse was actually the moment that forced us to get serious about infrastructure.
moments.summary: Notion's near-collapse during COVID with only 10 people forced the team to finally invest in infrastructure at scale.

moments.speaker: Ivan Zhao
moments.text: Staying small is a feature, not a bug. When you're small, you can move fast. Every person you add is a communication overhead. We stayed at about 50 people for a very long time intentionally.
moments.summary: Notion deliberately stayed small because each additional person adds communication overhead that slows down product velocity.

moments.speaker: Ivan Zhao
moments.text: The joy and suffering of building horizontal is that you're competing with everyone. Every feature you build, there's a company that does only that one thing. But the magic is in the integration.
moments.summary: Horizontal products compete with every specialist tool, but the real value is in how features integrate together.

podcasts.title: The creator of WordPress opens up about becoming an internet villain, why he’s taking a stand, and the future of open source | Matt Mullenweg (founder and CEO, Automattic)
podcasts.description: Why products like Meta’s Llama are “fake open source”.

moments.speaker: Matt Mullenweg
moments.text: WordPress.org is the open source project, WordPress.com is our hosted service, and WP Engine is a company that makes a ton of money from WordPress and gives almost nothing back. They're strip-mining the commons.
moments.summary: The WordPress vs WP Engine conflict centers on a company profiting from open source without contributing back, which Mullenweg calls strip-mining the commons.

moments.speaker: Matt Mullenweg
moments.text: Products like Meta's Llama are fake open source. You can look at the code but you can't actually modify it and use it however you want. That's not open source, that's source-available.
moments.summary: Mullenweg draws a sharp distinction between true open source and source-available products like Meta's Llama.

moments.speaker: Matt Mullenweg
moments.text: Tumblr had these multiple post types. You could post a chat, an image. They were one of the first to support video, so they did a lot of product innovation under the leadership of David Karp.
moments.summary: Tumblr pioneered product innovations like native image and video posting that later became standard features on other social networks.

moments.speaker: Matt Mullenweg
moments.text: We bought Tumblr for $3 million. But it was free like a puppy, not free like beer. We took on all liabilities, including FTC investigations and lawsuits.
moments.summary: Acquiring Tumblr cheaply still meant inheriting massive liabilities, illustrating that acquisition cost is not the full picture.

moments.speaker: Matt Mullenweg
moments.text: We say publicly it's about a half a billion dollars in sort of ARR revenue right now.
moments.summary: Automattic generates roughly half a billion dollars in annual recurring revenue, with WooCommerce driving the majority.

podcasts.title: An inside look at X’s Community Notes | Keith Coleman (VP of Product) and Jay Baxter (ML Lead)
podcasts.description: An inside look at X’s Community Notes, covering consumer products, product design, and AI product work.

moments.speaker: Keith Coleman
moments.text: Someone on X can see a post. If they think it's misleading, they can propose a note that they think other people might find informative. Other people can then rate that note.
moments.summary: Community Notes works by letting anyone propose context on misleading posts, with quality determined by crowd ratings.

moments.speaker: Jay Baxter
moments.text: We actually look for agreement from people who have disagreed in the past. And what we see is when people actually have that sort of surprising agreement, that's what makes the notes so neutral and accurate.
moments.summary: Community Notes achieves neutrality by specifically seeking consensus among people who normally disagree with each other.

moments.speaker: Keith Coleman
moments.text: Every post is eligible for notes. We shouldn't exempt Elon. We shouldn't exempt government figures. Even advertisers can get notes.
moments.summary: Community Notes applies equally to everyone including the platform owner and advertisers, with no exemptions.

moments.speaker: Keith Coleman
moments.text: The opt in is important, and it may even be that you want to opt in at one point in your life, and maybe at another point in your life something else is better. Whatever it is you're choosing to do, it's nice to be opting in to feel like it's aligned with how you want to spend your time.
moments.summary: High-intensity work culture functions best when people opt in voluntarily rather than being forced into it.

podcasts.title: OpenAI researcher on why soft skills are the future of work | Karina Nguyen (Research at OpenAI, ex-Anthropic)
podcasts.description: Why soft skills are the future of work, covering AI product work, product design, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Karina Nguyen
moments.text: When I first came to Anthropic and I was like, 'Oh my God, I really love front-end engineering.' And then the reason why I switched to research is because I realized, 'Oh my God, Claude is getting better at front-end. Claude is getting better at coding.'
moments.summary: Nguyen switched from front-end engineering to AI research after realizing models were rapidly catching up to human coding abilities.

moments.speaker: Karina Nguyen
moments.text: Creative thinking and you kind of want to generate a bunch of ideas and filter through them. I think it's actually really, really hard to teach the model how to be aesthetic or really good visual design or how to be extremely creative.
moments.summary: Creative thinking and aesthetic judgment will be the most valuable human skills as AI handles more technical execution.

moments.speaker: Karina Nguyen
moments.text: When you taught the model some of the self-knowledge of you actually don't have a physical body to operate in the physical world, the model would get extremely confused.
moments.summary: Teaching AI models about their own limitations and lack of physical embodiment creates deep confusion in the training process.

moments.speaker: Karina Nguyen
moments.text: The biggest misconception is that these models are just next-token predictors. There's actually a lot of emergent behavior that we don't fully understand yet.
moments.summary: Large language models exhibit emergent behaviors beyond simple next-token prediction that researchers still do not fully understand.

podcasts.title: Tobi Lutke
podcasts.description: Yeah, again, I only lived one life, so I can't Monte Carlo all the decisions I make and just figure out which ones ended up being load baring, right, covering product design, career development, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: Tobi Lutke
moments.text: What is your energy source? My energy source is dissatisfaction with status quo. I think today is the dystopia of the future. It behooves us to try to build the kinds of products that leads towards progress.
moments.summary: Lutke's driving force as a builder is dissatisfaction with the status quo, viewing the present as the dystopia that future generations will want to improve upon.

moments.speaker: Tobi Lutke
moments.text: Pessimism sounds extremely sophisticated. Optimism always sounds dumb or at least naive. The most powerful unquantifiable things in the world of business are fun and delight.
moments.summary: Optimism is underrated in business because pessimism sounds more sophisticated, yet fun and delight are the most powerful unquantifiable business advantages.

moments.speaker: Tobi Lutke
moments.text: I only lived one life, so I can't Monte Carlo all the decisions I make and just figure out which ones ended up being load bearing.
moments.summary: You cannot run simulations on your life decisions, so you have to make bold bets without knowing which choices will prove most consequential.

moments.speaker: Tobi Lutke
moments.text: Everyone at Shopify has to be able to build something with AI. If you're not learning how to use these tools, you're falling behind. We made this a company-wide expectation.
moments.summary: Shopify mandated that every employee learn to build with AI tools, making AI literacy a universal company expectation.

moments.speaker: Tobi Lutke
moments.text: If most people are doing it a certain way, I by default don't want to do it that way. That's not contrarianism for its own sake, it's just that the default path is usually optimized for average outcomes.
moments.summary: Avoiding the default path is not contrarianism but a recognition that conventional approaches are optimized for average outcomes.

podcasts.title: Linear’s secret to building beloved B2B products | Nan Yu (Head of Product)
podcasts.description: Linear’s secret to building beloved B2B products, covering product design, B2B products, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Nan Yu
moments.text: People talk about this as if there were a trade-off because when they think about speed, the thing they over-index on is rushing or being sloppy. What they should be indexing on is being really competent.
moments.summary: Speed and quality are not trade-offs; truly competent people produce high-quality work faster because mastery enables velocity.

moments.speaker: Nan Yu
moments.text: You have some rough time budget for how long you think something's going to take. By the time 10% of it has passed, after week one, you have something that works that tests some key hypothesis internally.
moments.summary: At Linear, teams aim to have a working prototype testing a key hypothesis within the first 10% of the time budget.

moments.speaker: Nan Yu
moments.text: We think about it as we're building the tool we want to use. And if it becomes bloated, we'll feel it first. We're our own most demanding users.
moments.summary: Linear avoids bloat by dogfooding relentlessly, ensuring the team feels any degradation in quality before customers do.

moments.speaker: Nan Yu
moments.text: Craft is not about making things pretty. Craft is about caring about every detail of the experience. It's the error states, the loading states, the edge cases that nobody thinks about.
moments.summary: True product craft means obsessing over error states, loading states, and edge cases, not just visual polish.

moments.speaker: Nan Yu
moments.text: We don't have product managers at Linear. Engineers own the product decisions. That forces everyone to think about why they're building something, not just how.
moments.summary: Linear operates without product managers, requiring engineers to own both the why and how of product decisions.

podcasts.title: An operator’s guide to product strategy | Chandra Janakiraman (CPO at VRChat, ex-Meta, Headspace, Zynga)
podcasts.description: The difference between “small s” and “big S” strategy.

moments.speaker: Chandra Janakiraman
moments.text: I started noticing that there was a certain mystique and aura about product strategy. There was this perception that some people were intrinsically really good at strategy and others were not. It was almost as if there was a strategy gene.
moments.summary: Product strategy is wrongly perceived as an innate talent rather than a learnable craft with systematic processes.

moments.speaker: Chandra Janakiraman
moments.text: There is a smallest flavor of it which focuses on solving problems, they're called present forward, and it typically operates in a two-year horizon. We use a five-stage process to get there and it takes about eight to 12 weeks.
moments.summary: Small-s product strategy uses a five-stage, 8-12 week process focused on solving present problems within a two-year horizon.

moments.speaker: Chandra Janakiraman
moments.text: I actually don't recommend thinking about resources in the strategy phase, because what you're saying is 'these are the areas of focus' and the resourcing question becomes more relevant from a road mapping standpoint.
moments.summary: Separating strategy from resourcing keeps strategic thinking pure, deferring resource allocation to the roadmapping phase.

moments.speaker: Chandra Janakiraman
moments.text: The reason I think this process works is there is a ton of alignment built in. It goes back to human psychology of just something that comes from you feels a lot more familiar and easy to accept.
moments.summary: Strategy processes succeed when they build alignment through co-creation, leveraging the psychological principle that people accept ideas they helped shape.

podcasts.title: How to break out of autopilot and create the life you want | Graham Weaver (Stanford GSB professor, founder of Alpine Investors)
podcasts.description: Break out of autopilot and creating the life you want, covering product design, career development, and B2B products.

moments.speaker: Graham Weaver
moments.text: Imagine that you're walking home from work, you see this bright, shiny object, and you realize it's a magic lamp. And you rub the lamp and this genie comes out and the genie says, 'Hey, I can give you one wish. Whatever you throw yourself into with your whole life and your career, it's going to turn out great.'
moments.summary: The magic genie exercise helps people identify what they would pursue if success were guaranteed, revealing their true calling.

moments.speaker: Graham Weaver
moments.text: Most people are on autopilot. They're making decisions based on what they fell into, not what they actually want. Breaking out of autopilot requires you to first notice you're on it.
moments.summary: Most people run their careers on autopilot based on what they fell into rather than deliberately choosing their path.

moments.speaker: Graham Weaver
moments.text: The hard path and the easy path both take the same amount of time. The hard path is the one where you do the thing you're afraid of. The easy path is the one where you avoid it. But at the end of your life, the hard path gives you a much better outcome.
moments.summary: The hard path and easy path take the same time, but the hard path of confronting fears yields dramatically better life outcomes.

moments.speaker: Graham Weaver
moments.text: Write down the things you're tolerating. The things you're putting up with that you know you shouldn't be. That list is your roadmap for transformation.
moments.summary: Your list of things you are tolerating but should not be is effectively a roadmap for personal and professional transformation.

podcasts.title: Behind the founder: Drew Houston (Dropbox)
podcasts.description: Drew Houston (Dropbox), covering product design, startup building, and go-to-market execution.

moments.speaker: Drew Houston
moments.text: For the first several years, it was doubling, 10-xing every year. Taping user counts that we printed out to the wall, and then running out of space on the wall.
moments.summary: Dropbox's early growth was so explosive they literally ran out of wall space taping up user count milestones.

moments.speaker: Drew Houston
moments.text: We started getting all the incumbents. Apple, Microsoft, Google. All of them launched competing products, but weirdly, it was sort of like you see the videos where there's the mushroom cloud in the distance. You see it. But you don't hear, or notice it.
moments.summary: When Apple, Microsoft, and Google all launched competing products, Dropbox could see the threat coming but initially underestimated its impact.

moments.speaker: Drew Houston
moments.text: 2015 was a pivotal year where things started to shift. It was clear that winter was coming. We had to completely rethink who we were and what we were building for.
moments.summary: 2015 forced Dropbox to fundamentally rethink its identity and strategy as competitive pressure from tech giants intensified.

moments.speaker: Drew Houston
moments.text: The biggest lesson from that era is you have to be willing to cannibalize yourself. If you're not willing to disrupt your own product, someone else will do it for you.
moments.summary: Companies must be willing to cannibalize their own products because if they do not disrupt themselves, competitors will.

moments.speaker: Drew Houston
moments.text: What I've learned building Dropbox Dash is that AI is going to change the fundamental interface for how people interact with their information. Search becomes the new file system.
moments.summary: AI will transform how people interact with information, making search the new file system through products like Dropbox Dash.

podcasts.title: Scripts for difficult conversations: Giving hard feedback, navigating defensiveness, the three questions you should end every meeting with, more | Alisa Cohn (executive coach)
podcasts.description: Giving hard feedback, navigating defensiveness, the three questions you should end every meeting with, more, covering team leadership, career development, and product design.

moments.speaker: Alisa Cohn
moments.text: Hope for the future is so important. I know this is going to be challenging for you to hear, not going to promote you, but I want you to know this. It's really important to me that you're able to succeed in your career here.
moments.summary: When delivering hard news like a denied promotion, leading with genuine hope for the person's future makes the conversation more constructive.

moments.speaker: Alisa Cohn
moments.text: They're trying now to be the leader who everyone loves, but what really needs to happen very often is, we need to drive towards results. This employee continuing to not really do a great job, you don't want to push them because you don't want to upset them. Ultimately, that leads to the demise of your company.
moments.summary: Leaders who prioritize being liked over driving results end up tolerating poor performance, which can ultimately destroy the company.

moments.speaker: Alisa Cohn
moments.text: The three questions you should end every meeting with: What did we decide? Who is going to do what by when? And what do we need to communicate to others?
moments.summary: End every meeting with three questions: what did we decide, who owns what by when, and what needs to be communicated to others.

moments.speaker: Alisa Cohn
moments.text: When someone gets defensive, don't match their energy. Say: 'I can see this is hard to hear. I want to give you a moment. We can come back to this.' That pause is incredibly powerful.
moments.summary: When someone becomes defensive during feedback, pausing and acknowledging the difficulty rather than matching their energy is the most powerful response.

moments.speaker: Alisa Cohn
moments.text: The best script for hard feedback is SBI: Situation, Behavior, Impact. Here's the situation, here's the behavior I observed, here's the impact it had. It removes the judgment and makes it about observable facts.
moments.summary: The SBI framework (Situation, Behavior, Impact) removes personal judgment from feedback by grounding it in observable facts.

podcasts.title: Why great AI products are all about the data | Shaun Clowes (CPO Confluent, ex-Salesforce, Atlassian)
podcasts.description: Why great AI products are all about the data, covering product strategy and execution, team leadership, and AI product work.

moments.speaker: Shaun Clowes
moments.text: Why is it that product management is still such a relatively undeveloped discipline? We're like 15 to 20 years into this, and so there's something about the current state of product management that isn't getting at the truly important things.
moments.summary: Product management remains an undeveloped discipline 15-20 years in, failing to consistently develop practitioners in the truly value-added skills.

moments.speaker: Shaun Clowes
moments.text: In everything always talk from the customer's perspective, from the market's perspective, from the competitor's perspective. The very small number of PMs do that. They get dragged into internal politics, scrum management.
moments.summary: The best PMs consistently frame everything from customer, market, and competitor perspectives rather than getting dragged into internal politics and scrum management.

moments.speaker: Shaun Clowes
moments.text: The way AI will most impact product management is data management. If you don't have your data house in order, your AI products will be garbage. Data is the moat, not the model.
moments.summary: AI's biggest impact on product management will be through data management because the quality of your data, not your model, determines product quality.

moments.speaker: Shaun Clowes
moments.text: If we were doctors, you'd be like, 'That's totally unacceptable.' Imagine if after 20 years of medical practice, most doctors still couldn't reliably diagnose common conditions.
moments.summary: The low bar for PM competency would be unacceptable in fields like medicine, highlighting how much the discipline still needs to mature.

podcasts.title: Behind the founder: Marc Benioff
podcasts.description: Number two is I need to find more fuel in the company to fuel this idea because this is clearly a breakthrough product, so how do I get everyone focused on it, covering B2B products, product design, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Marc Benioff
moments.text: I'm throwing everything against the wall. I'm looking at what's going to stick. I am looking to try to find the winning tactic and turn it into a winning strategy.
moments.summary: Benioff's approach to launching Salesforce was relentless experimentation, throwing everything at the wall and turning winning tactics into strategy.

moments.speaker: Marc Benioff
moments.text: I actually never look at the stock. I find the stock to be very distracting. The stock isn't the goal. That's not why we're doing this.
moments.summary: Benioff deliberately ignores the stock price because he finds it a distraction from the actual mission of building Salesforce.

moments.speaker: Marc Benioff
moments.text: I keep having these existential freakout moments about AI. This is really moving fast.
moments.summary: Even a seasoned CEO like Benioff has existential freakout moments about the pace of AI advancement.

moments.speaker: Marc Benioff
moments.text: Number two is I need to find more fuel in the company to fuel this idea because this is clearly a breakthrough product, so how do I get everyone focused on it.
moments.summary: When you identify a breakthrough product opportunity, the key leadership challenge is redirecting company-wide focus and resources toward it.

podcasts.title: How Shopify builds a high-intensity culture | Farhan Thawar (VP and Head of Eng)
podcasts.description: How Shopify builds a high-intensity culture, covering product design, engineering tradeoffs, and career development.

moments.speaker: Farhan Thawar
moments.text: If you do the hard path and it doesn't work, actually you still win because you've now done something hard. You've probably worked with smart people. You've learned something along the way that is valuable.
moments.summary: Taking the hard path is always worthwhile because even failure produces learning and growth that easy paths never provide.

moments.speaker: Farhan Thawar
moments.text: Everyone says, 'Oh yeah, work hard and do more hours when you're young, whatever.' I'm like, 'What if you just did more per minute?'
moments.summary: Instead of working longer hours, Thawar advocates for maximizing output per minute through higher intensity.

moments.speaker: Farhan Thawar
moments.text: We have a Delete Code Club. We can always almost find a million-plus lines of code to delete, which is insane.
moments.summary: Shopify's Delete Code Club consistently finds over a million lines of code to remove, reflecting a culture of aggressive simplification.

moments.speaker: Farhan Thawar
moments.text: Tobi looked at me and he goes, 'You should tell everyone this story.' He goes, 'I will always come down harshly on people who do not take risks, and you did not take a risk in this case. But if you take a risk and it doesn't work out, you'll never get in trouble.'
moments.summary: Tobi Lutke punishes risk-aversion rather than failed risks, and Thawar's hedged React Native decision cost 18 months of 100 engineers' time.

moments.speaker: Farhan Thawar
moments.text: I had 120 direct reports. I just didn't think I needed managers. I figured out ways to not have managers be the answers to questions. Pair programming helps you get unblocked quickly. Having a product backlog can tell you what to work on.
moments.summary: Thawar managed 120 direct reports by replacing traditional management with systems like pair programming and product backlogs for unblocking.

moments.speaker: Farhan Thawar
moments.text: Everything you know is wrong. The reason I like that one is it's this notion of if all the knowledge you knew was incorrect, could you from first principles build up a view of the world?
moments.summary: Thawar's life motto 'everything you know is wrong' drives constant experimentation and first-principles thinking.

podcasts.title: Behind the product: Duolingo streaks | Jackson Shuttleworth (Group PM, Retention Team)
podcasts.description: Duolingo streaks, covering growth systems, product design, and measurement and analysis.

moments.speaker: Jackson Shuttleworth
moments.text: Streaks is the most impactful feature. We have, right now, over 9 million users with a year plus streak. If you look at the numbers, I think it's been our biggest growth lever.
moments.summary: Duolingo streaks, with over 9 million users maintaining year-plus streaks, is the company's most impactful feature and biggest growth lever.

moments.speaker: Jackson Shuttleworth
moments.text: Test everything. We've run in the last four years over 600 experiments on the streaks, so every other day. We've actually set up really good infrastructure for copy testing.
moments.summary: Duolingo runs an experiment on streaks every other day, totaling 600+ experiments in four years, with dedicated copy testing infrastructure.

moments.speaker: Jackson Shuttleworth
moments.text: We used to say continue, our standard CTA is continue, and we changed that to commit to my goal, and it was a massive win.
moments.summary: Changing a single CTA from 'continue' to 'commit to my goal' was a massive win, demonstrating the power of psychologically resonant copy.

moments.speaker: Jackson Shuttleworth
moments.text: What Duolingo really focuses on is, how do we help users build habits around language learning? Getting a user to come back the next day is the biggest problem to solve.
moments.summary: Duolingo frames its core challenge as habit formation, not language teaching, with next-day retention as the most important metric.

moments.speaker: Jackson Shuttleworth
moments.text: Loss aversion is way more powerful than reward seeking. The fear of losing your streak is more motivating than any badge or reward we could give you.
moments.summary: Loss aversion through streak protection is far more motivating than any positive reward, which is why streaks drive Duolingo's retention so effectively.

podcasts.title: Seth Godin's best tactics for building remarkable products, strategies, brands and more
podcasts.description: Seth Godin's best tactics for building remarkable products, strategies, brands and more, covering product design, team leadership, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Seth Godin
moments.text: AI very soon is going to stop being a feature the same way electricity is not a feature. What AI companies and all companies need to do is say, what's in this for the user? What promise do I want to make, a difficult promise, a remarkable promise, and then how do I keep it?
moments.summary: AI will stop being a differentiating feature like electricity stopped being one; companies must focus on the promise they make to users, not the technology.

moments.speaker: Seth Godin
moments.text: I define good taste as knowing what other people want just before they do. People are seen as having good taste when they bring something to the world that the world didn't necessarily expect but is glad to see.
moments.summary: Good taste is the ability to anticipate what people want before they know it themselves, delivering the pleasantly unexpected.

moments.speaker: Seth Godin
moments.text: Quality is not luxury. Quality is not perfection. Quality means meeting spec, and if you meet spec, you're done. If you don't think the spec is good enough, make a better spec.
moments.summary: Quality means meeting spec, not perfection; if the spec feels insufficient, the answer is to raise the spec, not to endlessly polish.

moments.speaker: Seth Godin
moments.text: If you don't build the network effect into what you are making, you are almost certainly going to fail. The question is, will this work better for my users if they tell other people about it?
moments.summary: Software products that do not have network effects built in from the start are almost certainly going to fail.

moments.speaker: Seth Godin
moments.text: When you choose your smallest viable audience, what language they speak, how much money they have, what problem they're trying to solve, you have chosen everything that's going to go into the product and what your future is going to be like.
moments.summary: Choosing your smallest viable audience is the most consequential product decision because it determines everything about what you build.

moments.speaker: Seth Godin
moments.text: I have a very emotional connection to Claude.ai. I think they have a brand. ChatGPT's reputation with me is not good because it regularly over promises and under delivers and it does it without kindness or humility.
moments.summary: Godin sees Claude as having a genuine brand built on kindness and humility, while ChatGPT over-promises and under-delivers.

podcasts.title: How a great founder becomes a great CEO | Jonathan Lowenhar (co-founder of Enjoy The Work)
podcasts.description: How a great founder becomes a great CEO, covering startup building, product design, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Jonathan Lowenhar
moments.text: To be a founder is a state of being, it's an attitude. To be a CEO is a craft. The more founders who can accept that those are two separate things and they're both equally important to build an ascendant startup, the better all of us will be.
moments.summary: Being a founder is a mindset while being a CEO is a learnable craft, and recognizing these as separate skills is crucial for startup success.

moments.speaker: Jonathan Lowenhar
moments.text: Founder mode gets me angry. That article just got me hot. It really felt like an excuse. We were giving founders permission to not learn the job.
moments.summary: Lowenhar sees the 'founder mode' concept as a dangerous excuse that gives founders permission to avoid learning the craft of being CEO.

moments.speaker: Jonathan Lowenhar
moments.text: There are these two phases to a startup journey. Phase one is finding product-market fit. Phase two is building the company. Most founders focus entirely on phase one and never learn how to do phase two.
moments.summary: Most founders obsess over finding product-market fit but never learn the equally critical second phase of actually building the company.

moments.speaker: Jonathan Lowenhar
moments.text: The greatest CEOs know when to calibrate which one is needed. It's not manager mode is bad, it's that you need both modes and you need to know when to deploy each.
moments.summary: Great CEOs do not choose between founder mode and manager mode; they calibrate between both based on what the situation demands.

moments.speaker: Jonathan Lowenhar
moments.text: The founders who scale are the ones who realize that their job description changes every six months. What got you here won't get you there, and that's not a failure, it's the nature of the journey.
moments.summary: Founders who scale successfully accept that their job description fundamentally changes every six months as the company grows.

podcasts.title: Behind the product: Replit | Amjad Masad (co-founder and CEO)
podcasts.description: Behind the product: Replit, covering product design, AI product work, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Amjad Masad
moments.text: The idea behind Replit is that making software today is very difficult. We want to make it easier. People view this as a developer in their pocket essentially. We have 34 million users globally.
moments.summary: Replit's mission is to democratize software creation, serving as a developer-in-your-pocket for 34 million global users.

moments.speaker: Amjad Masad
moments.text: Typically, you're bottlenecked where your ideas are not fitting in because they need to be made and they need to be made quickly. Now, you open up that bottleneck. So now actually making things is a lot easier. Actually, you become limited by how fast you can generate ideas.
moments.summary: AI removes the implementation bottleneck in product development, shifting the constraint from building speed to idea generation speed.

moments.speaker: Amjad Masad
moments.text: I could imagine whatever five years from now, someone running a billion-dollar company with zero employees where it's like the support is handled by AI, the development is handled by AI, and you're just building and creating this thing.
moments.summary: Masad envisions billion-dollar companies run by a single person within five years, with AI handling support, development, and operations.

moments.speaker: Amjad Masad
moments.text: The evals are everything. You can't ship an AI feature without a way to measure whether it's actually good. We built an entire internal evaluation infrastructure before we shipped our agent.
moments.summary: Replit built comprehensive internal evaluation infrastructure before shipping their AI agent because evals are the foundation of AI product quality.

moments.speaker: Amjad Masad
moments.text: The best way to build an AI product is to use it yourself constantly. Our team uses Replit Agent to build Replit. That feedback loop is the fastest way to improve.
moments.summary: Replit uses its own AI agent to build Replit itself, creating the tightest possible feedback loop for product improvement.

podcasts.title: Building Wiz: the fastest-growing startup in history | Raaz Herzberg (CMO and VP Product Strategy)
podcasts.description: The fastest-growing startup in history, covering product design, team leadership, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Raaz Herzberg
moments.text: I was hired as the first product manager. I sat in on those calls. I still did not exactly understand what we were going to build, which was confusing. And that ended up pivoting us around to cloud security.
moments.summary: Wiz's first PM joined before the product idea was figured out, and her confusion about what to build actually catalyzed the pivot to cloud security.

moments.speaker: Raaz Herzberg
moments.text: We would have 10 to 15 meetings every day with potential customers. At the time, we didn't really have a solid product yet.
moments.summary: Wiz conducted 10-15 customer meetings daily before having a product, using extreme customer proximity to find product-market fit.

moments.speaker: Raaz Herzberg
moments.text: We went from zero to $100 million ARR faster than any SaaS company in history. But it wasn't luck. It was the combination of a team that had built and sold a company together before, and a market that was screaming for a solution.
moments.summary: Wiz's record-breaking growth from zero to $100M ARR came from a team with prior exit experience targeting a market with extreme urgency.

moments.speaker: Raaz Herzberg
moments.text: The founders had a principle: always sell to the CISO, never to the security engineer. Because if you sell to the engineer, you get a pilot. If you sell to the CISO, you get a deployment.
moments.summary: Wiz deliberately sold to CISOs rather than security engineers because executive buyers commit to deployments, not pilots.

moments.speaker: Raaz Herzberg
moments.text: Our product strategy is simple: we want to be the operating system for cloud security. Every new product we build has to connect to the graph. That's our moat.
moments.summary: Wiz's moat is its connected security graph, and every new product must integrate into it, creating compounding value.

podcasts.title: Breaking the rules of growth: Why Shopify bans KPIs, optimizes for churn, prioritizes intuition, and builds toward a 100-year vision | Archie Abrams (VP Product, Head of Growth at Shopify)
podcasts.description: Why Shopify bans KPIs, optimizes for churn, prioritizes intuition, and builds toward a 100-year vision, covering measurement and analysis, growth systems, and product design.

moments.speaker: Archie Abrams
moments.text: When you have teams naturally break up the world into different funnel stages, it gets very seductive to look at my part of the funnel and what's my conversion rate. But in practice, it's actually almost always easier to just make it harder to do the thing right before your step in the funnel to increase your conversion rate.
moments.summary: Funnel-stage teams are tempted to optimize their conversion rate by making the prior step harder, which is counterproductive to overall growth.

moments.speaker: Archie Abrams
moments.text: The best way to get more people to get to a step is just get more people in the door in the first place. That will always hurt your conversion rate, but it may actually give you more people on the outside.
moments.summary: Widening the funnel top hurts conversion rates but produces more absolute outcomes, which matters more than percentages.

moments.speaker: Archie Abrams
moments.text: Shopify doesn't have KPIs in the traditional sense. We have a north star which is GMS, gross merchandise sales, and everything else is context. We trust people to make good decisions.
moments.summary: Shopify avoids traditional KPIs in favor of a single north star metric (GMS) and trusts teams to make decisions with context.

moments.speaker: Archie Abrams
moments.text: We actually optimize for churn. Not to reduce it to zero, but to make sure we're acquiring the right merchants. If we reduce churn by making it hard to leave, we're just trapping bad-fit customers.
moments.summary: Shopify optimizes for healthy churn rather than minimizing it, because trapping bad-fit merchants with exit barriers hurts long-term growth.

moments.speaker: Archie Abrams
moments.text: We build toward a 100-year vision. That sounds abstract but it changes every decision. You stop optimizing for this quarter's numbers and start building things that compound.
moments.summary: Shopify's 100-year vision changes daily decisions by shifting focus from quarterly optimization to building compounding value.

podcasts.title: Shreyas Doshi Live
podcasts.description: How is it doing?" And you want to be able to say, you are the PM leader, you want to be able to say something smart, and something that makes you look competent, covering cross-functional alignment, product design, and product strategy and execution.

moments.speaker: Shreyas Doshi
moments.text: One of the core reasons was I was always super busy. There were about 16 or 17 years where I was just incredibly busy, and because I was incredibly busy, I was extremely stressed.
moments.summary: Doshi spent 16-17 years as a PM leader being overwhelmingly busy before realizing that scope, not efficiency, was the root problem.

moments.speaker: Shreyas Doshi
moments.text: Because I had a real product strategy, not one of those fake ones, a real product strategy that I had gotten alignment on with everybody, my planning for this major product for Stripe took me like three days.
moments.summary: Having a genuine, pre-aligned product strategy reduced Doshi's annual planning from the typical 4-6 weeks to just three days at Stripe.

moments.speaker: Shreyas Doshi
moments.text: Most doors that look like two-way doors are actually one-way doors. They are two-way doors at Bezos' level, but as a PM leader, for you, they are a one-way door, and that's making you busy.
moments.summary: The two-way door framework is misleading for most PM leaders because features that seem reversible almost never actually get killed.

moments.speaker: Shreyas Doshi
moments.text: Taste is about the ability to identify what is really good without needing to see its results. It requires zero taste right now for anybody to say Jensen is a genius. To be able to say that in 2010, that requires taste.
moments.summary: True taste is the ability to recognize greatness before results validate it; calling Jensen Huang a genius in 2024 requires zero taste.

moments.speaker: Shreyas Doshi
moments.text: Our jobs get frustrating when we behave, most of the time, in misalignment with our superpowers and who we truly are at our core. I have a preferred level at which I like to operate.
moments.summary: Career frustration stems from spending most of your time operating at a level misaligned with your core superpowers and natural strengths.

podcasts.title: 4 questions Shreyas Doshi wishes he’d asked himself sooner | Former PM leader at Stripe, Twitter, Google
podcasts.description: 4 questions Shreyas Doshi wishes he’d asked himself sooner, covering team leadership, product design, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Shreyas Doshi
moments.text: The pre-mortem is the most powerful tool I've found. Before you start a project, imagine it's failed. Now write down why it failed. You'll surface risks your team would never bring up otherwise.
moments.summary: Pre-mortems surface hidden risks by asking teams to imagine project failure before starting, unlocking candid discussion of concerns.

moments.speaker: Shreyas Doshi
moments.text: The LNO framework: categorize every task as Leverage, Neutral, or Overhead. Do leverage tasks at 100%, neutral at 80%, and overhead at the minimum acceptable quality.
moments.summary: The LNO framework categorizes tasks as Leverage, Neutral, or Overhead, with effort allocated proportionally to each category's impact.

moments.speaker: Shreyas Doshi
moments.text: Product work happens at three levels: impact, execution, and optics. Most execution problems are actually strategy problems. When you can't get alignment, it's because there's no clear strategy, not because people aren't executing.
moments.summary: Most execution problems are actually strategy problems in disguise; lack of alignment signals missing strategy, not poor execution.

moments.speaker: Shreyas Doshi
moments.text: The biggest prioritization mistake is using impact-effort matrices. They give you a false sense of rigor. In reality, the highest-impact items are almost never low effort, and the low-effort items are almost never high impact.
moments.summary: Impact-effort matrices create false rigor because truly high-impact work is almost never low-effort, making the framework misleading.

moments.speaker: Shreyas Doshi
moments.text: Thinking is cheap, so you should do more thinking, not less. The bias toward action in tech is overdone. Sometimes the highest leverage thing you can do is sit and think for two days before making a decision.
moments.summary: The tech industry's bias toward action is overdone; sometimes the highest-leverage activity is spending two days thinking before deciding.

podcasts.title: Meta’s Head of Product (and 29th employee) on working with Mark Zuckerberg, early growth tactics, why PMs are like conductors, and more | Naomi Gleit
podcasts.description: Meta’s Head of Product (and 29th employee) on working with Mark Zuckerberg, early growth tactics, why PMs are like conductors, and more, covering product strategy and execution, team leadership, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Naomi Gleit
moments.text: I really believe in frameworks for things that helps drive extreme clarity. There needs to be one canonical doc. Everyone should know exactly where the canonical doc is. That's the one place I can go to get all the information I need about a project and it will link to all the other docs.
moments.summary: Every project needs a single canonical document that serves as the definitive source of truth, linking to all other relevant docs.

moments.speaker: Naomi Gleit
moments.text: It also gives people a framework to plug into. A lot of times the creation of a pre-read for these discussions involves many different people from many different teams and functions. If you have a traffic light, they can own filling out their cell, they can own the rationale behind the legal position on option one, two, and three.
moments.summary: Traffic-light frameworks in pre-reads let cross-functional contributors clearly own and present their perspectives on decisions.

moments.speaker: Naomi Gleit
moments.text: People are always like, 'What are you training for?' And I'm like, 'I'm training for life.' I have four musties: eat, sleep a long time, and exercise. Those are the things that I need in order to perform.
moments.summary: Treating exercise as a non-negotiable daily requirement -- training for life, not for a specific event -- is essential for sustained high performance.

moments.speaker: Naomi Gleit
moments.text: I'm trying to find blocks in my day that I can spend time thinking and also within those blocks, they don't have to be alone time. They can also be scheduling my chief of staff and my head of data to bounce ideas off of as a sounding board because that is the process that I know best for me in terms of really developing a first party perspective.
moments.summary: Developing a first-party point of view as a PM requires dedicated thinking time, which can include sparring with one or two trusted people rather than solo reflection.

moments.speaker: Naomi Gleit
moments.text: A PM cannot outsource their perspective or delegate their thinking through people and process. For me that has been a learning curve and I am trying to, as someone who's very consensus driven, I want to hear all the different opinions from all the different people. I can still do that... and then use all of that to synthesize my own.
moments.summary: PMs must synthesize their own first-party perspective rather than outsourcing their thinking, even if they gather input from many stakeholders.

moments.speaker: Naomi Gleit
moments.text: The biggest thing that I can do to improve my surfing is to improve my confidence. The best thing you can actually do in that situation is stand up into your fear, is to ride the wave. That is the safest thing you can do.
moments.summary: In surfing and in leadership, committing fully and standing up into fear is both the safest and most productive approach -- hesitation creates more danger.

podcasts.title: How to see like a designer: The hidden power of typography and logos | Jessica Hische (Lettering Artist, Author)
podcasts.description: The hidden power of typography and logos, covering product design, team leadership, and go-to-market execution.

moments.speaker: Jessica Hische
moments.text: Most people are better at understanding the feelings and sensations that typography and logos give us than they give themselves credit for, because what we are as people are endless absorbers of patterns, and information, and all this kind of stuff as we move throughout the world.
moments.summary: Non-designers intuitively sense when typography or logos feel wrong because humans are constant pattern absorbers -- they just lack the vocabulary to name it.

moments.speaker: Jessica Hische
moments.text: A good exercise is just like looking at fonts that are available in the world and asking yourself, 'What feeling does this give me?'
moments.summary: Building design literacy starts with simply asking yourself what feeling a font gives you, training your ability to articulate visual intuition.

podcasts.title: Becoming more strategic, navigating difficult colleagues, harnessing founder mode, and more | Anneka Gupta (Chief Product Officer at Rubrik)
podcasts.description: Becoming more strategic, navigating difficult colleagues, harnessing founder mode, and more, covering startup building, team leadership, and strategic decision-making.

moments.speaker: Anneka Gupta
moments.text: When people say, 'I want someone that's strategic,' what they're really saying is, 'I want someone that can come up with and articulate a compelling and simple why behind the decisions and the direction of the company and product.'
moments.summary: Being strategic means articulating a compelling why behind decisions and championing hard but necessary changes for the long-term good.

moments.speaker: Anneka Gupta
moments.text: The second piece is, 'I want someone that's going to champion and be a change agent to do things that may be hard but actually best for the long-term interest of the product or company, even though those things are not going to be easy to execute on.' And I think if you have one without the other, ultimately people are not going to see you as strategic.
moments.summary: True strategic leaders combine articulating a clear vision with the courage to champion difficult changes -- one without the other falls short.

podcasts.title: Why most public speaking advice is wrong—and how to finally overcome your speaking anxiety | Tristan de Montebello (CEO & co-founder of Ultraspeaking)
podcasts.description: Why most public speaking advice is wrong—and how to finally overcome your speaking anxiety, covering product design, operator lessons, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Tristan de Montebello
moments.text: People tend to get into a public speaking voice. We'll be in a class and they'll be chatting normally and look super normal. And then we'll say, 'Okay, now just a timer, I'm just going to give you a speech. Just speak for 60 seconds so we get a baseline,' and I click play, and suddenly they enter into a different version of themselves, a professional version.
moments.summary: Most people unconsciously switch into an inauthentic 'public speaking voice' when a timer starts -- the key is learning to speak conversationally instead.

moments.speaker: Tristan de Montebello
moments.text: The games are meant to put you in a state of turbulence and find out what was easy, what was hard, what am I noticing? If you did a rep and you got it and it went perfectly, then you're learning nothing. A really easy rep is not worth much. The only reps that are worth something are the ones where you feel an edge.
moments.summary: Meaningful practice in public speaking comes from reps that push you to an edge of discomfort -- perfect reps teach you nothing.

moments.speaker: Tristan de Montebello
moments.text: It's so much more freeing, powerful, connecting, and effective to speak conversation.
moments.summary: Conversational speaking is more powerful and connecting than polished presentation style -- it frees both the speaker and the audience.

podcasts.title: Becoming a conscious leader: Leading without fear, finding your life’s objective function, and getting better at vision and strategy | John Mark Nickels (Uber, Waymo, DoorDash)
podcasts.description: Leading without fear, finding your life’s objective function, and getting better at vision and strategy, covering team leadership, product design, and pricing decisions.

moments.speaker: John Mark Nickels
moments.text: Get clear on your objective function, and one way that I've gotten clear on it is trying to think about it from future me because five years from now, I'm not going to give a if I made the presentation slightly better, but I'm going to care a lot about what kind of relationship I have with my daughters.
moments.summary: Clarify your life's objective function by asking what future-you will actually care about -- it reframes daily priorities away from trivial work tasks.

moments.speaker: John Mark Nickels
moments.text: Not to be morbid, but most of us just aren't really tuned into an awareness that our lives will come to an end. We try to pretend like we're going to live forever and just not think about it. And the horror of it is that we succeed. To me, an awareness and mindfulness that our lives will come to an end punctuates reality in a way that requires me to rethink my priorities.
moments.summary: Accepting mortality is not morbid but clarifying -- it forces a genuine reprioritization of what actually matters in work and life.

podcasts.title: Thinking like a gardener not a builder, organizing teams like slime mold, the adjacent possible, and other unconventional product advice | Alex Komoroske (Stripe, Google)
podcasts.description: Thinking like a gardener not a builder, organizing teams like slime mold, the adjacent possible, and other unconventional product advice, covering product design, AI product work, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Alex Komoroske
moments.text: So much of the way that we tackle problems and build products is this builder mindset. Part of the problem though is it can't possibly create more value than the effort that you put into it. What I look for instead are things that can be gardened, things that can grow on their own and that you can direct or maybe give a little bit of extra energy to or curate over.
moments.summary: The gardener mindset creates compounding value beyond your direct effort, unlike the builder mindset which caps output at the effort you invest.

moments.speaker: Alex Komoroske
moments.text: We've forgotten a world without aggregators. Aggregators make sense in the late stage of an era. At the beginning, they curtail too much exploration.
moments.summary: Aggregators are a late-stage phenomenon that restricts exploration -- the current app paradigm has squeezed software's potential into a dozen boxes on your phone.

moments.speaker: Alex Komoroske
moments.text: Software is alchemy. It's the ability to extend human agency beyond ourselves to create something that can then combine with what others have created in unexpected and unforeseen ways. And somehow in the past decade, we've become convinced that all of this potential should be squeezed into about a dozen little boxes on your phone.
moments.summary: Software should extend human agency and combine with others' creations in unforeseen ways, not be trapped inside monolithic apps controlled by a few companies.

moments.speaker: Alex Komoroske
moments.text: Do things that give you energy that you're proud of, and happiness is reality minus expectations I think are really simple clarifying words.
moments.summary: Two clarifying life principles: pursue work that gives you energy and pride, and remember that happiness equals reality minus expectations.

podcasts.title: How marketplaces win: Liquidity, growth levers, quality, and more | Benjamin Lauzier (Lyft, Thumbtack, Reforge)
podcasts.description: Liquidity, growth levers, quality, and more, covering strategic decision-making, product design, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: Benjamin Lauzier
moments.text: I think when you're running a marketplace, you tend to sit in your ivory tower a little bit, looking at stats and thinking like, 'If only we could get people to do X, it'd be better for everyone.' I think that's missing the point that we're humans.
moments.summary: Marketplace operators must resist the ivory tower trap of pure data optimization and remember that supply-side participants are humans, not variables.

moments.speaker: Benjamin Lauzier
moments.text: I'm a huge believer in market forces and empowerment, so provide guardrails for what a good experience is in your marketplace, set a clear bar for quality, and provide the right coaching and tools for supply to be successful, and then take a step back and see where the gaps are.
moments.summary: Marketplace quality comes from setting clear guardrails and empowering supply with coaching and tools, then identifying gaps rather than micromanaging.

podcasts.title: Lessons in product leadership and AI strategy from Glean, Google, Amazon, and Slack | Tamar Yehoshua (Product at Glean, ex-Google and Slack)
podcasts.description: Lessons in product leadership and AI strategy from Glean, Google, Amazon, and Slack, covering team leadership, product design, and career development.

moments.speaker: Tamar Yehoshua
moments.text: Make sure you go somewhere where you have a good engineering partner. Because if you have great ideas of what to build but you can't get them built, then you go nowhere. So that has to be part of your evaluation criteria that you meet and value your engineering partner before you join.
moments.summary: Before joining a company, evaluate your engineering partner -- great product ideas go nowhere without strong engineering collaboration.

moments.speaker: Tamar Yehoshua
moments.text: You don't want any of this... Like people in the organization, they ask mom, they asked dad and they got different opinions and playing one against the other. That doesn't work.
moments.summary: Product and engineering leaders must be aligned on roles, responsibilities, and direction to prevent teams from playing them against each other.

podcasts.title: Rethinking SEO in the age of AI | Eli Schwartz (SEO advisor, author)
podcasts.description: Rethinking SEO in the age of AI, covering AI product work, go-to-market execution, and growth systems.

moments.speaker: Eli Schwartz
moments.text: Think of SEO as a product. The product managers are the people that should be thinking about this SEO question because it's a product question. This is a user that's doing their own self-discovery journey. If you can't answer the question about what is it that someone's going to do a search on, then don't do SEO.
moments.summary: SEO should be treated as a product problem, not a marketing trick -- PMs should own it because it is about serving users on a self-discovery journey.

moments.speaker: Eli Schwartz
moments.text: Transparently, I thought this was going to be an apocalypse. Up until AI Overviews, whoever won on that long form piece of content would get that first click. But now that doesn't exist anymore.
moments.summary: AI Overviews in search have fundamentally changed SEO by eliminating the first-click advantage that long-form content used to enjoy.

moments.speaker: Eli Schwartz
moments.text: In their documentation, they say that AI itself is not the problem. It's the helpfulness, the usefulness of the content that would be a problem.
moments.summary: Google's stance on AI content is that AI authorship itself is not penalized -- what matters is whether the content is genuinely useful to users.

podcasts.title: The things engineers are desperate for PMs to understand | Camille Fournier (author of “The Manager’s Path,” ex-CTO at Rent the Runway)
podcasts.description: The things engineers are desperate for PMs to understand, covering team leadership, product design, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Camille Fournier
moments.text: Hoarding credit. PMs, they tend to be the front-facing person for initiative. Engineers sometimes think that they don't get the credit for their work because the PM takes all the glory and all the credit for the project that they really worked very hard on.
moments.summary: The number one thing PMs do that annoys engineers is hoarding credit -- being the front-facing person while engineers feel invisible for the real work.

moments.speaker: Camille Fournier
moments.text: The next thing that engineers really get annoyed about with PMs, when they just don't understand the details and act like they don't matter, it just shows a real lack of empathy for the work that engineers are doing and I think it really can be very off-putting.
moments.summary: PMs who dismiss technical details signal a lack of empathy for engineering work, which is deeply off-putting to their engineering partners.

podcasts.title: Land your dream job in today’s market: negotiation tactics, job search councils, and more | Phyl Terry (Author, “Never Search Alone”)
podcasts.description: Negotiation tactics, job search councils, and more, covering strategic decision-making, career development, and consumer products.

moments.speaker: Phyl Terry
moments.text: When you're looking for a job, you need a spear and not a net. What happens when we're building a product? Same thing, right? We want this product to be for everyone, but we've learned with product market fit that doesn't work. We need a narrow, clear focus.
moments.summary: Job searching requires a spear, not a net -- just like product-market fit, you need narrow focus rather than trying to appeal to everyone.

moments.speaker: Phyl Terry
moments.text: While it's hard to figure out your candidate market fit, it's also a relief to know it's not about you. So what I ask people to do is I ask them to think about what they want and what they don't want. Now, you might not think that that's a radical step, but most people don't do that. When they get laid off, they spray and pray.
moments.summary: Most people skip the fundamental step of defining what they want and do not want in a job -- instead they spray and pray, which rarely works.

moments.speaker: Phyl Terry
moments.text: There's no I in team. Well, there is an I in village, and the I in village is that when you start to interview and negotiate, you've got to be in charge. I want you to play to win, not not to lose.
moments.summary: In job negotiations, shift from playing not-to-lose to playing to win -- take charge of the interview and negotiation process.

podcasts.title: The original growth hacker reveals his secrets | Sean Ellis (author of “Hacking Growth”)
podcasts.description: The original growth hacker reveals his secrets, covering growth systems, strategic decision-making, and product design.

moments.speaker: Sean Ellis
moments.text: The question is, how would you feel if you could no longer use this product? Once you got a high enough percentage of users saying they'd be very disappointed, most of those products did pretty well. If you felt too low, those products tended to suffer.
moments.summary: The Sean Ellis test -- asking users how disappointed they would be without the product -- reliably predicts product-market fit when 40%+ say very disappointed.

moments.speaker: Sean Ellis
moments.text: Just ignore the people who say they'd be somewhat disappointed. They're telling you it's a nice to have. If you start paying attention to what your somewhat disappointed users are telling you and then you start tweaking onboarding and product based on their feedback, maybe you're going to dilute it for your must have users.
moments.summary: Focus on 'very disappointed' users, not 'somewhat disappointed' ones -- optimizing for nice-to-have feedback risks diluting the experience for your core fans.

moments.speaker: Sean Ellis
moments.text: It's usually much more function of onboarding to the right user experience than it is about the tactical things that people try to do to improve retention.
moments.summary: Retention is driven more by onboarding users to the right experience than by tactical retention hacks.

moments.speaker: Sean Ellis
moments.text: Before the referral program, Dropbox had amazing referral rate. Companies that are trying to copy it are like, 'Why isn't anyone talking about a product? Let's add a referral program with incentives.' To me, I think it's a great accelerant when it's already working, but it can't fix it if people don't want to talk about your product.
moments.summary: A referral program is an accelerant for existing word of mouth -- it cannot create organic sharing that does not already exist.

moments.speaker: Sean Ellis
moments.text: I start with the value that's uncovered through the test. So with a company, I'll say, 'This is what the must have value is according to our most passionate customers, and we want to think about a metric that reflects us delivering that value.'
moments.summary: Your North Star Metric should reflect the must-have value identified by your most passionate users, not revenue -- revenue should be a byproduct.

podcasts.title: How to consistently go viral: Nikita Bier’s playbook for winning at consumer apps (co-founder of TBH, Gas, advisor, investor)
podcasts.description: Nikita Bier’s playbook for winning at consumer apps (co-founder of TBH, Gas, advisor, investor), covering product design, consumer products, and go-to-market execution.

moments.speaker: Nikita Bier
moments.text: I looked on the App Store and the number one app in the United States was an app called Surah, but the entire app was in Arabic, like the strongest signal that you could ever have that people want something.
moments.summary: Finding an app in Arabic that was number one in the US App Store was the ultimate signal of latent demand -- the insight that led to building TBH.

moments.speaker: Nikita Bier
moments.text: We launched this app, it immediately took off, servers started crashing. I looked at our numbers and I'm like, 'We will be number one in the United States in six days.'
moments.summary: TBH went from launch to number one in the US in six days, validating the power of building for pre-existing demand.

moments.speaker: Nikita Bier
moments.text: Honored to be on a product management podcast for a person who doesn't believe product management is real.
moments.summary: Nikita Bier's provocative stance that product management as a discipline is not real reflects his builder-first, ship-fast philosophy.

podcasts.title: Unorthodox PM wisdom: Automating user insights, unselling job candidates, logging every decision, more | Kevin Yien (Stripe, Square, Mutiny)
podcasts.description: Automating user insights, unselling job candidates, logging every decision, more, covering product design, product strategy and execution, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Kevin Yien
moments.text: The PM job can become a little too internal, influencing my stakeholders and getting alignment and all these things. But if you can't sell or support your own product, I don't trust you to build the product.
moments.summary: PMs who cannot sell or support their own product have no business building it -- the role must stay grounded in customer reality, not internal politics.

moments.speaker: Kevin Yien
moments.text: We all talk about product sense. To me, it's just a fancy way of saying you can make good decisions with insufficient data. PMs need as many reps as possible in making decisions, documenting the rationale behind those decisions, and then crucially seeing the outcome of them.
moments.summary: Product sense is the ability to make good decisions with insufficient data, developed by logging decisions, rationale, and outcomes over many reps.

moments.speaker: Kevin Yien
moments.text: When you get to offer stage, I send an email and I say all the terrible things that are probably going to reinforce their fears. If you can tell them that upfront and they can read that whole email and still be equally excited to join you, find yourself a A+ hire.
moments.summary: The unsell email -- sharing all the terrible things about the role at offer stage -- filters for candidates who are truly committed and becomes a powerful hiring tool.

moments.speaker: Kevin Yien
moments.text: I use AI to essentially do a voice-of-the-customer pipeline automatically. It processes all customer calls, tags them with themes, and then I can go pull up exactly what people said about a given topic.
moments.summary: AI can automate the voice-of-the-customer pipeline by processing all customer calls and tagging them by theme, giving PMs instant access to real feedback.

podcasts.title: Improve strategy, influence, and decision-making by understanding your brain | Evan LaPointe (founder of CORE Sciences)
podcasts.description: Improve strategy, influence, and decision-making by understanding your brain, covering product design, team leadership, and measurement and analysis.

moments.speaker: Evan LaPointe
moments.text: The brain is like a college campus that has different departments in it. Most people rely on their history department way too much. If you instead send things to the more experimental, open-minded science department, the more creative art department, you get dramatically better answers.
moments.summary: Most people over-rely on their brain's history department (past patterns) instead of engaging the science department (experimentation) or art department (creativity).

moments.speaker: Evan LaPointe
moments.text: It's critical to ask what kind of experience am I? Not how good am I at my job, how much do I know, how critical am I to this process, but am I a miserable experience? If the answer is yes, don't worry too much about the other pieces yet. You got to fix that first.
moments.summary: Before worrying about skills or knowledge, ask whether you are a pleasant experience for others to work with -- fix that first because everything else depends on it.

moments.speaker: Evan LaPointe
moments.text: If mission, vision, values was an airline, you would not allow any family to fly on that airline. It does not arrive at most of its intended destinations.
moments.summary: Traditional mission-vision-values has a terrible track record for shaping culture -- logical deduction from your role in the world works far better than performative inspiration.

moments.speaker: Evan LaPointe
moments.text: Alpha is quite simply daydreaming. Beta is productivity mode. And then gamma is your brain's intense focus. We generally spend too much time in beta in work.
moments.summary: Teams over-index on beta (productivity) mode and under-invest in alpha (daydreaming) and gamma (deep thinking), which is where breakthrough ideas and innovations emerge.

moments.speaker: Evan LaPointe
moments.text: If 25% of your year is spent in gamma and alpha, you're probably a lot better off than the teams who spend less than 25% of their year thinking deep, and being in this more daydreaming mode.
moments.summary: Teams should aim for roughly 25% of their time in deep thinking and daydreaming modes -- most teams spend less than 5%, severely limiting innovation.

podcasts.title: How embracing emotions will accelerate your career | Joe Hudson (executive coach, Art of Accomplishment)
podcasts.description: How embracing emotions will accelerate your career, covering measurement and analysis, product design, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Joe Hudson
moments.text: It's the fact that they have emotions that they are not sitting, feeling, or expressing. Whatever emotion that you're trying to avoid, you are inviting into your life in exactly the way that you're trying to avoid it.
moments.summary: The emotions you avoid are the ones that dominate your life -- suppressed feelings manifest in the exact situations you are trying to prevent.

moments.speaker: Joe Hudson
moments.text: What most people try to do is they try to stop it, and that doesn't work very well. I think the best way to work with the voice in the head is to pick an experiment every day and respond to the voice in the head in a new way every day. One of my favorite responses is, 'Oh, I see that you're really scared. Don't worry. I'm right here with you. I got you.'
moments.summary: Instead of fighting your inner critic, try responding to it differently each day -- acknowledging its fear with compassion rather than suppressing it.

moments.speaker: Joe Hudson
moments.text: A lot of the people in my circles may have spent hundreds of millions of dollars to try to arrange a life that they enjoy, and it doesn't fucking work.
moments.summary: No amount of money can compensate for unprocessed emotions -- external arrangements cannot create internal satisfaction.

podcasts.title: Lessons from scaling Uber and Opendoor | Brian Tolkin (Head of Product at Opendoor, ex-Uber)
podcasts.description: Lessons from scaling Uber and Opendoor, covering product design, team leadership, and go-to-market execution.

moments.speaker: Brian Tolkin
moments.text: Uber always has this mentality and Opendoor does two of the product operations, twin turbine jet plane where you can fly the plane on one engine for a little bit if you need to, but it's operating most efficiently and effectively if both are working together.
moments.summary: Product and operations function like twin turbine engines -- you can run on one briefly, but peak performance requires both working in concert.

moments.speaker: Brian Tolkin
moments.text: Gave a really deep understanding of how the business actually works. It's a pretty good foundation for then going on to say, okay, what do we actually want to build in a more scalable technology way.
moments.summary: Working in operations gives product leaders a deep understanding of how the business actually works, which becomes the foundation for building scalable technology.

moments.speaker: Brian Tolkin
moments.text: I've slept on the floor in China before launching uberPOOL, and when you reflect the stress onto your teams, everybody tenses out. It counterintuitively doesn't produce better outcomes.
moments.summary: Reflecting stress onto your team counterintuitively produces worse outcomes -- staying calm under pressure unlocks better performance from everyone.

podcasts.title: The ultimate guide to performance marketing | Timothy Davis (Shopify)
podcasts.description: Performance marketing, covering strategic decision-making, growth systems, and product design.

moments.speaker: Timothy Davis
moments.text: Hot take, paid is for everyone. If you look at the way each platform is doing, Google, you have to scroll pretty far down to get to an organic listing. Meta, it's almost a pay for play now.
moments.summary: Paid marketing is no longer optional -- organic reach on Google and Meta has shrunk so much that every company needs some performance marketing.

moments.speaker: Timothy Davis
moments.text: Instead of thinking about being on top of the page, and that's like ego marketing, I want to be number one. I want to be there all the time. It's about showing to the right person as often as possible.
moments.summary: Stop chasing top-of-page vanity positioning and focus on showing your ad to the right person as often as possible -- that is what drives actual conversions.

moments.speaker: Timothy Davis
moments.text: Way underestimating the power of creative. Do you remember Dollar Shave Club? There you go. You remember it. That was creative.
moments.summary: Marketers massively underestimate the power of creative in ad performance -- great creative like Dollar Shave Club's video creates lasting impact beyond any targeting strategy.

moments.speaker: Timothy Davis
moments.text: I'm always forward thinking, backwards planning. 'Where do you want to be and ultimately how do you think you're going to get there?' Because say your goal is to be on all platforms... forward think, that's where you want to be. Now let's backwards plan.
